title: Halfway Huddle — action map & all-hands anchors (v0.1)
preview:
Purpose: quick, practical plan for the company halfway checkpoint — align exec updates, finalize a trimmed OKR set for Q3/Q4 cadence, and provide a short run-of-show + action owners for the all-hands. This is a working playbook not the final deck. Keep it tight and actionable.

High-level goals for this doc:
- Surface 3 cross-company priorities to feature in the all-hands (no more than 3).
- Propose a condensed OKR set for public sharing (top-line only).
- Produce a run-of-show (15–20 minute core + breakouts).
- Assign owners and next steps for everything that requires follow-up before the townhall.

Context / constraints:
- Budget posture: small discretionary spend available for Q3 pilots but hiring freeze still on engineering ICs (exceptions reviewed by CFO).
- Customers: two large deals expected to close in next 6–8 weeks; should be mentioned in exec update but not highlighted as guarantees.
- Private deployment push: legal/compliance asks mean we need at least one customer success story before end of quarter to validate the product/infra checklist.

Proposed 3 company anchors (what we center the all-hands on):
1) Reliability & Determinism — reduce production latency variance, hit 99.9% on core hosted endpoints.
   - Why: customer trust + enterprise readiness.
   - Signal to share: p50/p95/p99 latency change vs. last quarter; one incident story we fixed end-to-end.
2) Cost-Per-Query Initiative — measurable improvements from Optimize recommendations and batching deployments.
   - Why: unit economics win that keeps our PLG motion healthy.
   - Signal to share: expected cost delta and a quick showcase of a model/quantization win.
3) Private Deployments Go-To-Market — a concrete pilot and operational checklist for first three enterprise partners.
   - Why: revenue motion and compliance moat.
   - Signal to share: pilot kickoff timeline + two named integration owners.

Condensed OKR draft (top-line statements only — trimmed to be shareable):
- Objective A: Improve inference reliability and predictability for hosted customers.
  - Key Result 1: Reduce p99 latency for text-generate by 25% on production traffic mix.
  - Key Result 2: Decrease error rate (5xx and model-fallbacks) by 50% for top 5 routes.
- Objective B: Drive unit-cost efficiency without degrading quality.
  - Key Result 1: Deliver 18% average cost reduction for mid-size query workloads via batching + quant profiles.
  - Key Result 2: Validate cost/quality for 2 model family quantization configs in production.
- Objective C: Operationalize Private offering for regulated customers.
  - Key Result 1: Close 2 pilot contracts with legal-approved SOWs and KMS integrations.
  - Key Result 2: Publish a hardened private-deploy runbook + audit checklist.

Note: these are intentionally compact. We will expand measurable targets and owners post-feedback.

Executive update talking points (1–2 lines each, for CEO / CTO / CFO):
- CEO (30s): state the anchors, recognize teams, quick call-to-action for org (focus + customer empathy).
- CTO (2–3 min): highlight runtime improvements, one before/after latency story, and roadmap for model routing fallbacks.
- CFO (90s): short cost posture, where we are vs. committed spend, and conservative guidance for Q3.

All-hands run-of-show (45min total with optional breakouts):
- 0:00–0:03 Welcome + housekeeping (CEO) — quick note on tone and ask for questions in Slack thread.
- 0:03–0:08 CEO anchors (see above).
- 0:08–0:16 CTO update + demo clip (runtime latency graph + a short video of failover).
- 0:16–0:22 Product highlight (hosted API improvements + new checklist for private deployment).
- 0:22–0:28 Customer/CS spotlight (short recorded clip + one live engineer/customer ops Q&A).
- 0:28–0:36 Breakouts (two 8-minute rooms): 1) Reliability deep-dive (SRE + serving runtime). 2) Private pilot ops and legal checklist (product + legal).
- 0:36–0:40 Regroup + quick decisions (execs summarize breakout takeaways).
- 0:40–0:45 Closing + call-to-action (CEO) + linked follow-up thread.

Action owners & immediate next steps (week-by-week leading to all-hands):
- Aisha (owner): finalize the condensed OKR wording and circulate to execs by EOD Friday.
- Marco (CTO): provide p95/p99 latency graphs and 2 incident blurbs by Tue.
- Priya (Product): draft customer pilot checklist + one pager for private deployment by Wed.
- Ben (SRE): prepare short failover demo clip and notes for breakout by Thurs.
- Sofia (Legal): confirm KMS/HSM language and share redlines for playbook by Thu end.
- Comms (TBD): assemble 1-slide per anchor and a one-page FAQ for employee thread.

Open questions / risks (flag these in the thread so we can decide quickly):
- Do we want to highlight the two expected deals by name or keep them anonymized? (Risk: premature expectations).
- Hiring freeze exceptions — what process do managers follow to request headcount for critical pilots?
- Is the metric baseline (latency/cost) agreed across teams? We need a canonical dashboard ID.

Draft messaging for the all-hands announcement (short):
"Halfway Huddle: join us for a concise update on where we are and where we're headed — reliability wins, cost work that matters, and how we're scaling private deployments. Bring questions; we'll have focused breakouts and a one-page follow-up."

Appendix: suggested artifacts to attach to the all-hands folder (links to be added):
- p95/p99 latency CSV export (Marco)
- One-pager: Private pilot checklist (Priya)
- Playbook draft: Private deployment audit (Sofia)
- Demo video (~90s): failover + KV cache behavior (Ben)

Formatting notes for the final deck (practical):
- 8–10 slides max. Use the 3-anchor structure as headers. Each anchor: 1 slide with signal + 1 slide for ask.
- Slide 1: TL;DR anchors + one-sentence ask for the company.
- Keep data visuals simple: single graph per slide. Avoid multi-axis charts.

Decision log (what we need decided before the townhall):
- Approve the 3 anchors as the official all-hands narrative (vote: execs).
- Finalize the condensed OKR strings (Aisha to collect approvals).
- Confirm whether to name expected deals (risk assessment).

Notes to self (rough):
- This doc is a running map — add links as artifacts land.
- Aim to lock deck by 6 business days before the townhall.
- Keep FAQ length <= 1 page; employees appreciate blunt, honest answers.

Quick timeline (dates relative to a hypothetical townhall on 2026-02-20):
- 2026-02-05: Execs sign-off anchors and OKR wording (target).
- 2026-02-08: Deck lock.
- 2026-02-10: Internal rehearsal.
- 2026-02-20: All-hands.

---
Version notes: v0.1 — initial working map. Expect iterative edits. Add comments in this doc or drop flagged items in #company-all-hands.

title: Compact role proof assignment and day-one checklist
preview:
Purpose: quick, low-friction way to validate candidate fit for small IC roles (eg. Platform/Infra/Serving) where we want a 72-hour signal + a succinct day-one onboarding plan. Use when we need faster decisions than a multi-week take-home + full onsite loop.

High-level flow (target: 10 business days from screen to offer/no-go):
- Day 0: Recruiter screen, confirm availability for 72-hour trial and right-to-work/KYC checks.
- Day 1-3: Compact proof assignment (candidate given 72 hours to complete a constrained task).
- Day 4: Hiring manager + reviewer readout (30–45m).
- Day 5: Practical interview (60m) focused on design + operational judgement.
- Day 6–7: Reference checks + final calibration.
- Day 8–10: Offer / close loop.

Goals for this doc:
- Standardize the compact assignment instructions and grading sheet.
- Provide a simple day-one checklist for hires who come through this path (so hiring managers can onboard a 72-hour hire with minimal ramp friction).
- Surface risk signals and mitigations for short trials.

Compact proof assignment (example brief):
- Role target: Serving Runtime Engineer (senior IC).
- Timebox: 72 hours after assignment delivered. Clarify that small team will read but not run full end-to-end integration.
- Deliverable: A short repo (or gists) with: 1) A README describing the approach and tradeoffs (max 600 words). 2) One or two runnable scripts illustrating a reproducible micro-benchmark and a small optimization (e.g., reduce latency of a simple transformer inference loop by applying batching or KV cache pattern). 3) A short test or simple harness and instructions for how we evaluated it.
- Constraints: Candidate may use third-party libs but must note runtime and cost implications; no non-free software dependencies.
- Evaluation time for reviewers: target 45–60 minutes total per reviewer.

Communications templates (short):
- Assignment intro email (to candidate): Welcome, why we do this, link to repo template, deliverables, what success looks like, explicit timeline, and contact person.
- Follow-up: Reminder at 48h with offer to clarify questions; final deadline notice 12h before end.

Evaluation rubric (compact, numeric):
- Technical correctness (0–5): Does the code do what it says? Edge cases handled?
- Engineering judgment (0–5): Tradeoffs called out, cost/latency awareness, testability.
- Clarity of communication (0–5): README quality, commit messages, explanation of approach.
- Operational awareness (0–5): Observability, failure modes, rollout considerations described.
- Speed of delivery (0–2): Met deadline and provided runnable artifacts.
- Overall fit signal (0–3): Collaboration notes from interviewers; cultural/peer-fit.
- Total: 0–25. Recommendation bands: 20–25 strong hire, 15–19 hire with concerns/managed ramp, 10–14 needs more evidence, <10 no-go.

Readout guidance for hiring manager (30–45m):
- Candidate 10‑minute walkthrough (candidate narrates their repo).
- 15 minute Q&A focused on tradeoffs and edge cases.
- 10 minute deeper dive by reviewer on one area we care about (e.g., batching and latency).
- 5–10 minute calibration and note-taking (use scorecard).

Practical interview focus (post-proof, 60m):
- System design at 1–3 component level (20m). Emphasize operational failure scenarios and mitigations.
- Behavioral + collaboration (15m): How they work with PMs/SREs/infra teams.
- Live debugging / small whiteboard (20m): Short problem derived from their proof; look for approach rather than perfect solution.
- Closing and candidate questions (5m).

Day-one checklist (for hires from compact path) — minimal first day to accelerate value:
- Pre-day-one (HR + IT): laptop provisioned, corp email set up, Slack + OKTA invites, KMS access request ticket created (if needed).
- Morning (first 3 hours): 1:1 with hiring manager (30m) to align expectations, 1:1 with buddy (30m) to go over repo and running tests, quick orientation (30m) to infra, codebase, and local dev scripts.
- Afternoon (3–8 hours): run candidate's compact proof locally with buddy, identify one small follow-up improvement to ship (paired) — goal: 1 small PR by EOD or next day.
- Admin: add candidate to on-call and incident channels if role requires (read-only first week), enroll in security training micro-module.

First 2-week goals (sample):
- Week 1: Reproduce proof, ship one follow-up PR (tests and CI), attend team standups and 1:1 cadence, basic observability runbook created.
- Week 2: Pair on a small production-facing task, shadow 1–2 incidents, present 15m learnings review.

Buddy and mentor pairing:
- Immediate buddy: peer-level engineer for day-to-day pairing in first 2 weeks.
- 30/60/90 mentor check-ins: hiring manager schedules 30m touchpoints at 30/60/90 days; mentor provides technical feedback.

Risk signals (what to watch for during/after compact trial):
- Incomplete artifacts or non-runnable code without explanation -> request follow-up (not immediate no-go for junior roles).
- No operational considerations (observability, failure modes) -> low confidence for production roles.
- Poor communication in README / commit messages -> collaboration risk.
- Missed deadlines without communication -> reliability concern.
- Strong technical work but poor behavioral signals -> discuss with hiring manager and recruiter for fit mitigation.

Notes to recruiter / hiring manager (practical):
- Only use compact path for roles with clear measurable micro-tasks and when recruiter can set expectations with candidate clearly.
- Keep candidate informed of decision timeline; compact trials feel short — transparency reduces drop-off.
- If a candidate requests more time for a deeper take-home, allow it but treat it as a different flow.

Appendix: quick checklist for reviewers
- Clone + run? yes/no
- README clarity score (0–5)
- Tests included? Y/N and quality note
- Tradeoffs documented? Y/N
- Observability/rollback notes present? Y/N
- Overall numeric score and free text bullets (3–7 lines)

To iterate: try this path on 3 hires in Q1 as an experiment. Track outcomes: time-to-offer, acceptance rate, 30/60/90 ramp metrics. Adjust rubric weights if we see false positives/negatives.

Notes to self: keep instructions terse; avoid giving away infra secrets; maintain same assignment across candidates for fairness; share rubric with candidate on ask (post-submission) to help feedback.

TODO: finalize template repo, make small recruiter playbook card, and create Notion page linking to this doc (owner: Catherine Morales).

title: Executive bridge canvas and talking trajectories (Q2→Q3 handoff) v0.1
preview:
Purpose:
This doc is a compact bridge between exec priorities and the all-staff narrative for the Q2→Q3 transition.
It is intentionally terse: use for rehearsal notes, talking trajectories for founders/ELT, and the canonical list of asks we cascade to teams this week.

Executive summary (1 paragraph):
We will focus the next quarter on three cross-cutting themes: (1) Make inference economics explicit — measurable unit cost improvements without degrading quality; (2) Harden reliability and predictable fallbacks across Hosted/Dedicated/Private; (3) Improve developer onboarding velocity for 3rd-party integrators. These map to the OKR draft below.

High-level timeline (milestones):
- Feb 2: Exec review of OKR seeds (this doc).
- Feb 9: Leadership sync — finalize objectives and critical signals.
- Feb 16: Leadership message drafted; slides + rehearsal plan ready.
- Feb 23: All-staff comms + townline rehearsal.
- Mar 2: Public product notes and updated roadmap tiles.

Draft OKRs (candidate):
Objective A: Improve inference unit economics and predictable performance
- KR A1: Reduce 95th percentile per-response token cost on typical L2 workloads by 18–22% (baseline: Jan '26 measurement).
- KR A2: Increase cache hit rate for prefix/KV caches from 11% → 30% on identified high-volume endpoints.
- KR A3: Ship two model-quantization profiles (auto-applied suggestions in Optimize) for top 5 usage models.

Objective B: Reliability and graceful degradation across deployments
- KR B1: Meet 99.95% uptime for Dedicated pods (monthly SLA window) for two consecutive months.
- KR B2: Implement region-aware fallback routing with simulated failover tests for top 3 regions.
- KR B3: Reduce mean time to recovery (MTTR) for inference hot-path incidents from 14m → <8m.

Objective C: Developer adoption & onboarding velocity
- KR C1: Cut time-to-first-successful-call for new registrants (hosted API) from 18m → 8m (measured via quickstart flow telemetry).
- KR C2: Publish three turnkey reference integrations (SaaS connector templates) and instrument usage.
- KR C3: Increase self-serve activation conversion from trial → paying by 12 percentage points.

Notes on OKR wording & risks:
- These KRs are intentionally metric-forward; we will annotate baselines after Feb 9 measurement pass.
- Risk: pushing aggressive cost targets without tying to quality regressions — we must bind each cost KR to quality guardrails (eval prompt sets).

Townline / All-staff message outline (for founders/ELT):
1) One-sentence mission refocus: enabling predictable, economical LLM serving for every engineering org.
2) Why this quarter: external pressure (model/API spend) + customer asks for reliability + enterprise adoption opportunities.
3) The three themes (economics / reliability / developer velocity) and 2–3 anchor KRs.
4) Where each team plugs in (concrete asks).
5) What success looks like in 90 days (clear signal examples).
6) Slack/office hours + how we will surface progress weekly.

All-staff rehearsal notes (speakers + timings):
- Founders (opening): 3m — framing + signal of urgency.
- CTO (runtime): 6m — 2 quick wins and upcoming infra milestones.
- Head of Product (hosted/dedicated/private roadmap): 6m — roadmap tiles and customer stories.
- Ops (metrics and cadence): 4m — how we will report progress and where to find the dashboard.
- Q&A: 10m — curated crowd questions + live.
Total: 30m

Owner asks (what to prepare this week):
- Eng-serving-runtime: publish baseline perf/cost dashboard (owner: Diego) — due Feb 6.
- Product-hosted-api: write 1-page mapping of product routes to cost-impact tiers (owner: Priya) — due Feb 8.
- Solutions-eng: prepare two case vignettes where Dedicated solved a scaling pain (owner: Samira) — Feb 8.
- Devex: update quickstart to reflect new SDK flow and instrument TTF success (owner: Morgan) — Feb 9.

Communications cadence & channels:
- Week-of-Feb 16: pre-brief to team leads + rehearsal notes in this doc.
- Feb 23: live townline + recorded video.
- Weekly: short pulse email from Ops every Monday with 3 metrics (cost, uptime, TTF).
- Slack: #company-trajectory for day-to-day updates; pinned weekly digest.

Risk register (top items):
- Quality regressions from quantization: mitigation = gated rollout + eval runbook.
- Infrastructure capacity shortfall during rollout: mitigation = canary + temporary burst pool.
- Messaging fatigue: mitigation = keep comms to 3 signals and concrete team asks only.

Decision log (open):
- D1: Approve binding cost target range for KR A1? (need exec greenlight; action: founders to confirm by Feb 9).
- D2: Prioritization of region fallback work vs. cache optimization for first two sprints (engineers want both).

Metrics & dashboards (where to look):
- Cost per token (hosted) — infra/perf dashboard (linked_artifacts: JIRA INF-1938).
- Cache hit rate by route — Optimize telemetry tile.
- New user time-to-first-successful-call — Devex quickstart funnel.

Open questions (short):
- Q: How strictly do we treat KR A1 as gating compensation/bonus calibration? (HR input requested).
- Q: Do we run customer-facing comms about cost reductions or keep internal only?
- Q: Which two reference connectors do we prioritize (CRM vs. search vs. analytics)?

- [ ] Rehearsal 2 (with comms team) scheduled.

text:
Purpose:
This doc is a compact bridge between exec priorities and the all-staff narrative for the Q2→Q3 transition.
It is intentionally terse: use for rehearsal notes, talking trajectories for founders/ELT, and the canonical list of asks we cascade to teams this week.

Executive summary (1 paragraph):
We will focus the next quarter on three cross-cutting themes: (1) Make inference economics explicit — measurable unit cost improvements without degrading quality; (2) Harden reliability and predictable fallbacks across Hosted/Dedicated/Private; (3) Improve developer onboarding velocity for 3rd-party integrators. These map to the OKR draft below.

High-level timeline (milestones):
- Feb 2: Exec review of OKR seeds (this doc).
- Feb 9: Leadership sync — finalize objectives and critical signals.
- Feb 16: Leadership message drafted; slides + rehearsal plan ready.
- Feb 23: All-staff comms + townline rehearsal.
- Mar 2: Public product notes and updated roadmap tiles.

Draft OKRs (candidate):
Objective A: Improve inference unit economics and predictable performance
- KR A1: Reduce 95th percentile per-response token cost on typical L2 workloads by 18–22% (baseline: Jan '26 measurement).
- KR A2: Increase cache hit rate for prefix/KV caches from 11% → 30% on identified high-volume endpoints.
- KR A3: Ship two model-quantization profiles (auto-applied suggestions in Optimize) for top 5 usage models.

Objective B: Reliability and graceful degradation across deployments
- KR B1: Meet 99.95% uptime for Dedicated pods (monthly SLA window) for two consecutive months.
- KR B2: Implement region-aware fallback routing with simulated failover tests for top 3 regions.
- KR B3: Reduce mean time to recovery (MTTR) for inference hot-path incidents from 14m → <8m.

Objective C: Developer adoption & onboarding velocity
- KR C1: Cut time-to-first-successful-call for new registrants (hosted API) from 18m → 8m (measured via quickstart flow telemetry).
- KR C2: Publish three turnkey reference integrations (SaaS connector templates) and instrument usage.
- KR C3: Increase self-serve activation conversion from trial → paying by 12 percentage points.

Notes on OKR wording & risks:
- These KRs are intentionally metric-forward; we will annotate baselines after Feb 9 measurement pass.
- Risk: pushing aggressive cost targets without tying to quality regressions — we must bind each cost KR to quality guardrails (eval prompt sets).

2) Why this quarter: external pressure (model/API spend) + customer asks for reliability + enterprise adoption opportunities.

title: Micro-certification Track and 72hr Try-On Activation
preview:
Purpose:
This doc lays out a lightweight micro-certification track (skill badges + short assessments) paired with a 72-hour candidate ‘try-on’ activation for high-velocity hiring pockets (platform infra, support SRE, and junior inference engineers). Goal: reduce time-to-productivity, improve signal before offers, and create a standardized, fair rubric for convert/decline decisions.

Who this is for:
- Hiring managers and interviewers in eng/platform/SRE pods
- People Ops recruiters running fast cycles
- Mentors who will run the 72hr try-on

High level flow (timeline):
1) Recruiter screens -> short coding/knowledge pre-badge (24–48h)
2) Micro-cert assessment (remote, 45m) -> badge awarded if >=70%
3) Invite to 72hr paid try-on (candidate works with a mentor on a small scoped task)
4) Mentor debrief + rubric scoring -> hiring decision window (48h after try-on ends)

Design principles:
- Lightweight: each badge takes <=60 minutes to complete (instructions + 30–45m exercise).
- Work-like: try-on tasks mirror first-week real work (small infra changes, bug fix, doc + PR).
- Paid & short: 72 hours, up to 12 hours of work total, paid at contractor rate.
- Equity: same rubric applied across all candidates; anonymized scoring where feasible.

Micro-cert catalog (examples):
- Linux fundamentals (shell, permissions, logs): 6 questions + 1 short exercise
- Basic Python infra scripting: fill-in + mini-script to parse logs
- Simple RPC/debugging scenario: identify root cause in supplied trace
- Oncall triage checklist: prioritize incidents and compose PS summary

Pre-badge mechanics (recruiter step):
- Send candidate link to assessment platform (internal tool).
- Time window: complete within 48 hours of receipt.
- Pass threshold: 70% -> invite to try-on; 85% -> fast-track to offer if manager wants.

72hr Try-on orchestration (operational playbook):
- Day 0 (Monday): Candidate receives onboarding packet, repo access to sandbox, mentor assigned. Mentor:senior engineer (paid mentor time = 6 hours).
- Day 1: Kickoff call (30m) to set goals and clarify boundaries. Candidate starts task (e.g., add logging and a small optimization), opens a draft PR.
- Day 2: Continue work, mentor provides async feedback, candidate iterates. Candidate writes a short design note (250–500 words) describing approach and tradeoffs.
- Day 3: Finalize PR, run tests. End-of-day: mentor schedules 30m debrief and completes rubric. People Ops collects time log and closes contractor invoice.

Mentor responsibilities (short):
- Provide sandbox access and a single clear task statement (no more than 4 bullets).
- Aim for 3–4 substantive feedback exchanges (async or short calls).
- Do not assign production-facing escalations or extended context pick-up beyond task scope.
- Complete rubric within 24 hours of try-on finish.

Evaluation rubric (flat scoring fields; use numeric 1-5 scale and a yes/no pass threshold):
- Technical correctness (1-5): Does the solution work and handle edge cases?
- Code quality & hygiene (1-5): Readability, tests, small comments, consistent style
- Problem decomposition (1-5): Breaks down issue into tractable steps and tradeoffs
- Communication & documentation (1-5): Design note + PR description clarity
- Team fit signals (1-5): responsiveness, receptiveness to feedback
- Time management (1-5): Completed within the allocated hours and prioritized effectively
- Recommendation (yes/no): Hire / No-hire / Keep-in-pipe (with reasons)

Scoring guidance:
- Pass threshold: average >=3.5 across technical, code quality, decomposition, and communication, AND mentor recommendation = Hire/Keep-in-pipe.
- Auto-fail signals: plagiarism, security violations, refusal to sign NDA for sandbox, or evidence of toxic behaviour.

Offer & conversion cadence:
- If Mentor recommends Hire: manager + people ops release verbal offer within 48 hours and aim for written offer within 5 business days.
- If Keep-in-pipe: candidate remains in active pool for 90 days; recruiter keeps touchpoints every 2 weeks.
- Decline: provide short, actionable feedback (3 points) and suggest next steps to improve.

Operational checklist for People Ops (pre-run):
- [ ] Confirm hiring manager and mentor availability (calendar blocks for 45m across 3 days)
- [ ] Create sandbox repo and test harness; ensure no PII or real customer data
- [ ] Generate access tokens and short-lived credentials (expiry 7 days)
- [ ] Payment terms confirmed with finance (contractor rate, invoice template)
- [ ] NDA/engagement doc sent and signed before access
- [ ] Slack channel created for async clarifications (invite candidate + mentor)

Candidate experience script (kickoff email template blurbs):
- Welcome note + context (what we expect in 72h)
- How we’ll compensate you and how to invoice
- Who your mentor is and how they’ll support you
- How to ask for clarifications (Slack thread dedicated)

Risks & mitigations:
- Risk: Mentor overload -> Mitigation: cap mentor slots to 2 try-ons concurrently and rotate mentors monthly.
- Risk: Legal/compliance with paid contractor -> Mitigation: pre-approved contractor template + finance sign-off.
- Risk: Unfair comparisons -> Mitigation: anonymize candidate metadata in rubric sheet and rotate reviewers.

Examples of 72hr tasks (templates):
- Task A (Infra): Add missing metrics and a small rate-limiting cache; deliver PR + smoke tests + design note
- Task B (SRE): Investigate a synthetic incident and produce a postmortem stub + remediation PR
- Task C (Platform): Write a small script to ingest a sample trace format and normalize fields (unit tests required)

Post-hire onboarding tie-in:
- Badge(s) from micro-cert transfer into first-week onboarding checklist (manager sees which badges the new hire holds).
- Mentor passes written handoff notes into the new hire’s first sprint card and links to the issued PRs.

Metrics to track (success signals):
- Time-to-offer median for try-on hires vs traditional pipeline
- Acceptance rate for try-on cohort
- New hire 30/60/90-day ramp KPIs (engineer-specific metrics)
- Candidate satisfaction survey (nps-style) after try-on

Open questions & next steps (for this draft):
- Decide which teams will pilot (proposal: platform infra, SRE, and solutions-eng interns)
- Finalize contractor rates with finance and legal review for US/EU variants
- Build assessment bank and map badges to interview stages (owner: Marcus Lee)
- Pilot schedule: Q1 2026 run with 10 try-ons, retro after each cohort

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally prescriptive but modular — teams can opt-in and adopt badges that map to their role.
- Looking for feedback on pass thresholds, fair-pay band for try-on, and operational load on mentors.

Author: Elena Park (People Ops)
Contributors: Marcus Lee (Hiring manager, infra), Priya Natarajan (Recruiting), Diego Alvarez (SRE mentor)

Examples of 72hr tasks (templates):
- Task A (Infra): Add missing metrics and a small rate-limiting cache; deliver PR + smoke tests + design note
- Task B (SRE): Investigate a synthetic incident and produce a postmortem stub + remediation PR
- Task C (Platform): Write a small script to ingest a sample trace format and normalize fields (unit tests required)

Post-hire onboarding tie-in:
- Badge(s) from micro-cert transfer into first-week onboarding checklist (manager sees which badges the new hire holds).
- Mentor passes written handoff notes into the new hire’s first sprint card and links to the issued PRs.

Metrics to track (success signals):
- Time-to-offer median for try-on hires vs traditional pipeline
- Acceptance rate for try-on cohort
- New hire 30/60/90-day ramp KPIs (engineer-specific metrics)
- Candidate satisfaction survey (nps-style) after try-on

Open questions & next steps (for this draft):
- Decide which teams will pilot (proposal: platform infra, SRE, and solutions-eng interns)
- Finalize contractor rates with finance and legal review for US/EU variants
- Build assessment bank and map badges to interview stages (owner: Marcus Lee)
- Pilot schedule: Q1 2026 run with 10 try-ons, retro after each cohort

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally prescriptive but modular — teams can opt-in and adopt badges that map to their role.
- Looking for feedback on pass thresholds, fair-pay band for try-on, and operational load on mentors.

Author: Elena Park (People Ops)
Contributors: Marcus Lee (Hiring manager, infra), Priya Natarajan (Recruiting), Diego Alvarez (SRE mentor)

title: First-Week Sandbox and Role-Proving Sprint
preview:
Purpose:
This doc describes a lightweight first-week sandbox designed to both (a) give candidates a fast, real-feel task to demonstrate role fit and (b) bootstrap a new hire's first-week momentum if hired. The approach sits between a 72hr trial and a full takehome: short, timeboxed, mentored, and explicitly scoped for a hiring decision + immediate onboarding carryover.

Scope (who/use-cases):
- Roles: Individual contributor engineers (infra/platform/serving-runtime), SRE, small-ish product-engineering hires, and solutions engineers. Not intended for senior ICs > L6 or managers (use extended takehome + onsite).
- When to use: Final-stage candidates when we need faster validation of day-to-day signals (ownership, debugging, triage, communication) and when team bandwidth to run a small scaffolded trial exists.

High-level timeline:
- Day -2 (pre-week): Candidate receives sandbox brief, access instructions, and optional reading (~30–45 min prep).
- Day 0 (kickoff call, 30 min): Meet hiring buddy, scope detailed deliverables, confirm env setup.
- Days 1–3: Candidate works on sandbox tasks (max 8 hours total signal). Daily 20–30 minute async check-ins with buddy (Slack + short notes).
- Day 4: Demo (30–40 min), walkthrough of decisions, Q&A. Hiring panel fills rubric within 24 hours.
- Day 5: Decision window + offer comms if green. If hired, handoff artifacts to onboarding owner; candidate begins first-week activation tasks (list below).

Deliverables (candidate-facing):
- Short: implement a small feature or reproduce a fault and write a short plan to fix it. Deliverables must be runnable locally or in a provided sandbox container and include a 1–2 page writeup describing intent, tradeoffs, and next steps.
- Optional stretch: a short e2e test or a checklist for rollout considerations.

Scaffold (what we provide):
- A repo fork with seed code and failing test(s) or a small infra sandbox (docker image + seeded logs).
- A runbook template (one page) and a demo script template.
- A dedicated Slack channel name pattern: #sandbox-<role>-<candidate-last>-<date>.
- Access: a time-limited GitHub repo invite, read-only access to an internal mock service, and test dataset.

Security & legal checklist (People Ops):
- Candidate NDA checkbox included in invite.
- Time-limited tokens created and revoked by SRE.
- No production data. All datasets sanitized.

Evaluation rubric (panel completes after demo):
- Problem understanding (1–5): Did candidate summarize the problem and constraints quickly?
- Approach & prioritization (1–5): Clear tradeoffs, realistic rollout plan?
- Code quality & reproducibility (1–5): Can reviewers run the demo and read the changes easily?
- Debugging & triage (1–5): When surprised, did the candidate form experiments and narrow causes?
- Communication (1–5): Clarity in writeup and demo; async check-ins quality.
- Team fit & ownership propensity (1–5): Will candidate proactively follow through and ask for help?
- Overall recommendation: hire / no-hire / hire-with-reservations (free text justification required).

Scoring guidance (quick heuristics):
- 24–26: strong hire (offer + typical comp).
- 19–23: hire with reservations (possible leveling/comp calibration or follow-up loop).
- <19: decline; summary note required for candidate feedback.

Manager / Buddy checklist (pre-trial):
- Confirm scope fits the role and can be completed in 8–12 hours of candidate time.
- Seed repo and CI set up; tests deterministic.
- Prep 30–40 minute demo slot in calendars.
- Assign buddy who will perform daily async check-ins.
- Prepare rubric and feedback template.

Manager / Buddy checklist (if candidate hired):
- Convert candidate's sandbox fork into new-hire onboarding branch/playlist where appropriate.
- Add candidate writeup to their onboarding folder in People Ops: /onboarding/new-joiner/<name>/week-0-sandbox-notes.
- Set first-week pairing sessions based on observed weaknesses/gaps.

Templates & comms (short snippets):
- Kickoff invite (subject): Redwood sandbox kickoff — 30 min
  Hi <candidate>,
  Thanks for taking part in our quick sandbox. This is a scoped opportunity to show how you work; we estimate ~8 hours of effort spread across a few days. On Day 0 we’ll meet for 30 min to align. Repo link + quickstart are here: <repo-link>. NDA accepted? Reply yes and we’ll confirm access.

- Demo invite (subject): Sandbox demo w/ hiring panel — 40 min
  Hi all,
  Candidate: <name>. Agenda: 1) 10m demo, 2) 15m Q&A + design discussion, 3) 5m decision next steps. Please complete rubric after the call.

- Decline feedback (short):
  Thanks for spending time with us. We appreciated your work on the sandbox. After careful review we’ve decided to move forward with other candidates. Specific note: <one concrete signal>. If you’d like more detail reply and we’ll follow up.

Operational notes (people-ops runbook):
- Who signs off: Hiring manager + People Ops lead must both sign off on an offer after positive rubric aggregate.
- Timebox: Keep trial <= 7 calendar days from kickoff to decision.
- Reporting: People Ops tracks sandbox outcomes in hiring dashboard under tag "sandbox_trial" and aggregates time-to-decision and offer acceptance rate monthly.

Failure modes & mitigations:
- Candidate stalls on environment setup: Provide a docker fallback image and offer a same-day buddy pairing session.
- Candidate overbuilds: If >12 hours invested, flag to manager and treat as extra sample; do not penalize, but cap scoring to standard rubric time expectations.
- Sensitive work surfaced: Immediately escalate to security; kill tokens and pause trial.

First-week carryover tasks (if hired):
- Day 0–1: environment replication of sandbox + tour with buddy.
- Day 2–3: small, reviewable ticket derived from sandbox to close feedback loop.
- Day 4–5: pairing sessions and roadmap sync; assign a 30–60 day success objective based on sandbox signals.

Notes to self / open questions (work-in-progress):
- Should we keep a short canonical set of sandbox templates per role family (serving-runtime, infra, console) to reduce manager prep time? Likely yes — create a small repo of templates.
- Consider automated rubric aggregation widget in the hiring dashboard (low priority but high signal).
- Track fairness: ensure the task doesn't advantage candidates with specific prior repo access (avoid org-specific infra).

Appendix: Example sandbox brief (serving-runtime internals):
- Goal: Given a tiny inference service with an intermittent latency spike when sequence length increases, reproduce problem locally, add a lightweight telemetry check and propose one mitigation (e.g., cache warm path, input bucketing). Deliver: runnable demo, README with reproduction steps, 1–2 rationales for mitigation, and a short deployment checklist.

Quick links / placeholders:
- Template repo: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sandbox-templates
- Hiring dashboard: https://internal.redwood.ai/hiring-dashboard?filter=sandbox_trial
- People Ops playbook (master): CONFLUENCE-12345

Revision history:
- 2025-10-02 — initial draft (Avery Chen)
- 2025-11-12 — added scoring guidance and anchors (Priya N.)
- 2026-01-12 — tightened security checklist, clarified timebox (Liam O')

End of doc.

title: Approval playtest: synthetic evidence protocol
preview:
Purpose
---
This is a lightweight PRD-style playbook for a focused playtest: exercise approval gates and audit-log retention semantics in Private deployments using synthetic (fabricated) change events. The goal is to validate operational assumptions, measure evidence size/provenance, and exercise customer-managed-network handshakes without touching real customer configs.

Why this helps
---
- Low-risk way to verify that approval sampling, compact evidence bundles, and rollback tokens behave end-to-end in on-prem environments.
- Lets us measure network cost, archive size, and query latency for common forensic queries before committing to a retention/ingest model.
- Provides concrete UX/automation gaps for operator-side agents and control-plane attestation.

Scope (what we will test)
---
1) Approval gate flow: generate a sequence of synthetic VPC-change requests that exercise each approval tier (operator, delegated approver, emergency bypass).
2) Evidence packaging: create compact evidence bundles (metadata + signed delta + optional snapshot). Measure bundle size across varying payload heuristics.
3) Audit log ingestion paths: on-prem forwarder -> customer-edge aggregator -> control-plane ingest. Validate backpressure and retry semantics.
4) Query and replay: run a sample forensic query (time-window, actor, changed-cidrs) against the stored evidence set and a replay harness.
5) Fallbacks: simulate control-plane unavailability and ensure graceful local audit retention + later reconciliation.

Non-goals
---
- This playtest will not attempt live customer traffic or real credential rotation. No production customer state will be modified.

High-level design / components
---
- Synthetic change generator (local): emits deterministic change events (JSON) with controllable branching, sizes, and frequency.
- Edge forwarder (on-prem agent): collects events, signs them with ephemeral operator key, and forwards to customer-edge aggregator.
- Attestation layer: compact witness token creator that includes: event-hash, operator-sig, control-plane-ack (if available).
- Evidence packager: converts event stream into compact bundles (delta only / delta+snapshot) with an index for fast query.
- Control-plane ingest + indexer (staging): receives bundles, stores in object store, updates index for tfast queries.
- Forensic query harness: runs a set of canonical queries and measures latency, false positives, and data completeness.

Synthetic evidence structure (prototype)
---
- event_id: stable string
- timestamp: ISO string
- actor: synthetic principal name (operator-e2e-1)
- change_summary: short text (e.g., "add-prefix 10.40.0.0/16 to tenant-abc")
- delta_blob: base64-encoded minimal patch (optional snapshot flag)
- operator_sig: base64 signature of (event_id + delta_blob)
- witness_token: optional base64 token returned by control-plane after ack

Note: we'll keep event payloads intentionally small (128B / 1KB / 10KB sizes) to observe compression/archival effects.

Playtest scenarios (initial set)
---
A. Normal approval path (canary): 1) operator submits change, 2) delegated approver approves, 3) control-plane returns witness token.
B. Tiered approval (long path): change escalates through 3 approvers; simulate 5s, 30s, 2m delays to test queuing/backpressure.
C. Emergency bypass: operator marks emergency flag; change applies with emergency token; verify audit marks and extra retention.
D. Control-plane interruption: forwarder continues collecting locally for 24h and reconciles later; verify no evidence lost and dedup works.
E. Bulk churn test: burst 10k synthetic changes over 10 minutes to stress packaging and indexing.

Acceptance criteria (measurable)
---
- End-to-end: For 1000 normal-path synthetic changes, forensic queries by event_id return correct bundle 100% of the time after eventual consistency window (<= 60s).
- Bundle size: 95th percentile evidence bundle (delta-only) should be <= 2.5x of raw change payload after compression.
- Query latency: simple predicate queries on index (actor, prefix) should return results in <= 250ms P95 on staging hardware.
- Local retention: during control-plane down test, on-prem agent must hold 24h worth of events without dropping, and reconcile within 30m of control-plane recovery.
- UX: operator-side agent CLI/console shows clear failure/retry state and exposes a quick-export for legal/compliance.

Metrics to collect
---
- events emitted / s (by size class)
- forwarder throughput (events/s, bytes/s) and backlog depth
- bundle generation latency and bundle sizes (min/median/p95)
- control-plane ack latency for witness tokens
- query latency distribution for canonical queries
- reconciliation success rate and time-to-consistency after outage

Operator UX experiments (quick ideas)
---
- Show a compact "evidence card" in Console that summarizes an approval: event_id, actor, time, size, snapshot? yes/no, proof-hash. Click to download bundle.
- One-click legal export: compile events+signatures for time range and tenant, zipped with human-readable changelog and a signature manifest.
- Local CLI: redwood-evidence verify <bundle> to validate signatures and token consistency.

Risks and unknowns
---
- Key management: ephemeral operator keys vs long-lived keys. How does rotation affect evidence validation?
- Proof size with real network configs: synthetic payloads might underrepresent real-world diff sizes when customers have large ACL lists or BGP policies.
- Privacy: even synthetic events could leak shape of network tests to infosec. Need guardrails.
- Cross-tenant indexing costs if index includes many attributes.

Runbook / steps for this playtest
---
1) Prep: staging cluster, object-store bucket, indexer instance, and on-prem agent docker image.
2) Generate baseline synthetic corpus (500 events across sizes).
3) Exercise scenarios A..E, capture logs and metrics.
4) Run forensic queries and full replay for a random 10% subset and compare results vs local generator ground truth.
5) Document divergences, enlarge corpus if needed, and file follow-ups.

Deliverables from playtest
---
- Metrics report (CSV + short writeup) with key acceptance criteria results.
- Sample evidence bundles (3 sizes) and a validation script.
- UX notes: operator CLI/console mockups and friction points.
- Follow-up tickets: KM rotation impact, index sizing changes, retention policy proposals.

Open questions (to resolve in next week)
---
- Is the witness_token model required for all flows? (tradeoffs: immediate control-plane ack vs offline-signed receipts)
- What default retention class should we recommend for emergency-change bundles?
- How do we signal that a bundle contains PII-like network metadata that requires tighter access?

Next steps
---
- Schedule a 2h playtest kickoff with eng-sre, eng-platform, and product-private-deployments (owner: Priya).
- Assign Marco to build the synthetic generator; Sofia to wire the forwarder; Jordan to prepare indexer staging.
- Run initial baseline by 2025-12-10 and produce first metrics deliverable by 2025-12-17.

Notes to reviewers
---
This is intentionally concise and operational — treat as experiment plan not final spec. I expect a few iterations, especially around proof format and retention classes. Add comments inline or drop test-requests into #prod-private-playtests on Slack.

title: campus-huddle-kickoff-notes-and-commitment-matrix-v0-1
preview:
Purpose:
This doc is a working brief for the November–January company cadence experiment we're calling the ‘Campus Huddle’. It bundles a short all-staff forum, an exec sneak-preview, and a cross-functional commitment matrix so teams leave with concrete 30/60/90 commitments.

TL;DR:
- Why: tighten alignment between product roadmaps, infra capacity, and go-to-market for the next quarter so we ship predictable throughput improvements and a prioritized customer onboarding pilot.
- What: 90-minute live campus-huddle (hybrid) + 5 follow-up micro-sessions for handoffs.
- Outcome: signed commitment matrix, 3 company-level OKRs in draft, and 2 immediate exec asks.

Audience:
- All staff invited; primary listeners: Eng (serving-runtime, infra, SRE), Product (hosted-api, dedicated), GTM (growth + enterprise), Customer Success.

Proposed timeline (compressed):
- Week 0 (Nov 18): Exec preview + leadership alignment (30m)
- Week 1 (Nov 25): Campus Huddle all-staff (90m) + live Q&A (30m overflow)
- Weeks 2–4: Micro-sessions (30m each) to resolve cross-team owners and finalize commitments
- Dec 1: Publish final commitments + update console rollouts schedule

Agenda for Campus Huddle (90m):
1) 10m — Opening: Context and the economic thesis (Founders)
2) 15m — Quick state: latency/cost/usage signals (SRE + Observability)
3) 20m — Three candidate company-level OKRs (Product lead)
4) 20m — Demo: Dedicated capacity improvements + private deploy checklist (Infra + Solutions Eng)
5) 15m — Customer story / risk brief (CS + Sales)
6) 10m — Live commit & next steps (Facilitator)

Draft Company-level OKRs (for discussion)
- Objective A: Reduce median gen latency for 2–16k token workloads by 25% without increasing unit cost more than 5%.
  - Key result A1: Deploy KV-cache smart routing to >=60% of hosted traffic by end of Q1.
  - Key result A2: Ship selective kernel pipeline improvements for 4 verified models with >15% p50 improvement.

- Objective B: Deliver a hardened private deployment offering for regulated early-adopter customer (security + audit + on-prem packaging).
  - Key result B1: Complete on-prem installable bundle + automated smoke tests for 2 reference sites.
  - Key result B2: Integrate KMS/HSM flow and provide audit log export in GA preview.

- Objective C: Improve unit economics for chat-heavy PLG flows by 20% through batching, caching, and quantization recommendations in Redwood Optimize.
  - Key result C1: Add adaptive batching option to SDK and get 3 customers to opt in.
  - Key result C2: Publish model-quantization playbook and run 5 targeted experiments.

Commitment matrix (working — teams should sign to a single owner + 30/60/90 milestone):
- Eng: Serving Runtime (owner: Noah Bennett) — prototype KV-cache intelligent router by 30d; production canary by 60d; perf roll by 90d.
- Eng: Observability (owner: Lina Rodriguez) — produce token-level latency/cost dashboard and alert runbook by 21d; integrate with rollouts by 45d.
- Infra / Private Deploy (owner: Ethan Park) — finalize on-prem packaging checklist by 30d; run 2 install tests by 60d; final docs by 90d.
- Product: Hosted API (owner: Maya Thompson) — refine API defaults for batching & quantization and coordinate customer pilots by 30d/60d/90d.
- GTM: Growth + CS (owner: Sana Iqbal) — recruit pilot customers (3 PLG, 2 enterprise) by 30d; run onboarding case studies by 60d.

Exec asks (decision needed during exec preview):
1) Approve temporary $120k runway from ops budget to accelerate two infra hires and a pre-approved cloud burst allocation for model profiling over 8 weeks.
2) Greenlight a 2-week release blackout during the Week 5 micro-sessions to reduce noise during handoffs.

Communications plan:
- Pre-huddle: short 1-pager + 90s video from founders (internal), send 72h ahead.
- During huddle: capture notes, stream Q&A to slack thread #campus-huddle, tag owners.
- Post-huddle: publish commitment matrix to Confluence, 30/60/90 updates as simple check-ins in cadence channel.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Overcommitment by teams leading to missed OKRs. Mitigation: require teams to list stretch vs committed items in matrix; escalate misalignments in week 2 micro-sessions.
- Risk: Customer pilots not representative. Mitigation: diversify pilot set (PLG + 2 regulated enterprise) and define measurement suite upfront.

Decision log (to capture during exec preview):
- D1: Approve $120k ops allocation — Pending
- D2: Approve release blackout — Pending
- D3: Accept proposed OKR A/B/C set as company-level priorities — Pending

Appendix: quick links + prep checklist for participants
- Pre-read: latency/cost snapshots (Observability link placeholder).
- Prep: teams bring 1 slide: "what we will stop doing to make this work" and 1 clear 30d milestone.
- Facilitator notes: keep demo concise, switch to chat Q&A if live overflow >10 questions.

Open questions for the huddle organizer (Maya):
- Who owns the canonical copy of the commitment matrix? (propose product-ops)
- Should we make the micro-sessions optional or required for owners? (recommend required for owners + optional viewers)
- How will we measure OKR health weekly without adding overhead? (propose automated dashboard and a 10m weekly triage)

Next steps (short):
- 11/06: circulate pre-read + founders 90s video draft (owner: Maya)
- 11/10: leadership dry run (owner: Ethan)
- 11/18: exec preview (owners: Founders + Maya)
- 11/25: Campus Huddle live (all)

Notes to self:
- Keep ask framing tight: execs respond to tradeoffs (cost vs reliability vs speed).
- Emphasize wins: demo must show measurable p50 improvements not just architecture slides.

Document history:
- v0-1 (2025-11-05) initial sketch and owners assigned
- v0-2 (2025-12-01) updated OKR wording and added commitment owners (this file is v0-2 draft in progress)

---
End of doc.

title: Mentor-Led Onboarding Playbook for New Graduates (WIP)
preview:
Purpose:
This doc lays out a lightweight, mentor-led onboarding playbook specifically for new-graduate hires (new grads, recent interns converting to FTE, and entry-level ICs). Goal: accelerate time-to-impact, increase retention for early-career cohorts, create consistent evaluation signals for conversion decisions.

Scope / audience:
- People Ops + Hiring Managers + Mentors + new-grad hires
- Applies to Engineering and Product entry-level roles first phase; will adapt for SRE, DX, and Solutions in Q2 pilot

High-level objectives:
- 30-day ramp: core repo familiarity, local dev flow, first minor PR landed
- 60-day ramp: ownership of a small bug/feature, independent debugging workflow
- 90-day ramp: clear promotion/conversion signal or development plan; mentor/manager alignment on outcomes
- Mentor satisfaction and mentee NPS > 7 (post-90-day survey)

Roles & responsibilities (practical):
- Manager: sets 90-day goals, meets weekly 1:1, decides conversion outcome in collaboration with mentor
- Mentor (peer-level): weekly pairing, guided code reviews, onboarding curriculum owner (see modules), daily slack/office hours in week 1
- Buddy (on-call social): calendar invites, logistics, lunch intro, helps new hire with access issues but not technical guidance
- People Ops: coordinates cohort orientation, supplies onboarding checklist, collects feedback, schedules conversion review

Mentor pairing process (runbook):
1) Assignment: People Ops assigns mentor at offer-acceptance. Prefer same subteam; if not available, assign cross-team mentor with similar stack.
2) 2-week overlap: mentor should block 4h/week for explicit pairing and 2x 30-min office hours slots per week.
3) Shadowing slots: week 1 schedule includes 3 shadow sessions (stack walk, infra tour, dev environment setup).
4) Weekly sync template: 15m check-in + 45m pairing (focus area). Mentor fills short note in shared doc after each session.

30/60/90-day checklist (tangible tasks):
- Day 0 (pre-start): Slack account, laptop spec/laptop build ticket created, manager welcome email sent, first-day schedule shared.
- Week 1:
  - Complete HR paperwork + security training
  - Local dev environment up + run test-suite locally
  - Repo tour: identify main repo and three high-level services it touches
  - Submit first "hello-world" PR (small README or lint fix)
- End of month 1:
  - Ship a small but non-trivial PR (bugfix or minor feature) with full review cycle
  - Add 1 doc to knowledge base (how-to, runbook, or troubleshooting note)
  - 1:1 with manager to review goals and blockers
- End of month 2:
  - Lead a small feature / own a bug scope end-to-end
  - Demonstrate ability to triage production issue (with mentor support)
  - Peer feedback collected from 2+ reviewers
- End of month 3:
  - Conversion review packet prepared (manager + mentor notes, PR list, learning plan)
  - Clear recommendation: convert / extend / develop (capture reasons)

Onboarding curriculum (suggested modules):
- Week 1: Company mission + infra rights + dev environment + first PR
- Week 2: Service architecture + observability tools (Console walkthrough, logs/tracing)
- Week 3: Testing & CI/CD patterns (how we do incremental deployments, canary flows)
- Week 4: Product/Customer context: sit in 1 customer call, read 2 product docs
- Ongoing: Security best practices, cost-awareness (Intro to Redwood Optimize), model serving basics (high-level)

Mini learning checkpoints (for mentors to sign off):
- Can run and debug core service locally
- Understand deployment / tracing flow for common failure mode
- Can add a meaningful unit/integration test

Sample interview rubric for early-career final-round (conversion or hire-from-intern):
- Technical competence (40%) - Writes correct, idiomatic code; communicates tradeoffs; basic data structures/algorithms 
- Problem solving & learning velocity (25%) - Ability to break down ambiguous problems, asks clarifying q's, learns from feedback
- Collaboration & communication (20%) - Clear PR descriptions, responds to feedback, participates in cross-functional syncs
- Ownership / product sense (15%) - Prioritizes, follows through on tickets, asks good clarifying product questions
Scoring: 5-point scale per area, 3 = meets bar for entry-level. Conversion threshold: average >= 3.5 and no area below 2.

Example conversion packet checklist (for manager):
- List of PRs (urls) and short impact statement (1–2 lines each)
- Mentor notes (summary of pairing sessions and sign-off)
- Feedback from 2 reviewers (copied replies or summary)
- Mentee self-reflection (1 page max)
- Manager recommendation + development plan if not converted

Campus / recruiting touchpoints (early signals):
- Pre-start: campus orientation invite + alumni mentor if applicable
- Conversion window: recruiting provides shortlist of interns for fast-track conversion interviews 3–4 weeks before end of internship
- Debrief: final campus interview includes manager + mentor + hiring committee rep

Metrics to track (people ops + eng ops):
- Time-to-first-PR (median days)
- 30/60/90 completion rates (binary per milestone)
- Conversion rate (intern->FTE within 120 days of offer)
- Mentor load (no. of mentees per mentor), mentor NPS
- Early churn (voluntary exits within 12 months)

Feedback & continuous improvement flow:
- Pulse survey at 2 weeks, 6 weeks, 12 weeks (3 questions + free text)
- Mentor retro every month for cohort feedback (15m async + 30m live)
- Quarterly review of modules: update curriculum + add new checklist items if recurring blockers seen

Operational notes & small helpers (practical pointers):
- Use the shared "New Grad Onboarding" calendar (People Ops) to auto-populate first 6 weeks slots
- Template PR checklist for first PR: add reviewer, link to ticket, test steps, local reproduction steps
- Slack channels: #new-hires (social), #eng-onboarding (technical help)
- Default mentor weekly template: what we did, what you should try next, 1 blocker, 1 win

Open questions / TODOs (explicit):
- Decide conversion committee composition for early-career hires (HR suggestion: manager + mentor + one other team lead). TODO: Ethan to draft proposal by 2025-11-28.
- Mentor stipend / recognition: should mentors get formal credit in perf cycles? Maya to discuss with Eng Mgrs.
- Pilot measurement: pick two teams (Serving Runtime, Console) for Jan 2026 pilot. People Ops to confirm capacity.

Quick templates (copy into Confluence or new hire packet):
- "First 90 days" slide (one-pager) - bullet goals + checklist
- Mentor sign-off short form: "Has mentee run local infra? yes/no; has mentee shipped PR? yes/no; mentor comments"

Meeting notes (recent sync 2025-11-20):
- Liam: suggested pairing 1 mentor per 2 mentees max
- Devon: wants stronger observability module in week 2 (add Console walkthrough + live debugging session)
- Outcome: update curriculum and run pilot with two hires in Dec cohort

Next steps (action owners):
- Sofia: finalize playbook + move to Confluence page (due 2025-12-01)
- Ethan: draft conversion committee proposal (due 2025-11-28)
- Maya: confirm mentor recognition approach with eng leads (due 2025-12-05)
- People Ops: schedule Dec pilot cohort and notify participating teams (due 2025-11-30)

Notes to self / informal: 
- Keep this doc intentionally lightweight — managers want checklists, not essays.
- Track blockers in a single spreadsheet for Q1 so we can iterate quickly.
- Consider a short "mentor playbook" separate doc with examples of pairing exercises (pair-programming prompts, micro-architectural walkthrough checklist).

Raw links / references used while drafting:
- Confluence template: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/PE/early-career-onboarding-template
- HR ticket for stipend discussion: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/HR-412
- Onboarding scripts repo (starter scripts): https://github.com/redwood-inference/onboarding-scripts/pull/98

End of draft.

title: Account Synthesis — SLI Playbook for Customer Reviews
preview:
Context and intent
This is a lightweight, opinionated playbook we can use when prepping account-level review materials (monthly/quarterly). Goal: ensure CS, Solutions Eng, and Observability teams speak the same measurement language and can quickly derive flags for renewal/expansion conversations.

High-level rules of engagement (notes to self)
- Use the same 30/90/365 windows consistently across accounts in a single review; prefer 30d as the short window, 90d for trend, 365d for annualized anomalies.
- Percentile latency semantics: explicit about request types (generate vs embed vs rerank) and sequence length buckets.
- For private/dedicated customers call out any on-prem telemetry gaps (we may need agent logs vs control-plane metrics).
- When in doubt, flag and attach raw time-series for engineering to investigate — don’t guess on remediation steps in a CS deck.

Metric definitions (canonical)
1) Request latency (ms) — generation and embeddings
- Definition: measured time from API ingress (received) to first-byte output for streaming endpoints; for non-streaming, measurement is request start to final response sent.
- Percentiles to report: p50, p95, p99.
- Bucket by: endpoint (generate/embed/rerank), model family, and sequence-length category (<=128, 129–512, >512 tokens).
- Notes: streaming first-byte is critical for UX; downstream time (client-side parsing) excluded.
- Example: "Gen-p95 (<=128) = 210 ms" means 95% of generate requests with ≤128 tokens returned first byte within 210ms.

Alerting guidance (latency)
- Warning: p95 above SLO for two consecutive days OR 30d moving average p95 > SLO
- Critical: p99 above SLO for any 1-hour window with calls > 1000 (or customer-specific throughput threshold)
- Suggested remediation ladder: check routing fallbacks, check autoscale events, check model variant/quantization changes, check cache hit regressions.

2) Uptime / availability
- Definition: percentage of successful requests (HTTP 2xx) out of total requests (excluding planned maintenance windows) during the measurement period. Count region-specific availability and aggregate to global only if customer uses multi-region.
- Include two flavors: control-plane availability (console/API + auth) and data-plane availability (inference endpoints). For renewal conversations, emphasize data-plane.
- SLA translation: 99.9% uptime = 43.2 minutes downtime monthly.

Uptime detection nuances
- Short bursts of errors (e.g., 30m of 500s) matter more for synchronous apps than background batch workloads.
- For cache/backpressure failures that return 200 with degraded content, include an annotation field but do not count as failure unless contractually defined.

3) Cost per token (effective cost allocation)
- Definition: allocated $ to the customer divided by billed token-equivalent units. For internal reviews we use an "effective cost" (hosting + amortized infra + cache savings adjustment) rather than raw cloud bill.
- Breakdown components: model runtime cost (GPU time), IO and networking, caching savings, dedicated pool overhead (if on Dedicated), and per-request ephemeral costs.
- Measurement cadence: report TCO per 1M tokens (generation tokens and embedding tokens normalized).
- Notes on comparability: embeddings are cheaper per token compute-wise but often denser in usage; call out usage mix.

Cost heuristics and watch flags
- >20% month-over-month increase in effective $/1M tokens attributable to model changes or cache eviction is a red-flag.
- Large oscillations due to bursty traffic should be annualized before calling as a trend.

4) Cache hit rate (prefix & KV cache semantics)
- Definition: percentage of inference requests served using cached state (prefix or KV cache) divided by total eligible requests.
- Prefix cache: identical prompt prefixes where we can reuse past outputs or model state. KV cache: internal attention/key-value cache reuse across sessions for long conversations.
- Measurement nuance: Denominator should include only requests eligible for caching (e.g., same model + same prompt canonicalization rules).

Cache taxonomy and its effects
- Cold-starts: new sessions that cannot use cache — expected lower hit rate initially.
- Cross-region cache miss: if routing changes region, cache hit rate may drop; annotate per-region.
- Effective impact: estimate tokens saved per hit (average length * hit rate) and translate to $ saved in the cost table.

Suggested thresholds and escalation triggers
- Healthy: cache hit >= 65% for conversational-heavy apps with sticky sessions.
- Warning: 40–64% with measurable increase in $/token or latency.
- Critical: <40% sustained for 30d or sudden drop >25 percentage points in 7d.

Sample KPI table (text table for copy/paste into slides)
Columns: Metric | Window | Baseline | SLO/Target | Current | Trend | Action
Rows (examples):
Latency (gen p95 <=128) | 30d | 180 ms | <=250 ms | 312 ms | up 18% vs 90d | investigate routing / consider smaller model variant
Uptime (data-plane) | 30d | 99.98% | >=99.9% | 99.85% | down 0.13pp | open SRE ticket, propose fallback config
Cost ($/1M tokens effective) | 30d | $42 | <=$50 | $57 | up 36% | analyze cache hit and model variant changes
Cache hit rate (eligible requests) | 30d | 72% | >=65% | 54% | down 18pp | check prompt normalization, region routing, cache TTLs

How to use this table in a review
- Put trends and one-line action items on the first slide for execs; attach the raw time-series and recent incidents in appendix.
- For renewals: translate metric deltas into business pain (latency -> CX drop, cost -> margin pressure, uptime -> direct SLA breaches).

Playbook checklist (for CS owner prepping an account review)
- [ ] Pull 30/90/365 windows for each metric and annotate any maintenance windows.
- [ ] Confirm which endpoints and models are in-use for the account (embed vs gen mix).
- [ ] Compute effective $/1M tokens and compare to plan; include cache adjustments.
- [ ] Tag any anomalies with supporting links to logs, SRE tickets, and PRs.
- [ ] Draft recommended technical asks (e.g., enable prefix caching for route X, apply fallback to smaller model Y, change autoscale policy).

Open questions / decisions to make (working)
- How do we canonicalize prompt normalization rules for cache eligibility across customers that perform client-side templating? (pending engineering RFC)
- Should we expose an "effective-cost" field in the console for customers so renewal conversations have a shared reference? (proposal: opt-in beta)

Appendix: quick calculation examples
- Translating cache hits to token savings: avg response length 120 tokens, hit rate 60% -> tokens saved per 1M requests = 1,000,000 * 120 * 0.6 = 72M tokens. Multiply by effective $/1M to get savings.
- Uptime to allowed downtime: 99.9% = 0.1% downtime -> 0.001 * 30d * 24 * 60 = ~43.2 minutes/month.

Notes to collaborators
- Ethan: can you attach the last three incident digests to the account folder?
- Sofia: please confirm whether the Dedicated pool overhead for AcmeCorp changed in October; include new amortized number if so.
- Lina: can SRE produce a per-region cache hit drill for accounts with multi-region routing?

End of draft — use as editable source for slides and renewal playbook checklist.
text:
Context and intent
This is a lightweight, opinionated playbook we can use when prepping account-level review materials (monthly/quarterly). Goal: ensure CS, Solutions Eng, and Observability teams speak the same measurement language and can quickly derive flags for renewal/expansion conversations.

High-level rules of engagement (notes to self)
- Use the same 30/90/365 windows consistently across accounts in a single review; prefer 30d as the short window, 90d for trend, 365d for annualized anomalies.
- Percentile latency semantics: explicit about request types (generate vs embed vs rerank) and sequence length buckets.
- For private/dedicated customers call out any on-prem telemetry gaps (we may need agent logs vs control-plane metrics).
- When in doubt, flag and attach raw time-series for engineering to investigate — don’t guess on remediation steps in a CS deck.

Metric definitions (canonical)
1) Request latency (ms) — generation and embeddings
- Definition: measured time from API ingress (received) to first-byte output for streaming endpoints; for non-streaming, measurement is request start to final response sent.
- Percentiles to report: p50, p95, p99.
- Bucket by: endpoint (generate/embed/rerank), model family, and sequence-length category (<=128, 129–512, >512 tokens).
- Notes: streaming first-byte is critical for UX; downstream time (client-side parsing) excluded.
- Example: "Gen-p95 (<=128) = 210 ms" means 95% of generate requests with ≤128 tokens returned first byte within 210ms.

Alerting guidance (latency)
- Warning: p95 above SLO for two consecutive days OR 30d moving average p95 > SLO
- Critical: p99 above SLO for any 1-hour window with calls > 1000 (or customer-specific throughput threshold)
- Suggested remediation ladder: check routing fallbacks, check autoscale events, check model variant/quantization changes, check cache hit regressions.

2) Uptime / availability
- Definition: percentage of successful requests (HTTP 2xx) out of total requests (excluding planned maintenance windows) during the measurement period. Count region-specific availability and aggregate to global only if customer uses multi-region.
- Include two flavors: control-plane availability (console/API + auth) and data-plane availability (inference endpoints). For renewal conversations, emphasize data-plane.
- SLA translation: 99.9% uptime = 43.2 minutes downtime monthly.

Uptime detection nuances
- Short bursts of errors (e.g., 30m of 500s) matter more for synchronous apps than background batch workloads.
- For cache/backpressure failures that return 200 with degraded content, include an annotation field but do not count as failure unless contractually defined.

3) Cost per token (effective cost allocation)
- Definition: allocated $ to the customer divided by billed token-equivalent units. For internal reviews we use an "effective cost" (hosting + amortized infra + cache savings adjustment) rather than raw cloud bill.
- Breakdown components: model runtime cost (GPU time), IO and networking, caching savings, dedicated pool overhead (if on Dedicated), and per-request ephemeral costs.
- Measurement cadence: report TCO per 1M tokens (generation tokens and embedding tokens normalized).
- Notes on comparability: embeddings are cheaper per token compute-wise but often denser in usage; call out usage mix.

Cost heuristics and watch flags
- >20% month-over-month increase in effective $/1M tokens attributable to model changes or cache eviction is a red-flag.
- Large oscillations due to bursty traffic should be annualized before calling as a trend.

4) Cache hit rate (prefix & KV cache semantics)
- Definition: percentage of inference requests served using cached state (prefix or KV cache) divided by total eligible requests.
- Prefix cache: identical prompt prefixes where we can reuse past outputs or model state. KV cache: internal attention/key-value cache reuse across sessions for long conversations.
- Measurement nuance: Denominator should include only requests eligible for caching (e.g., same model + same prompt canonicalization rules).

Cache taxonomy and its effects
- Cold-starts: new sessions that cannot use cache — expected lower hit rate initially.
- Cross-region cache miss: if routing changes region, cache hit rate may drop; annotate per-region.
- Effective impact: estimate tokens saved per hit (average length * hit rate) and translate to $ saved in the cost table.

Suggested thresholds and escalation triggers
- Healthy: cache hit >= 65% for conversational-heavy apps with sticky sessions.
- Warning: 40–64% with measurable increase in $/token or latency.
- Critical: <40% sustained for 30d or sudden drop >25 percentage points in 7d.

Sample KPI table (text table for copy/paste into slides)
Columns: Metric | Window | Baseline | SLO/Target | Current | Trend | Action
Rows (examples):
Latency (gen p95 <=128) | 30d | 180 ms | <=250 ms | 312 ms | up 18% vs 90d | investigate routing / consider smaller model variant
Uptime (data-plane) | 30d | 99.98% | >=99.9% | 99.85% | down 0.13pp | open SRE ticket, propose fallback config
Cost ($/1M tokens effective) | 30d | $42 | <=$50 | $57 | up 36% | analyze cache hit and model variant changes
Cache hit rate (eligible requests) | 30d | 72% | >=65% | 54% | down 18pp | check prompt normalization, region routing, cache TTLs

How to use this table in a review
- Put trends and one-line action items on the first slide for execs; attach the raw time-series and recent incidents in appendix.
- For renewals: translate metric deltas into business pain (latency -> CX drop, cost -> margin pressure, uptime -> direct SLA breaches).

Playbook checklist (for CS owner prepping an account review)
- [ ] Pull 30/90/365 windows for each metric and annotate any maintenance windows.
- [ ] Confirm which endpoints and models are in-use for the account (embed vs gen mix).
- [ ] Compute effective $/1M tokens and compare to plan; include cache adjustments.
- [ ] Tag any anomalies with supporting links to logs, SRE tickets, and PRs.
- [ ] Draft recommended technical asks (e.g., enable prefix caching for route X, apply fallback to smaller model Y, change autoscale policy).

Open questions / decisions to make (working)
- How do we canonicalize prompt normalization rules for cache eligibility across customers that perform client-side templating? (pending engineering RFC)
- Should we expose an "effective-cost" field in the console for customers so renewal conversations have a shared reference? (proposal: opt-in beta)

Appendix: quick calculation examples
- Translating cache hits to token savings: avg response length 120 tokens, hit rate 60% -> tokens saved per 1M requests = 1,000,000 * 120 * 0.6 = 72M tokens. Multiply by effective $/1M to get savings.
- Uptime to allowed downtime: 99.9% = 0.1% downtime -> 0.001 * 30d * 24 * 60 = ~43.2 minutes/month.

Notes to collaborators
- Ethan: can you attach the last three incident digests to the account folder?
- Sofia: please confirm whether the Dedicated pool overhead for AcmeCorp changed in October; include new amortized number if so.
- Lina: can SRE produce a per-region cache hit drill for accounts with multi-region routing?

End of draft — use as editable source for slides and renewal playbook checklist.

title: quiet-launch-15-day-skill-anchoring-and-interview-evidence-kit
preview:
Context and intent:
This is a compact, low-friction onboarding and interview-evidence bundle intended for small IC hires (engineer / infra / platform) who will be given a quiet/solo launch for their first 15 days. The goal is to reduce cognitive load, create a defensible skill signal for cross-functional partners, and accelerate contributor impact while keeping mentor time bounded. Draft for People Ops + hiring manager alignment before piloting with 2 hires.

High-level goals:
- First 15 days: remove >80% of non-essential access friction, ship 1 microtask to prod or customer-facing integration, and capture structured evidence for performance calibration.
- Mentor time budget: 3–4 hours/week active pairing + asynchronous feedback.
- Hiring alignment: ensure interview evidence maps to day-1–15 success signals so we can correlate interview rubric to ramp outcomes.

Acceptance criteria for quiet launch pilot (success signals):
- New hire completes infra onboarding checklist and deploys microtask within 10 business days.
- Hiring manager rates 5/7 on independence and 6/7 on code quality in the day-15 pulse.
- Mentor confirms at least one asynchronous code review with constructive feedback captured in the evidence log.
- No more than 2 outstanding access tickets remain after day 3.

Pre-hire checklist (People Ops pre-reqs):
- Confirm offer cadence and day-0 equipment shipment date.
- Provision core accounts (G Suite, Okta, Redwood VPN, internal dev org) before day 0.
- Create onboarding calendar invite series: day0-7 sync, day8-15 ramp check.
- Assign primary mentor and buddy (names + Slack handles).
- Add to onboarding tracker and email hiring manager + infra SRE for early context.

Interview evidence mapping (how interview signals connect to day-15 outcomes):
- System design exercise -> ability to own microtask architecture and write rollout steps.
- Pair-programming diff -> independent PR hygiene, basic CI fixes.
- Behavioral probe (autonomy) -> escalation choices and communication frequency.
- Coding test -> passes baseline unit tests and can extend an existing test.

15-day onboarding plan (candidate-facing summary):
Day 0: welcome + infra access validation
- 30m welcome call (People Ops): logistics, payroll, benefits links, timezone scheduling.
- 60m manager 1:1 (context, team norms, immediate goals).
- Access validation (Okta, VPN, repo, CI access).
- Quick wins doc: microtask suggestion list (3 scoped tasks).

Day 1–3: environment & microtask pick
- Follow the 5-step dev-env checklist and verify by opening first issue.
- Mentor pairs for 1h to walk codebase layout and CI.
- Pick microtask and draft implementation plan in PR template.
- Evidence capture: create the evidence-card in Google Drive (link in tracker).

Day 4–9: implement & iterate
- Daily async updates in #onboard-[name] channel (3 bullets max).
- One 60m pair-review session mid-week with mentor.
- Open PR with tests and follow CI until green.
- QA + canary deploy to staging if available.

Day 10–15: canary, handoff, and retrospective
- Coordinate short canary with PM/QA (10–30m).
- Finalize docs: README snippet + rollout steps.
- Day-15 review: manager + mentor + People Ops quick sync (30m) to fill scored pulse.
- Publish evidence-card (artifact list: PR link, test run screenshot, deployment log excerpt).

Mentor & manager responsibilities (timeboxed):
- Mentor: 3–4 hours/week (pairing + reviews + 2 async notes).
- Manager: 30m twice (day0 & day15), ad-hoc priority alignment.
- People Ops: 30m day0 setup, 30m day15 retrospective.

Interview rubric (compact version we use to correlate to ramp):
Format: each probe scored 1–5 with a short evidence sentence.
- Technical breadth (system design, 1–5): assesses ability to pick appropriate tradeoffs for small services. Evidence required: design diagram or bullet tradeoffs.
- Code hygiene and tests (1–5): quality of PRs, test coverage. Evidence required: example PR link.
- Autonomous debugging (1–5): ability to triage and fix simple incidents. Evidence required: incident note or postmortem fragment.
- Communication & async updates (1–5): clarity of status updates and issue descriptions. Evidence required: Slack thread or issue comment.
- Cultural fit / prioritize (1–5): alignment to team norms and prioritization choices. Evidence required: short manager note.

Scoring guidance (quick):
- 4–5: candidate demonstrates clear signals and minimal coaching required for microtask work.
- 3: can do with some light mentoring; target for initial hire bucket.
- 1–2: mismatch for quiet-launch; escalate to manager for expanded onboarding plan.

Evidence-card template (one-pager we store per hire):
- PR(s) delivered: <urls>
- Tests + CI status: <summary>
- Deploy log excerpt: <snippet>
- Mentor notes: <bulleted lessons learned>
- Manager pulse scores: technical, autonomy, communication (numbers + 1-sentence rationale)
- Open friction tickets (list)

People Ops quick forms (to reduce back-and-forth):
- Day0 confirmation email template (copyable): Hi <name>, welcome — equipment and initial access details ...
- Day15 pulse form link placeholder (we will wire this to short Google Form)
- Access escalation instruction: if repo access fails, ping #infra-oncall with subject 'onboard-access <hire-name>'.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: candidate blocked on hardware or VPN for >48 hours. Mitigation: overnight backup cloud image for dev tasks, or schedule remote pairing with environment pre-provisioned.
- Risk: mentor bandwidth drops. Mitigation: assign secondary buddy and fallback office hours time-block.
- Risk: interview rubric mismatch. Mitigation: capture granular evidence early and run a 30-day correlation review for pilot hires.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Finalize small-bounty microtask list per team (ask eng leads). (TODO: Priya)
- Wire Day15 pulse to People Ops form and autopopulate into hire tracker. (TODO: Rafael)
- Agree on 2 pilot hires (who and when) and share metrics baseline. (TODO: Samir + hiring managers)

Appendix: quick templates (copy into offer/onboard sequences)
- Subject: Welcome to Redwood — Day 0 checklist
  Body: Hi <name> — congrats. Please find your day-0 checklist below... [link to drive card]

- Day15 pulse sample (questions):
  1) Did the hire ship the microtask? Y/N
  2) Rate independence 1–7
  3) Note one clear improvement area

Notes to self (informal):
- Keep this intentionally lightweight. We expect variance across teams; the pilot's point is to measure how interview signals map to ramp, not to enforce a strict regimen.
- If pilot shows high mentor load, move to an extended 30-day quiet launch with more timeboxed mentor hours.

Last updated: 2025-11-18 by Ava Chen
