title: Early Performance Hypotheses and Trial Onramp Checklist
preview:
Purpose
This working doc collects a compact, evidence-driven onramp for candidate evaluation and the first 30-day success hypotheses we expect to validate for IC and senior IC technical hires. Aim: reduce time-to-decision, standardize trial assignments, and capture signal that maps to Day 30 outcomes.

Audience
- Recruiting and hiring managers
- Interview panel leads
- Hiring committee reviewers
- New hire onboarding partners

Context / why this is different
- We often over-index on whiteboard problems and miss signals for real-world triage, system design, and cross-team collaboration. This artifact proposes a short trial-centric rubric + compact onboarding checks to bridge interview signals to early performance outcomes (impact in 30d).

High-level flow
1) Intake: hiring manager lists top 3 impact areas for role (product metrics / ownerable area / cross-team dependency).
2) Lightweight trial: 1–3 small artifacts (code patch, design note, customer triage thread) representing actual job work. Trial capped: 6 hours of candidate time.
3) Interview slate: mix of small-system design, paired debugging, and behavioral scenarios anchored to trial artifacts.
4) Evidence capture: single-page candidate dossier summarizing signals across skill dimensions (craft, design, collaboration, learning).
5) Decision & onboarding alignment: hiring manager picks 3 Day-30 hypotheses to validate with the new hire and defines who will own each validation.

Trial assignment templates (pick one)
- Quick bug + PR: repo subset + failing unit test + spec to extend. Candidate has 2–4 hours to submit a small PR and a brief rationale doc.
- Incident triage memo: a short simulated customer incident and traces/logs. Candidate drafts priority action plan and a stakeholder communication blurb.
- Design micro-brief: 1–2 page note on scaling a simple service (caching, KV cache, batching tradeoffs). Include backwards compatibility and rollout plan.

Rubric (single-line descriptors to keep scoring compact)
- Problem framing (0/1/2): picked correct scope, clarified constraints.
- Solution tradeoffs (0/1/2): shows awareness of latency/cost/complexity and measurable tradeoffs.
- Implementation clarity (0/1/2): PR or doc is complete, testable, and understandable.
- Collaboration posture (0/1/2): communicates assumptions, asks clarifying Qs, proposes handoffs.
- Learning velocity (0/1/2): acknowledges gaps and proposes ramp plan.
- Ownership signals (0/1/2): identifies metrics or SLAs they'd own and rollback criteria.

Scoring guidance
- 9–12: Strong hire for immediate ramp into ownerable work.
- 6–8: Hire if complemented by strong collaborator or evidence of learning velocity; consider 60-day mentorship plan.
- 0–5: Insufficient signals for this role. Document observed gaps and suggest alternative roles (supporting IC, SRE, or rotational).

Evidence capture template (single page, submit to hiring packet)
- Candidate name:
- Role applied:
- Trial type used: (bug / incident / design)
- Raw rubric scores (list):
- Key qualitative notes (3 bullets):
- Risk areas (3 bullets):
- Recommended Day-30 hypotheses (3 short statements):
- Suggested onboarding owner(s):

Day-30 hypothesis examples (to copy-edit into each role packet)
- Hypothesis: Candidate will independently own the data pipeline ingestion flow and reduce mean time to alert from 45m to under 20m for the component within 30 days; Validation: has triaged 2 production alerts and authored a runbook for the top failure mode.
- Hypothesis: Candidate will ship a low-risk performance patch that improves p50 latency by >=10% for the targeted endpoint; Validation: PR merged, metrics measured and shared.
- Hypothesis: Candidate will demonstrate cross-team coordination by owning a small RFC and scheduling at least two stakeholder syncs; Validation: meeting notes + RFC version history.

Onboarding checklist (minimum baseline, to be assigned during offer acceptance)
- Pre-day-1 deliverables: access requests made (repo + infra view), trial artifacts saved into People folder, initial meeting with buddy scheduled.
- Day 1–7: reading list (product brief, service playbook), pair with buddy on 1 small triage, shadow on-call rotation setup.
- Week 2–4: take ownership of 1 small backlog item from trial, present a 10-minute show-and-tell to the immediate pod.
- Week 5–8: start executing Day-30 hypotheses, weekly sync with hiring manager and mentoring checkpoints.

Notes to interviewers (quick dos/don’ts)
- Do: anchor questions to the trial artifact, seek to observe how candidate reduces ambiguity.
- Don’t: require large contrived algorithms; prefer practical tradeoffs.
- Do: call out any environmental assumptions and note whether candidate asked clarifying Qs.

Logistics and fairness considerations
- Trial must be optional and reasonable (max 6 hours unpaid); candidates can request an alternate assignment (e.g., whiteboard + take-home summary) for accommodation.
- All rubric scores must be recorded in the dossier; anonymous notes acceptable, but final recommendation should be explicit about gaps.
- For senior IC roles, require at least 2 interviewers from cross-functional teams to validate collaboration posture.

Open questions / items to test in pilot
- Should we make trial assignments role-specific or standardized per ladder band?
- What’s the conversion delta (interview->hire) when trial is offered vs not offered? Track for next 3 months.
- Does requiring a trial reduce acceptance rate? Add questions to offer survey.

Immediate next steps (owner: Maya)
- Run a 4-role pilot in Q1 2026: infra-engineer (IC3), product platform (IC4), SRE (IC3), infra-tooling (IC2).
- Create 3 standard trial templates in the recruitment GitHub repo and link to job descriptions.
- Build a one-page scoring sheet in Greenhouse for the hiring committee to use.

Appendix: sample scoring sheet (copy into candidate dossier)
- Candidate: _________  Role: ________  Trial: ________  Date: ________
- Scores: Problem framing /2, Tradeoffs /2, Implementation /2, Collaboration /2, Learning /2, Ownership /2. Total /12.
- Recommendation: Hire / Hire with mentorship / No hire. Rationale (2–3 sentences).

Rough meeting notes from brainstorm (raw ideas)
- "Make trials short and factual; people who ship fast are often better signals than perfect design answers."
- "We should track acceptance delta; if too high we can make trial optional or provide honoraria for senior roles."
- "Pairing tests for collaboration are gold — hard to fake in a phone screen."

Version history
- 2025-11-04: initial note (Maya)
- 2025-11-10: added rubric and trial templates (Omar)
- 2025-11-18: added Day-30 hypotheses and pilot plan (Sofia)

Purpose
This working doc collects a compact, evidence-driven onramp for candidate evaluation and the first 30-day success hypotheses we expect to validate for IC and senior IC technical hires. Aim: reduce time-to-decision, standardize trial assignments, and capture signal that maps to Day 30 outcomes.

Audience
- Recruiting and hiring managers

Context / why this is different
- We often over-index on whiteboard problems and miss signals for real-world triage, system design, and cross-team collaboration. This artifact proposes a short trial-centric rubric + compact onboarding checks to bridge interview signals to early performance outcomes (impact in 30d).

High-level flow
1) Intake: hiring manager lists top 3 impact areas for role (product metrics / ownerable area / cross-team dependency).
2) Lightweight trial: 1–3 small artifacts (code patch, design note, customer triage thread) representing actual job work. Trial capped: 6 hours of candidate time.
3) Interview slate: mix of small-system design, paired debugging, and behavioral scenarios anchored to trial artifacts.
4) Evidence capture: single-page candidate dossier summarizing signals across skill dimensions (craft, design, collaboration, learning).
5) Decision & onboarding alignment: hiring manager picks 3 Day-30 hypotheses to validate with the new hire and defines who will own each validation.

Trial assignment templates (pick one)
- Quick bug + PR: repo subset + failing unit test + spec to extend. Candidate has 2–4 hours to submit a small PR and a brief rationale doc.
- Incident triage memo: a short simulated customer incident and traces/logs. Candidate drafts priority action plan and a stakeholder communication blurb.
- Design micro-brief: 1–2 page note on scaling a simple service (caching, KV cache, batching tradeoffs). Include backwards compatibility and rollout plan.

Rubric (single-line descriptors to keep scoring compact)
- Problem framing (0/1/2): picked correct scope, clarified constraints.
- Solution tradeoffs (0/1/2): shows awareness of latency/cost/complexity and measurable tradeoffs.
- Implementation clarity (0/1/2): PR or doc is complete, testable, and understandable.
- Collaboration posture (0/1/2): communicates assumptions, asks clarifying Qs, proposes handoffs.
- Learning velocity (0/1/2): acknowledges gaps and proposes ramp plan.
- Ownership signals (0/1/2): identifies metrics or SLAs they'd own and rollback criteria.

Scoring guidance
- 9–12: Strong hire for immediate ramp into ownerable work.
- 6–8: Hire if complemented by strong collaborator or evidence of learning velocity; consider 60-day mentorship plan.
- 0–5: Insufficient signals for this role. Document observed gaps and suggest alternative roles (supporting IC, SRE, or rotational).

Evidence capture template (single page, submit to hiring packet)
- Candidate name:
- Role applied:
- Trial type used: (bug / incident / design)
- Raw rubric scores (list):
- Key qualitative notes (3 bullets):
- Risk areas (3 bullets):
- Recommended Day-30 hypotheses (3 short statements):
- Suggested onboarding owner(s):

Day-30 hypothesis examples (to copy-edit into each role packet)
- Hypothesis: Candidate will independently own the data pipeline ingestion flow and reduce mean time to alert from 45m to under 20m for the component within 30 days; Validation: has triaged 2 production alerts and authored a runbook for the top failure mode.
- Hypothesis: Candidate will ship a low-risk performance patch that improves p50 latency by >=10% for the targeted endpoint; Validation: PR merged, metrics measured and shared.
- Hypothesis: Candidate will demonstrate cross-team coordination by owning a small RFC and scheduling at least two stakeholder syncs; Validation: meeting notes + RFC version history.

Onboarding checklist (minimum baseline, to be assigned during offer acceptance)
- Pre-day-1 deliverables: access requests made (repo + infra view), trial artifacts saved into People folder, initial meeting with buddy scheduled.
- Day 1–7: reading list (product brief, service playbook), pair with buddy on 1 small triage, shadow on-call rotation setup.
- Week 2–4: take ownership of 1 small backlog item from trial, present a 10-minute show-and-tell to the immediate pod.
- Week 5–8: start executing Day-30 hypotheses, weekly sync with hiring manager and mentoring checkpoints.

Notes to interviewers (quick dos/don’ts)
- Do: anchor questions to the trial artifact, seek to observe how candidate reduces ambiguity.
- Don’t: require large contrived algorithms; prefer practical tradeoffs.
- Do: call out any environmental assumptions and note whether candidate asked clarifying Qs.

Logistics and fairness considerations
- Trial must be optional and reasonable (max 6 hours unpaid); candidates can request an alternate assignment (e.g., whiteboard + take-home summary) for accommodation.
- All rubric scores must be recorded in the dossier; anonymous notes acceptable, but final recommendation should be explicit about gaps.
- For senior IC roles, require at least 2 interviewers from cross-functional teams to validate collaboration posture.

Open questions / items to test in pilot
- Should we make trial assignments role-specific or standardized per ladder band?
- What’s the conversion delta (interview->hire) when trial is offered vs not offered? Track for next 3 months.
- Does requiring a trial reduce acceptance rate? Add questions to offer survey.

Immediate next steps (owner: Maya)
- Run a 4-role pilot in Q1 2026: infra-engineer (IC3), product platform (IC4), SRE (IC3), infra-tooling (IC2).
- Create 3 standard trial templates in the recruitment GitHub repo and link to job descriptions.
- Build a one-page scoring sheet in Greenhouse for the hiring committee to use.

Appendix: sample scoring sheet (copy into candidate dossier)
- Candidate: _________  Role: ________  Trial: ________  Date: ________
- Scores: Problem framing /2, Tradeoffs /2, Implementation /2, Collaboration /2, Learning /2, Ownership /2. Total /12.
- Recommendation: Hire / Hire with mentorship / No hire. Rationale (2–3 sentences).

Rough meeting notes from brainstorm (raw ideas)
- "Make trials short and factual; people who ship fast are often better signals than perfect design answers."
- "We should track acceptance delta; if too high we can make trial optional or provide honoraria for senior roles."
- "Pairing tests for collaboration are gold — hard to fake in a phone screen."

Version history
- 2025-11-04: initial note (Maya)
- 2025-11-10: added rubric and trial templates (Omar)
- 2025-11-18: added Day-30 hypotheses and pilot plan (Sofia)

title: Recruit prioritization matrix and probe bank
preview:
Purpose: quick decision-support for short-term hiring sprints (2–6 weeks) that balances business impact, role difficulty, and candidate fill-time. Working doc: use this during weekly recruiting syncs and interview calibration sessions.

Notes to self / context:
- Many teams need faster TTM for high-leverage ICs (infra, serving runtime, SRE). We only have limited recruiter hours and interview slots.
- Objective: create a lightweight, repeatable way to prioritize reqs and a small bank of behavioral probes that maps to our competency rubric.
- Keep interview probes short (30–90s read) so interviewers can land follow-ups rapidly.

1) Recruit Prioritization matrix (one-pager)
- Inputs (for each open req): business impact (1–5), time-to-value (weeks estimate), role difficulty (1–5), market scarcity (1–5), internal ramp cost (1–5).
- Scoring: weighted sum = (impact * 3) + (1 / time-to-value * 2) + (5 - role difficulty) + (5 - scarcity) + (5 - ramp_cost). The math is intentionally simple; higher = higher priority.
- Quick thresholds (for sprint):
  - Green: score >= 35 -> immediate top push (expedite interviews, offer lanes)
  - Amber: 25–34 -> standard cadence, increase sourcing if stagnates
  - Red: <25 -> deprioritize / freeze outbound until later sprint

Example (mock):
- Req: Serving Runtime Senior Engineer
  - business impact 5, time-to-value 6 weeks, role difficulty 5, scarcity 5, ramp cost 3
  - score = (5*3) + (1/6*2~=0) + (0) + (0) + (2) => ~17 -> Amber/Red -> consider warming pipeline but focus on internal mobility + stretch hires

2) Interview funnel micro-adjustments for sprint hires
- Slot budget per week (example): 8 first-round, 4 second-round, 2 final/offers. Reallocate from low-priority reqs when green flagged.
- Fast-track rules: if candidate meets >4 on both technical and product sense AND scorecard >= 18/25 from first-round, promote directly to hiring manager loop (skip take-home unless conflicting signals).

3) Core competency rubric (summary for scoring)
- Technical craft (0–5): depth of system knowledge, debugging pattern, architecture tradeoffs.
- Product sense & tradeoffs (0–5): ability to weigh lat/cost/reliability tradeoffs, user impact focus.
- Collaboration & ownership (0–5): async communication, cross-team influence, incident ownership examples.
- Learning velocity & humility (0–5): concrete examples of learning hard concepts quickly, code review examples.
- Communication clarity (0–5): ability to explain complex things simply.

Scoring note: aim for 3+ on at least three categories for mid-level ICs; 4+ across categories for senior.

4) Behavioral probe bank (30+ short probes grouped by competency)
- Technical craft probes:
  - \"Tell me about a time you diagnosed a production latency issue end-to-end. What data did you collect first?\"
  - \"Describe a decision you made to sacrifice latency for cost or vice versa. What tradeoffs did you test?\"
  - \"When you learned a new infra concept under time pressure, what did you read/try first?\"

- Product & tradeoffs probes:
  - \"Give an example where you pushed back on a product request for technical reasons. How did you articulate risk to PMs?\"
  - \"How have you prioritized performance work that benefits multiple teams vs a single team?\"

- Collaboration & ownership probes:
  - \"Walk me through a time you led a postmortem. How did you ensure blamelessness?\"
  - \"Describe a cross-functional disagreement you resolved. What concessions did you make?\"

- Learning & humility probes:
  - \"Tell me about a technical area you previously misunderstood. How did you correct course?\"
  - \"Give an example of feedback you received that changed how you work.\"

- Communication clarity probes:
  - \"Explain a complex system you built to a non-technical stakeholder in two minutes.\"
  - \"Describe an instance where you simplified a design to make it easier for the team to maintain.\"

Probe usage guidance:
- Pick 1–2 probes per competency for a 45m first-round. Reserve deeper scenario probes (root-cause debugging, architecture tradeoff) for second-round.
- Use follow-up template: \"What was the problem? What options did you evaluate? What did you choose? What changed after?\"

5) Fast scorecard template (copyable for interviewers)
- Section: notes (free text)
- Scores: Technical craft / Product sense / Collaboration / Learning velocity / Communication (0–5 each)
- Recommendation: Strong hire / Hire / Borderline / No-hire (with 1–2 line rationale)

6) Onboarding mini-checklist for sprint hires (first 30 days, minimal viable ops)
- Pre-day 0: clear repo access, seeded runbook links, 1:1 schedule with manager and scribe assigned, first-week micro-project defined (small measurable deliverable).
- Week 1: local dev loop, pager readouts, join team slack channels, pair with buddy on first PR, run a small infra smoke test.
- Week 2–4: rotate through two key services, shadow on-call, demo micro-project to team at end of 30 days.

7) Quick templates (email/interview cadence)
- Outbound short message: \"Quick intro — we're fast-moving on [team]. Looking for engineers who ship infra improvements and care about latency/cost. Would you be open to a 30m chat this week?\"
- Interview schedule (example): 30m intro + cultural fit -> 60m technical deep-dive -> 45m system design -> brief hiring manager debrief (internal).

8) Open questions / unknowns (to resolve at next sync)
- Weighting formula needs calibration across 3 hiring managers (Priya, Diego, Riley) — propose running retrospective next sprint with five closed reqs.
- Need recruiter time allocation dashboard (can recruiting ops expose live slots?). JIRA ticket created (JIRA-ENG-7812).
- Candidate score normalization: propose pilot where three interviewers score same 2 candidates each sprint to quantify inter-rater variance.

9) Next steps (owners & timeline)
- Priya: run priority matrix for active 16 reqs by Thu (owner: Priya) — due 2025-11-07.
- Diego: add 10 probes into rubric doc in Confluence and map to competency tags (owner: Diego) — due 2025-11-10.
- Riley: schedule recruiter sync to reallocate slots for top-tier green reqs (owner: Riley) — due 2025-11-12.

Roughs / TODOs / scratch notes:
- Consider adding a short take-home for senior infra roles only; default = no take-home unless candidate is borderline or distributed remote and we need deeper signal.
- Experiment: 15m \"ops triage\" exercise in first-round for candidates who will join on-call (timebox scenario).

Footer: this is a living document — copy into team folder when mature. Keep probes short and conversational.

title: talent-rotation-rubric-and-90-day-buddy-sprint
preview:
Context and purpose:
This is a working doc for an experimental cross-role \"talent rotation\" hiring and onboarding pattern intended for early-stage hires and lateral ICs joining cross-functional pods. The goal is to: shorten time-to-productive, preserve hiring signal via a structured interview/debrief rubric, and validate fit quickly with a 90-day buddy/rotation sprint instead of a traditional 1:1 onboarding loop.

Who this is for:
- IC hires (SWE, ML infra, Platform, SRE, DevRel) at IC2–IC4 levels where initial scope is pod-level.
- Hiring managers who want a repeatable debrief template and scoring guidance.
- People Ops and Hiring Committee as an evidence package for fast offers.

High-level flow (timeline):
1) Screen -> take-home (optional) -> panel interviews (2 rounds) -> decision within 7 business days.
2) Offer accepted -> preboarding (one-week pre-arrival checklist).
3) Day 1–7: orientation + paired shadow sessions with assigned buddy.
4) Week 2–6: rotational sprints (2-week micro-projects across platform, infra, and product touchpoints).
5) Week 7–12: owner of a small scoped ticket and final presentation to pod + hiring manager.
6) Week 12: 90-day debrief and final confirmation for long-term placement.

Interview rubric (single-sheet guidance for interviewers):
Use 1–5 scale where 3 = meets expectations for role level, 4 = comfortably exceeds, 2 = below expectations but trainable, 1 = unacceptable / critical gap, 5 = exceptional.
Scoring axes (short definitions and sample prompts):
- Technical competence (depth): Can they reason about core system components? Prompt: \"Walk me through a recent design or debugging incident.\"
- Architectural judgment (system design): Trade-offs and capacity planning; prompt: \"Design a shard+cache strategy for a high-throughput KV workload.\"
- Problem decomposition (clarity of thought & experiment design): Prompt: \"How would you instrument and iterate on a flakey inference pipeline?\"
- Collaboration & communication: Cross-team empathy, conflict handling; prompt: \"Tell me about a time you changed a product decision after stakeholder feedback.\"
- Ownership & execution: Drive from idea to delivery; prompt: \"Describe a time you delivered a project with unclear requirements.\"
- Learning velocity & curiosity: Evidence of rapid ramp; prompt: \"What's a technical skill you taught yourself in <3 months and how did you apply it?\"
- Culture add / values alignment: Redwood values (ship, measure, iterate) — call out any red flags.

Scoring guidance (example):
- 5: Strong, specific evidence + impact metrics + shaping of team processes.
- 4: Multiple strong examples, measurable impact, minor gaps in breadth.
- 3: Solid on most axes, limited scale examples appropriate for level.
- 2: Has some skill but no clear impact or requires significant ramp.
- 1: Lack of required fundamentals or unsafe behaviors.

Debrief template (use in interviewer sync):
- Candidate name / role: 
- Interviewer: 
- Date/time: 
- Summary (2–3 sentences): quick synopsis of impression.
- Strengths (bulleted): concrete examples.
- Concerns / gaps (bulleted): clear mitigations or red flags.
- Role fit recommendation: hire / neutral / no-hire.
- Level recommendation: IC2 / IC3 / IC4 / escalate to manager.
- Required follow-ups: 1–2 targeted references or clarifying questions for an extra loop.

Evidence package for hiring committee (what to attach):
- Completed debriefs from at least 3 interviewers across disciplines.
- Example deliverable (take-home or past project repo) or code snippet with short explanation.
- Candidate-supplied learning plan or growth goals (optional).

90-day buddy rotation checklist (Day-level and goal-oriented):
Preboarding (week before start):
- Set up accounts + access matrix assigned by IT (list of required systems).
- Intro email + calendar invites for Day 1 + buddy pairing.
- Pre-read: short product/infra primer pack (2–3 pages).

Day 1–7: first impressions and shadowing
- Day 1: orientation, lunch with buddy, team standup, quick wins ticket assigned.
- Days 2–4: paired sessions: one-half day with infra lead, one-half day with product PM, one-half day with SRE.
- Day 5: 1:1 with hiring manager to clarify expectations and goals.

Week 2–6: rotational sprints (two-week cycles)
- Sprint 1 (Week 2–3): Diagnostics sprint — replicate a production incident in staging (mentored). Deliverable: postmortem-style note + remediation plan.
- Sprint 2 (Week 4–5): Feature-adjacent sprint — contribute a small feature/bug fix end-to-end. Deliverable: PR + short demo.
- Optional Sprint 3 (Week 6): Ops-focused sprint — create a dashboard or alert tuning doc. Deliverable: metric dashboard + runbook snippet.

Week 7–12: owner transition and final output
- Week 7–9: ownership of a small scoped ticket (same tech stack as pod). Deliverable: shipped change and monitoring.
- Week 10: prep final 15-min demo and a 1-page reflection (what went well, what I need).
- Week 12: 90-day review meeting (hiring manager + buddy + people ops). Decision: confirm placement / extend probation / reassign.

Success metrics for the experiment (quantitative + qualitative):
- Quant: Time-to-first-meaningful-merge (target <21 days), Number of production incidents caused by new hire (target 0 in first 90 days), % of hires confirmed to long-term placement after 90 days (target >85%).
- Qual: Hiring manager satisfaction score, buddy satisfaction score, candidate NPS after 90 days.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Candidate gets siloed; Mitigation: mandatory cross-pairing schedule and weekly rotation retrospective.
- Risk: Lack of hiring signal due to short interviews; Mitigation: mandatory sample deliverable + ref check targeted at recent project.
- Risk: Manager capacity to mentor; Mitigation: cap rotations per manager to 2 simultaneous new joiners and stipend for buddy time.

Open questions / TODOs (short-term):
- Define concrete access checklist for private deployments team (security approvals timeline).
- Draft a one-page preboarding packet tailored for ML infra hires.
- Pilot with 4 hires in Q1 2026 and measure success metrics.

Appendix: example interview prompts (copy-paste friendly)
- \"Explain a time you debugged an OOM in an inference pipeline. What were your first three investigations?\"
- \"Given a model with 10ms median latency and 95th percentile spikes to 200ms under load, how would you approach stabilizing tail latency?\"
- \"You have 2 GPUs and a bursty request pattern. How do you design cost-aware routing to balance latency and credits?\"

Notes to self (informal):
- Keep doc short for hiring managers; convert to checklist card in HR board.
- Sync with infra leads (Ravi) and ML Eng (Zoe) to finalize tech prompts.
- If pilot goes well, fold into Offer Flow and Hiring Committee rubric page.

End of working draft.

- Quant: Time-to-first-meaningful-merge (target <21 days), Number of production incidents caused by new hire (target 0 in first 90 days), % of hires confirmed to long-term placement after 90 days (target >85%).
- Qual: Hiring manager satisfaction score, buddy satisfaction score, candidate NPS after 90 days.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Candidate gets siloed; Mitigation: mandatory cross-pairing schedule and weekly rotation retrospective.
- Risk: Lack of hiring signal due to short interviews; Mitigation: mandatory sample deliverable + ref check targeted at recent project.
- Risk: Manager capacity to mentor; Mitigation: cap rotations per manager to 2 simultaneous new joiners and stipend for buddy time.

Open questions / TODOs (short-term):
- Define concrete access checklist for private deployments team (security approvals timeline).
- Draft a one-page preboarding packet tailored for ML infra hires.
- Pilot with 4 hires in Q1 2026 and measure success metrics.

Appendix: example interview prompts (copy-paste friendly)
- \"Explain a time you debugged an OOM in an inference pipeline. What were your first three investigations?\"
- \"Given a model with 10ms median latency and 95th percentile spikes to 200ms under load, how would you approach stabilizing tail latency?\"
- \"You have 2 GPUs and a bursty request pattern. How do you design cost-aware routing to balance latency and credits?\"

Notes to self (informal):
- Keep doc short for hiring managers; convert to checklist card in HR board.
- Sync with infra leads (Ravi) and ML Eng (Zoe) to finalize tech prompts.
- If pilot goes well, fold into Offer Flow and Hiring Committee rubric page.

End of working draft.

title: Cross-Functional Onboarding: Compact 45-Day Playbook & Micro-Rituals
preview:
Purpose:
This is a compact, pragmatic 45-day onboarding playbook tailored for cross-functional hires (PMs, Solutions Engineers, Data Analysts, Product Designers) who need a fast ramp into product, customers, and engineering partners. The goal is to provide a minimal set of high-impact activities, recurring micro-rituals, and a lightweight evidence rubric so managers and new hires know what ‘on track’ looks like.

Scope and assumptions:
- Applies to mid-level individual contributors (IC3–IC4 equivalent).
- Emphasizes asynchronous learning + short live syncs to reduce meeting load.
- Designed for hires joining product-adjacent teams who will interact with eng, infra, and customer success.

Roles & responsibilities (one-liners):
- Hiring manager: owns outcome, weekly 1:1s, priority mapping, success signals.
- Buddy: immediate triage for practical questions, shadowing, short demos.
- People Ops: coordinates access, equipment, compliance tasks, and first-week check-in.
- New hire: daily progress updates, owns at least one small deliverable by Day 30.

Quick start checklist (Day 0 -> Day 1 morning):
- Hardware & accounts delivered, verified (IT ticket closed).
- Access: GitHub, CI, Slack, Confluence, Jira, internal dashboards.
- First-day calendar blocks: 1:1 with manager, team huddle, buddy intro, security orientation, product overview.
- Slack intro message + short bio posted to #team channel.

45-day timeline (compact):
Day 0–3 — Stabilize
- Complete required security and compliance checklists.
- Configure dev environment or product sandbox (minimal working example).
- Manager: one short doc with top 3 priorities for next 45 days.
- Buddy: 30-minute "tour" of daily flows (how to read dashboards, typical customer SLA issues).

Day 4	6: Learn by doing
- Micro-learning sprints (2x 45m blocks per day): product demos + docs + 1 small hands-on task.
- Shadow 1 customer call (for CS-facing roles) or 1 deployment/design review for infra/product-facing roles.
- Complete 2 short quizzes: product domain fundamentals + safety/privacy basics.

Day 7	6/14: Early outputs
- Deliverable: short blameless summary of first bug/customer issue investigated, including steps taken and open questions.
- Manager check-in: align on scope of a 30-day small project.
- Cross-team intro: 15-minute syncs with key infra/observability/console owners.

Day 15	6/30: Embedded work
- Work on a scoped project (bug fix, small feature spec, customer triage playbook).
- Weekly demo to manager + 1 key partner (10 minutes).
- Start collecting launch/failure examples for quality and cost tradeoffs (for product and infra roles).

Day 31	6/45: Handoffs & autonomy
- Finalize small project; write a 1-page handoff with runbook/next steps.
- Manager: calibration meeting to decide next set of priorities and growth focus.
- New hire presents a 15-minute "What I fixed / What I learned" to the wider pod.

Micro-rituals (short recurring habits to form):
- Daily (10	615m): morning status in Slack thread (yesterday/today/blockers).
- Twice weekly (30m): sync with buddy for practical Q&A and demos.
- Weekly (20m): manager 1:1 focusing on priority alignment + 1 metric improvement.
- End of sprint (biweekly, 15m): demo + quick retrospective (what worked / what needs help).

Manager checklist & sample 1:1 template:
- Before Day 1: prepare priority doc (top 3 outcomes), invite cross-functional intros, confirm buddy.
- Weekly 1:1 template: 5m personal check-in, 10m progress vs priorities, 5m blockers/asks.
- Week 6 calibration: review evidence rubric and decide ramp status (on-track/needs-support/at-risk).

Buddy checklist (practical):
- Week 1: run through shell commands, environment, alert playbooks, common Slack channels.
- Week 2	6: sit in on one design/review meeting and explain meeting choreography.
- Ongoing: be available for 15-minute ad-hoc questions twice a week.

Lightweight evidence rubric (used by manager + people ops):
- Knowledge (score 0-3): understands product domain & primary customers. Evidence: can summarize a customer use case and list 3 pain points.
- Technical/operational fluency (0-3): can complete a small sandbox task end-to-end and diagnose a simple alert. Evidence: PR or runbook authored, incident triage notes.
- Collaboration (0-3): proactively engages cross-functional partners; attendance in required syncs and quality of ask. Evidence: meeting notes, follow-up actions.
- Deliverable impact (0-3): completed timely small project with clear handoff. Evidence: project doc + demo.
Interpretation: 9	612 = on track; 6	8 = needs support; <6 = manager & people ops support plan.

Sample Day 30 deliverable (template):
Title: "Sandbox to Production: Fix for customer latency regression"
Owner: [new hire]
Summary: 3-sentence problem statement.
What I did: steps, commands, PR link.
Open questions: 1-3 items.
Next steps: who to hand to, suggested SLA.

Feedback cadence & escalation:
- Week 2: short 15m people ops check-in to see if access/ops blockers remain.
- Week 4: manager + buddy calibration to confirm project scope and blocker removal.
- Week 6: formal ramp review + next 90-day plan.
If at-risk: manager files a short support plan (2	64 weeks) that includes clear expectations and dedicated checkpoints.

Common pitfalls & mitigation tips:
- Too many meetings: keep demos asynchronous (short recording + 5 bullet notes).
- Info overload: prefer "read-in-3" one-pagers instead of long Confluence dumps.
- Lack of context: require the new hire to publish a 1-page "current understanding" at Day 14 and Day 30.

Helpful templates & links (quick):
- Day 30 deliverable template: confluence://templates/day-30-deliverable
- Dev env checklist: confluence://playbooks/dev-env-quickstart
- Interview evidence rubric (for future hiring calibration): confluence://rubrics/interview-evidence

Notes to managers (tone & expectations):
- This playbook prioritizes autonomy & focused outputs over exhaustive orientation. Resist the impulse to add meetings; instead swap one meeting for an async demo.
- Use the evidence rubric early and often 
during weekly 1:1s to normalize data-driven ramp discussions.

Open questions / follow-ups (items people ops should track):
- Measure: what percentage of cross-functional hires hit "on-track" by Day 45? (propose instrumenting a simple form at Week 6).
- Should we create a small "sandbox badge" sign-off that indicates a hire can operate without 1:1 hand-holding for infra tasks?
- Pilot: try this format with 6 hires in the next quarter and collect time-to-first-impact and manager satisfaction scores.

Appendix: quick checklist for the first 45 days (copyable)
[ ] Equipment & accounts verified
[ ] First week calendar blocks scheduled
[ ] Buddy assigned and first buddy sync complete
[ ] Top 3 priorities doc shared by manager
[ ] Small project scoped & accepted by Day 14
[ ] Day 30 deliverable submitted + demo
[ ] Week 6 ramp review scheduled

End of playbook.

Notes: this is intentionally compact and pragmatic. Use as a basis for a lightweight template library and iterate based on the pilot.

title: Quiet bootcamp: 12-day hire activation guide
preview:
Purpose:
This is a compact, low-noise onboarding + interviewing experiment designed for hires who need to ramp quickly into product-impacting work while minimizing interruption across cross-functional teams. The ‘Quiet Bootcamp’ is intended for engineering and platform hires (IC or small leadership) where a short, focused 12-day activation reduces time-to-first-deliverable and avoids heavy synchronous interview/onboarding overhead.

Scope:
- Roles: backend infra, serving/runtime, SRE, platform engineering, developer experience engineers.
- Not intended for large-scale managerial programs, rotational grads, or long-term apprenticeship tracks.
- Works as both a final-stage interview format (trial week) and a rapid onboarding protocol after offer acceptance.

Design principles:
- Asynchronous-first: reduce meeting load for candidate and teams. Most evaluation and ramping work executed through written tasks, short recorded demos, and limited paired sessions.
- Evidence-focused: every score or pass/fail point must map to observable artifacts (PR, demo, log, infra runbook).
- Time-boxed: 12 calendar days with explicit daily goals and lightweight checkpoints.
- Low-friction hiring experience: clear instructions, templated repos, and a single coordinator for access and logistics.

Candidate timeline (12 days) - high level:
- Day -3 to 0 (preboard): access, account setup, welcome notes, small prework (read runbook, run a container).
- Day 1–3: environment & small impact task (setup + first PR).
- Day 4–6: medium-impact task + written systems design (asynchronous).
- Day 7–9: pairing session (2 hours total across two short sessions) + integration/bugfixs.
- Day 10–11: final deliverable (deployable prototype or PR) + recorded walkthrough.
- Day 12: retrospective and hiring debrief; decision and feedback.

Preboard checklist (owner: people-ops coordinator):
- Create account(s) and add to relevant Slack channels.
- Provide isolated sandbox repo and infra credits (temporary test project).
- Share a clear one-pager with timeline, expectations, scoring rubric, example tasks, and useful links.
- Assign a single onboarding buddy (engineering peer) and a hiring coordinator (people-ops).

Interview + evaluation rubric (asynchronous-first) - high level weights:
- Execution (code quality, CI, tests, PR hygiene): 40%
- System thinking and design (written design doc + tradeoffs): 25%
- Collaboration & communication (async updates, clarity in PR description, recorded walkthrough): 20%
- Reliability & operational sense (monitoring, runbook, rollback plan): 15%

Score buckets: 
- 5 = clear exceed expectations for role
- 4 = strong candidate, minor gaps
- 3 = meets expectations
- 2 = some concerning gaps
- 1 = significant concerns

Rubric details (what to look for):
- Execution: small change shipped in Day 1–3 task, passes CI, includes tests and clear commit history. Look for measurable output and ability to triage test failures.
- System design: concise doc (1–2 pages) that enumerates constraints, failure modes, scaling plan, and cost/latency tradeoffs. Prefer diagrams and a simple data flow.
- Collaboration: asynchronous notes each day in a shared doc or PR; uses concise subject lines, clear TODOs; responds to review comments within agreed SLA.
- Reliability: adds monitoring markers (metrics/logs), includes a rollback or mitigation step, and documents operational runbook for the prototype.

Interviewer playbook (role of buddy + reviewers):
- Buddy (peer): daily check-in via 15m async notes, available for up to 1x 30m pair, reviews Day 1 PR and gives actionable feedback.
- Tech reviewer(s): review PRs and design doc, mark scores independently within 48 hours, leave concrete evidence-based notes.
- Hiring lead (often manager): synthesize scores, conduct the Day 12 debrief with candidate.

Logistics and templates (kept in template repo):
- Minimal starter repo with three small tasks (hello-world infra change, add metric, create small API).
- Design doc template (constraints, goals, data flow, failure modes).
- PR checklist template (testing, security notes, changelog, rollback steps).

Onboarding checklist if candidate is hired (Day 0–12 converted to onboarding):
- Day 0: confirm accesses, intro note from manager, hardware/shipping schedule if required.
- Day 1: buddy intro, run local dev environment, merge a tiny commit (hello-world).
- Day 2–3: complete first assignment from bootcamp repo and pair for 30m on Friday.
- Day 4–6: join team board, claim an easy ticket, push a small fix under buddy guidance.
- Day 7–9: shadow oncall rotation readout, own a small rollback drill.
- Day 10–11: lead a short knowledge share (10m) on the bootcamp work and next steps.
- Day 12: manager sync—set 30/60/90 goals and confirm mentorship cadence.

Decision criteria and debrief template (Day 12):
- Pass: aggregate rubric >= 3.5 and no single critical 1/2 in reliability or execution.
- Borderline: 3.0–3.49 aggregate; consider role fit, growth plan, and references.
- No hire: < 3.0 or a single critical failing score in core areas.

Risk triggers and mitigation:
- Candidate non-response for >48h: pause trial, people-ops reach out, extend by up to 2 days if reasonable.
- Security infra issues in PR: gating blocker; team must triage immediately and provide remediation guidance.
- Excessive synchronous time requested (>3 hours total): review with hiring lead; ensure fairness to other teams.

Communication templates (short examples included in repo):
- Welcome note, Day 3 checkpoint prompt, Day 8 status nudges, Day 12 decision email with feedback.

Metrics to track for experiment (owner: people-ops):
- Time to first merged PR for hired candidates (target: <= 5 days).
- Offer acceptance rate after bootcamp (target: >= 70%).
- Hiring manager satisfaction (post-hire survey score).
- Candidate NPS for experience (post-trial survey).

Open questions for iteration:
- Should we standardize tasks per role band (junior vs senior)? Proposed: yes, small/medium/large buckets.
- How to account for candidates who prefer fully remote synchronous pairing? Proposed: offer hybrid path but keep core async expectation.

Appendix: sample Day 1 task (short):
- Goal: make the test service emit a new latency metric, add dashboard panel, and submit a small PR with tests.
- Acceptance: CI green, dashboard shows metric for synthetic traffic, PR description documents the tracing key and rollout impact.

This document is an experiment template. If you use it, copy the folder from the template repo and create a role-specific brief. Feedback and iteration notes should be added to CONFLUENCE-1234.

title: Contractor-to-Perm Early Integration Playbook
preview:
Purpose:
This playbook is a practical, low-friction plan for converting high-performing contractors into permanent employees with a focus on early integration, calibrated interview scoring, and manager-led retention touchpoints. It assumes conversion is driven by performance and business need rather than a simple time-based trigger.

Scope and audience:
- Audience: people-ops, hiring managers, recruiting partners, hiring panel members, and the conversions review committee.
- Scope: contractors in IC and engineering-adjacent roles (SWE, SRE, Applied ML infra, Product/PM), for mid-to-senior levels. Not intended for executive-level conversions (separate approval flow).

Why a different approach?
Contractor conversions are high-leverage hires: these candidates already understand product and culture. This playbook reduces duplication (don’t repeat full external hire loop), focuses interviews on gaps and permanency fit (benefits, long-term career alignment), and creates a deliberate preboarding cadence to lock in commitment.

Conversion decision ladder (high level):
1) Hiring manager nominates contractor with short justification (impact examples, dependencies to product roadmaps).
2) People-ops validates role funding and comp band.
3) Conversion packet assembled (one-pager performance evidence + manager endorsement).
4) Lightweight panel interview focused on permanence fit (max 2 interviews).
5) Offer and preboarding within 7 business days.

Hiring plan & timeline (recommended cadence):
- Day 0: Manager flags candidate and opens conversion packet in ATS (tag: CONTRACTOR_CONVERT).
- Day 1–2: People-ops checks comp & headcount, schedules panel.
- Day 3–5: Panel interviews + rubric scoring.
- Day 6–7: Offer approved, comp calibrated, extended.
- Offer accepted -> Preboarding begins immediately (see checklist).

Panel composition (recommended):
- Hiring manager (required)
- Peer from the same team (required)
- Eng/PM partner who has collaborated with the contractor (optional but preferred)
- People-ops or recruiter (observational role)

Interview scope and timebox:
- Total interviews: 1–2 sessions only
- Each session: 30–45 minutes
- Focus areas: technical gaps, cross-functional alignment, career ambitions, and culture/permanency considerations (benefits, growth path, relocation if relevant).

Rubric (single-sheet, numeric scoring):
Scoring: 1 = Not demonstrated, 2 = Weak, 3 = Adequate, 4 = Strong, 5 = Exceptional
- Role competence (technical/functional): 1–5
- System impact ownership (delivery + unblock history): 1–5
- Cross-functional communication & collaboration: 1–5
- Autonomy & problem framing: 1–5
- Culture & long-term fit (motivation for perm role): 1–5
- Manager recommendation (binary: yes/no; required)
Pass threshold: average >= 3.8 AND manager recommendation == yes. Any single category = 1 triggers an automatic follow-up conversation and may require an extended interview.

Sample interview prompts (calibrated for conversion):
- "Tell us about a cross-team incident where you drove the resolution. What choices did you make, and why?"
- "If this role was permanent tomorrow, what would you change about your current focus in the next quarter?"
- "Describe a technical decision you made that had downstream developer cost. How did you weigh trade-offs?"
- "What does long-term growth look like for you at Redwood? What gaps would you want us to support?"

Compensation & equity guidance (people-ops checklist):
- Reference existing comp band and contractor rate; typically, perm base should be market-adjusted within the band—document expectations in the packet.
- For conversions within 6 months of contract start, consider accelerated equity eligibility (manager + people-ops approval).
- Confirm benefits eligibility date and communicate major differences (paid time off, parental leave, 401k/stock).

Preboarding checklist (immediately after offer accept):
- HR send welcome packet and benefits enrollment link (within 24 hours).
- Manager schedules "Welcome + Role Transition" 30-min call: responsibilities, org chart, reporting line, initial expectations.
- Assign buddy (peer who is already perm) and schedule Day 1 lunch (virtual or in-person).
- Create perm employee account provisioning request (SAML, GitHub, Slack, calendar) flagged "conversion" for expedited access.
- Check contractor tools/entitlements that differ (e.g., MDM, VPN) and schedule any offboarding of contractor billing where applicable.
- Clarify payroll switching timeline and final contractor invoice submission.

First two-week integration sprint (manager playbook):
- Week 0 (Day 1–3): manager 1:1 to confirm top 3 priorities; buddy-led walkthrough of main repos and infra; setup dev environment checklist completion.
- Week 1: shadowing and structured transfers of knowledge (3 short demos); introduce to key partners across PM/infra/SRE; clarity on recurring meetings they’ll be expected to lead/attend.
- Week 2: owner of a small deliverable (bugfix or minor feature) with clear acceptance criteria and a retro at the end of the week.

30/60/90 check-ins and success criteria:
- 30-day: deployed contribution or completed onboarding deliverable; manager rates: technical ramp, collaboration, and systems knowledge (1–5).
- 60-day: independent ownership of a cross-team task and active contribution to planning.
- 90-day: long-term roadmap alignment, mentor pairing confirmed, and full benefits utilization completed.
Retention signals to capture:
- Early acceptance of internal mentorship invitations.
- Willingness to take on roadmap work vs. only tactical tasks.
- Increasing proactive suggestions for product or process improvements.

Edge cases and governance:
- If candidate declines offer: document reason in ATS and schedule "decline review" with hiring manager and recruiter within 5 business days.
- If comp band requires exception: route to people-ops leadership with a 1-paragraph business case and 2 comparables.
- For public-sector/regulatory work: ensure the conversion path includes security clearance/contract constraints review before offer.

Manager quick-reference (one-pager checklist):
- Confirm nomination + open packet in ATS (Day 0)
- Provide at least 3 concrete performance examples in packet
- Participate in panel and complete rubric (Day 3–5)
- Approve offer and preboarding items (Day 6)
- Schedule Day 1/Week 1/Week 2 meetings and assign buddy
- Complete 30/60/90 templates and submit to people-ops

Measurement & telemetry:
- Track conversion success in HR dashboard: conversion-to-accept rate, 90-day retention %, manager satisfaction score, time-to-perm days.
- Run a quarterly calibration: randomly sample 10 conversions, review rubric distributions, and adjust threshold if average drift > 0.3 points.

Open questions & next steps for pilot:
- Pilot scope: limit to up to 15 conversions across eng/product in Q1 2026.
- People-ops to prepare an internal communications template for managers and for contractors converting.
- Recruiting to build an ATS tag/report for CONTRACTOR_CONVERT and add required fields to conversion packet.

Appendix: conversion packet required fields (one-line each)
- Candidate name + contractor start date
- Short justification (3 bullet examples of impact)
- Manager endorsement sentence + proposed title & comp band
- Any procurement/contract constraints
- Recommended panel members and proposed interview dates

Notes to reviewers: this playbook prioritizes speed and low-friction conversion while maintaining calibration. It is intentionally lighter than a full external hiring loop and relies on manager evidence + a short permanence-focused panel to reduce candidate friction. Feedback welcome — especially from engineering managers and recruiting on the rubric wording and pass threshold.
text:
Purpose:
This playbook is a practical, low-friction plan for converting high-performing contractors into permanent employees with a focus on early integration, calibrated interview scoring, and manager-led retention touchpoints. It assumes conversion is driven by performance and business need rather than a simple time-based trigger.

Scope and audience:
- Audience: people-ops, hiring managers, recruiting partners, hiring panel members, and the conversions review committee.
- Scope: contractors in IC and engineering-adjacent roles (SWE, SRE, Applied ML infra, Product/PM), for mid-to-senior levels. Not intended for executive-level conversions (separate approval flow).

Why a different approach?
Contractor conversions are high-leverage hires: these candidates already understand product and culture. This playbook reduces duplication (don’t repeat full external hire loop), focuses interviews on gaps and permanency fit (benefits, long-term career alignment), and creates a deliberate preboarding cadence to lock in commitment.

Conversion decision ladder (high level):
1) Hiring manager nominates contractor with short justification (impact examples, dependencies to product roadmaps).
2) People-ops validates role funding and comp band.
3) Conversion packet assembled (one-pager performance evidence + manager endorsement).
4) Lightweight panel interview focused on permanence fit (max 2 interviews).
5) Offer and preboarding within 7 business days.

Hiring plan & timeline (recommended cadence):
- Day 0: Manager flags candidate and opens conversion packet in ATS (tag: CONTRACTOR_CONVERT).
- Day 1–2: People-ops checks comp & headcount, schedules panel.
- Day 3–5: Panel interviews + rubric scoring.
- Day 6–7: Offer approved, comp calibrated, extended.
- Offer accepted -> Preboarding begins immediately (see checklist).

Panel composition (recommended):
- Hiring manager (required)
- Peer from the same team (required)
- Eng/PM partner who has collaborated with the contractor (optional but preferred)
- People-ops or recruiter (observational role)

Interview scope and timebox:
- Total interviews: 1–2 sessions only
- Each session: 30–45 minutes
- Focus areas: technical gaps, cross-functional alignment, career ambitions, and culture/permanency considerations (benefits, growth path, relocation if relevant).

Rubric (single-sheet, numeric scoring):
Scoring: 1 = Not demonstrated, 2 = Weak, 3 = Adequate, 4 = Strong, 5 = Exceptional
- Role competence (technical/functional): 1–5
- System impact ownership (delivery + unblock history): 1–5
- Cross-functional communication & collaboration: 1–5
- Autonomy & problem framing: 1–5
- Culture & long-term fit (motivation for perm role): 1–5
- Manager recommendation (binary: yes/no; required)
Pass threshold: average >= 3.8 AND manager recommendation == yes. Any single category = 1 triggers an automatic follow-up conversation and may require an extended interview.

Sample interview prompts (calibrated for conversion):
- "Tell us about a cross-team incident where you drove the resolution. What choices did you make, and why?"
- "If this role was permanent tomorrow, what would you change about your current focus in the next quarter?"
- "Describe a technical decision you made that had downstream developer cost. How did you weigh trade-offs?"
- "What does long-term growth look like for you at Redwood? What gaps would you want us to support?"

Compensation & equity guidance (people-ops checklist):
- Reference existing comp band and contractor rate; typically, perm base should be market-adjusted within the band—document expectations in the packet.
- For conversions within 6 months of contract start, consider accelerated equity eligibility (manager + people-ops approval).
- Confirm benefits eligibility date and communicate major differences (paid time off, parental leave, 401k/stock).

Preboarding checklist (immediately after offer accept):
- HR send welcome packet and benefits enrollment link (within 24 hours).
- Manager schedules "Welcome + Role Transition" 30-min call: responsibilities, org chart, reporting line, initial expectations.
- Assign buddy (peer who is already perm) and schedule Day 1 lunch (virtual or in-person).
- Create perm employee account provisioning request (SAML, GitHub, Slack, calendar) flagged "conversion" for expedited access.
- Check contractor tools/entitlements that differ (e.g., MDM, VPN) and schedule any offboarding of contractor billing where applicable.
- Clarify payroll switching timeline and final contractor invoice submission.

First two-week integration sprint (manager playbook):
- Week 0 (Day 1–3): manager 1:1 to confirm top 3 priorities; buddy-led walkthrough of main repos and infra; setup dev environment checklist completion.
- Week 1: shadowing and structured transfers of knowledge (3 short demos); introduce to key partners across PM/infra/SRE; clarity on recurring meetings they’ll be expected to lead/attend.
- Week 2: owner of a small deliverable (bugfix or minor feature) with clear acceptance criteria and a retro at the end of the week.

30/60/90 check-ins and success criteria:
- 30-day: deployed contribution or completed onboarding deliverable; manager rates: technical ramp, collaboration, and systems knowledge (1–5).
- 60-day: independent ownership of a cross-team task and active contribution to planning.
- 90-day: long-term roadmap alignment, mentor pairing confirmed, and full benefits utilization completed.
Retention signals to capture:
- Early acceptance of internal mentorship invitations.
- Willingness to take on roadmap work vs. only tactical tasks.
- Increasing proactive suggestions for product or process improvements.

Edge cases and governance:
- If candidate declines offer: document reason in ATS and schedule "decline review" with hiring manager and recruiter within 5 business days.
- If comp band requires exception: route to people-ops leadership with a 1-paragraph business case and 2 comparables.
- For public-sector/regulatory work: ensure the conversion path includes security clearance/contract constraints review before offer.

Manager quick-reference (one-pager checklist):
- Confirm nomination + open packet in ATS (Day 0)
- Provide at least 3 concrete performance examples in packet
- Participate in panel and complete rubric (Day 3–5)
- Approve offer and preboarding items (Day 6)
- Schedule Day 1/Week 1/Week 2 meetings and assign buddy
- Complete 30/60/90 templates and submit to people-ops

Measurement & telemetry:
- Track conversion success in HR dashboard: conversion-to-accept rate, 90-day retention %, manager satisfaction score, time-to-perm days.
- Run a quarterly calibration: randomly sample 10 conversions, review rubric distributions, and adjust threshold if average drift > 0.3 points.

title: Onboarding Playbook: Day 0–30 Handoff & Early Impact Kit
preview:
Purpose:
This playbook is designed to standardize the first 30 days for new ICs and managers at Redwood Inference. It focuses on fast functional ramp, clear handoffs, and measurable micro-goals so hiring managers and buddies can coordinate without many back-and-forths.

Scope:
Applies to all full-time engineering hires, applied ML hires, and product managers hired into pods. For contractors and interns, use the abbreviated checklist (lower priority for system access and long-term comms).

Quick overview (one-line):
- Day 0: access, welcome, intro to pod and buddy assignment.
- Day 1–7: orientation sprints, 2 small measurable tasks, key readings and infra walkthrough.
- Day 8–30: stakeholder meetings, first PR, first oncall shadow if relevant, first 1:1s established.

Hiring rubric (to attach to offer & interview packet):
We use a weighted rubric to make offers consistent across hiring pairs. Scores 1–5 for each dimension, multiply by weight, total out of 50.
Weighted competencies:
- Core technical ability (code/architecture) — weight 3
- Problem decomposition & communicate tradeoffs — weight 2
- Product sense / user impact awareness — weight 1.5
- Ownership & reliability (evidence of delivery) — weight 2
- Collaboration & cross-team communication — weight 1.5
- Role-specific depth (ML, infra, SRE) — weight 2
Calibration guidance for interviewers:
- 4+: strong evidence in examples, concrete impact numbers.
- 3: competent, some evidence, small gaps.
- 2 or less: concerns, needs growth plan.
Minimum hire threshold: combined weighted score >= 33, with no dimension below 2 (unless hiring manager documents a targeted growth plan).

Interview flow (recommended for IC mid-senior):
1) Recruiter screen (30m): role fit, comp expectations, relocation/remote.
2) Technical take-home (optional) or paired live problem (60m).
3) System-design / architecture (60m) — include ops/scale questions for infra-facing roles.
4) Leadership / collaboration sync (45m) — cross-functional scenarios.
5) Hiring manager final (30m).

Scorecard template (copyable):
- Candidate name: 
- Role applied: 
- Interview date: 
- Interviewer: 
- Scores: Core tech [1-5], Decomp [1-5], Ownership [1-5], Collaboration [1-5], Role depth [1-5]
- Notes: examples, missing signals, recommended level and adjustments
- Decision: yes/maybe/no

Offer checklist (for people ops + hiring manager):
- Compensation band confirmation (HR + hiring manager sign-off)
- Manager acceptance of relocation/time-zone implications
- Draft onboarding plan (first 30 days milestones) attached to offer letter
- Slack + email invite sequence pre-start (welcome note + first-week schedule)

Day 0: immediate access and welcome (owner: People Ops + IT)
- Accounts created: Google, Slack, Redwood Console access, HR portal, code host (GitHub/GitLab).
- Laptop shipped or confirmed pickup with imaging notes (if remote, include hardware checklist).
- Add to pod mailing lists, calendar groups, and oncall schedule (if role requires).
- Welcome message template: manager introduces new hire + buddy in #<team>-welcome.

Day 1–7: orientation sprint (owner: manager + buddy)
- Day 1 morning: 30m manager welcome, 30m buddy tour, 60m required security & compliance readout (recorded).
- Access verification checklist: can clone the repo, run dev server, access CI dashboards, read monitoring runbooks.
- Micro-goal A: Complete and merge a one-hour labeled doc/README improvement PR.
- Micro-goal B: Ship a small infra-safe code change (test-only or docs) with CI passing.
- Required readings: product one-pager, infra runbook, service-level targets for pod models.
- Admin tasks: benefits enrollment, tax forms, calendar invites for recurring 1:1s.

Day 8–14: integration sprint
- Stakeholder 30m intro calls: product PM, SRE lead, analytics contact.
- Shadow rotation: 2-day rotation across codebases/modules most relevant to role.
- Pair-programing session: 90m with primary buddy on a live ticket.
- First retrospective entry: new hire writes 1-page 'first impressions & 30d goals' and shares with manager.

Day 15–30: first-deliverable sprint
- Deliverable: first substantive PR (feature, infra change, or eval harness) merged behind feature flag if applicable.
- Oncall shadow (if applicable): observe pager runbook and escalation path.
- 30-day review: manager completes a short assessment (ramp, blockers, 30d goals status).
- Roadmap sync: new hire presents a 5–7 min summary of what they will own in the next 90 days.

Buddy rotation matrix (example):
- Buddy week 0–1: Codebase tour + dev setup owner (buddy A)
- Buddy week 1–3: Product & experiment lifecycle orientation (buddy B)
- Buddy ongoing: Engineering culture, pull request norms (buddy C)
Notes: buddies should be different people to broaden social graphs and reduce single-person bottlenecks. Limit number to 3 for clarity.

Manager checklist (first 30 days):
- Prepare technical onboarding tickets that are self-contained, reviewable, and small (1–3 day scope).
- Schedule required stakeholder intros in week 1 and 2.
- Confirm training sessions (security, compliance, infra) and ensure recordings are available.
- Set explicit 30-day success criteria with measurable signals (first PR merged, test coverage baseline, monitoring alerts understood).

Remote-specific considerations:
- Ship a physical welcome pack where possible (company swag, quick reference printed).
- Schedule overlapping time blocks for core collaboration hours.
- Encourage async updates: daily 15–30m standup note in thread for first two weeks.

Reboarding / backfill quick-start (when hiring into a staffed pod):
- Check for legacy config that the outgoing IC owned (credentials, infra shortcuts) and add as onboarding tickets.
- Include a brief 'technical debt map' doc to orient the new hire to near-term priorities.

Calibration & mini-audit (recommended at hire + 30-day):
- People Ops runs a sample audit of 10% of scorecards to measure hiring consistency quarterly.
- Managers provide closed-loop feedback to interviewers on candidate ramp signals at 30 days.

Open questions and decisions still needed:
- Standardization of take-home test length across all engineering levels (proposed 2–4 hours max).
- Formalizing compensation approval SLAs for international hires.
- A small template for manager 30-day assessment (to be added as a linked Confluence page).

Appendix: quick templates (copy + paste)
- Welcome Slack message: "Please welcome <Name>, our new <Role> joining <pod>. They'll be pairing with <Buddy> this week — please say hi and share any urgent tips!"
- 30-day goals template: "Goal 1: Merge X; Goal 2: Shadow oncall; Goal 3: Present 90-day plan"

Notes to reviewer:
This doc intentionally mixes crisp checklists with suggested micro-goals rather than long checklists that managers ignore. Feedback requested on: minimum hire threshold score, whether to require take-homes for senior roles, and an approval SLA for laptop provisioning for remote hires.

Revision log:
- 2025-11-02: initial draft by Ava Chen
- 2025-11-06: added hiring rubric weights (Maya Patel)
- 2025-11-10: clarified buddy rotation and remote guidance (Liam O'Connor)

title: Mentor escape-hatch + Day 0-30 triage checklist for hybrid tech hires
preview:
Purpose:
- Short working doc for people-ops + hiring managers to run a low-friction Day 0 to Day 30 intake, pair a mentor, and define an 'escape-hatch' protocol if early signals suggest mismatch. Meant to be pragmatic: checkboxes, decision triggers, notes-to-self.

Context and why this exists:
- We hire a mix of hosted-api infra, serving-runtime, and product infra engineers. Hybrid (remote + office) hires often need a clearer early safety net than the default 1:1 schedule.
- Goal: reduce churn from poor mentor matches, catch ramp blockers early, and give managers an operational playbook for the first month.

Role summary (to paste into req):
- Role title: Senior Inference Engineer (hybrid)
- Core outcome 0-90d: Deliver one production rollout of a quantization profile that reduces per-token cost by >=10% without >1% quality regression.

Hiring plan highlights (for coordination with hiring manager):
- Interview loop: phone screen (talent), technical take-home (4–6 hour), onsite loop: system design + kernel optimization + culture/collaboration + manager round.
- Scorecard: Must-have: systems + GPU inference intuition. Nice-to-have: distributed autoscaling experience, infra for private deployments.
- Interview rubric summary (shorthand):
  - Systems design (30%): graded 1-4 on tradeoffs, deployment plan, and failure modes
  - Kernel/optimization depth (30%): graded 1-4 on vectorization/quantization thinking and experimental approach
  - Collaboration/pass-the-baton (20%): graded 1-4 on docs, PR hygiene, mentoring experience
  - Cultural fit & mission alignment (20%): graded 1-4, include specific examples of product thinking

Early onboarding checklist (Day 0 to Day 30) - items are checkboxes for manager/mentor/people-ops:
- Pre-Day 0 (people-ops): account + laptop shipped, KMS onboarding scheduled, initial swag + welcome doc sent
- Day 0 (manager): welcome call (30m), 90-day outcomes reviewed (15m), immediate teammate introductions (30m)
- Day 1-3 (mentor): environment setup pairing (2h/day), run a first small PR: 'hello infra' (logging added + smoke test)
- Week 1 (manager + mentor): prioritize a low-risk production-facing ticket the new hire can own; assign doc owner for rollout checklist
- Week 2: review first PRs, schedule 1:1 weekly cadence, run a fast experiment to exercise local perf profile (baseline numbers captured)
- Week 3: milestone check: has new hire shipped a non-trivial change? capture blockers in the triage table below
- Day 30: 30-day review with manager, mentor present (15m), signals and go/no-go decisions recorded

Triage table (use this for Day 7/14/21 quick captures) - short notes, do not over-index on prose:
- Signal kinds to capture: onboarding tickets open > 5, PRs blocked > 2, failing CI > 3 due to infra unfamiliarity, lack of access to required systems > 1, escalation to SRE or security for missing perms
- Fast remediation options: extend pairing time with mentor, swap in a second mentor for a 1-week shadow, reduce scope to a clearly delimited ticket, move to observation phase (no owner on high-risk tasks)

Mentor pairing protocol (practical rules):
- Mentor selection: ideally in the same subdomain, not the direct manager; 1:1 mentor capacity capped at 4 active mentees
- Commitment: 3x 90-min sessions in first 2 weeks + 3x 30-min async check-ins in next 2 weeks
- Replace mentor (escape-hatch): if mentee reports low signal after 14 days or manager reports repeated blocker, trigger swap. People-ops owns swap logistics and an optional 2-week 'float' mentor assigned from bench.

Escape-hatch decision rubric (manager + people-ops):
- Trigger if ANY of the following immutable rules occur by day 21:
  - >60% of onboarding tickets unresolved and no clear owner assigned
  - Repeated interpersonal friction flagged in 1:1s with concrete examples
  - Technical ramp is off-track: candidate cannot complete baseline sandbox tasks after paired sessions and remediation attempts
- Options on trigger: additional mentor + narrowed scope, formal performance improvement plan (very rare; coordinate with legal), or role pivot (switch to internal project with less customer risk)

Communication templates (quick snippets to use in Slack/email):
- To mentor pool: 'Hi — new hire X starts Day 0 in team Y. Please RSVP if you can do a 90-min pairing session this week. Tagged skills: kernel opt, quantization.'
- To candidate at Day 7: 'Quick check-in: how is access and pairing going? Any blockers I should escalate?'

Notes-to-self / unresolved questions (working):
- Should we standardize a 2-week mentor shadow (where mentor owns PR reviews) for hires without prior infra experience? pros/cons list below.
- Need to align with SRE on a fast-track perms flow: currently takes 3–5 days for HSM access — too slow for Day 1 experiments.
- Investigate creating a 1-page 'first PR template' for infra changes to reduce review friction.

Action items (owners):
- People-ops: draft mentor swap SOP and share with hiring managers (due 2025-11-14)
- Manager (Samir): create 0-30 outcome tracker in shared drive and assign first low-risk ticket (due 2025-11-07)
- Mentor lead (Lina): run a 45-min mentor onboarding clinic for mentors next week

Quick retrospective prompts for Day 30 review:
- What went well about pairing/setup?
- What were persistent blockers?
- Was mentor the right level of seniority/overlap?
- Quick wins for improving the process for the next hire

Sprinty, sketchy, intentionally practical - use and iterate.

Metadata notes: keep this doc as draft until the mentor swap SOP is approved. We'll link the final SOP into linked_artifacts after review.
text:
Purpose:
- Short working doc for people-ops + hiring managers to run a low-friction Day 0 to Day 30 intake, pair a mentor, and define an 'escape-hatch' protocol if early signals suggest mismatch. Meant to be pragmatic: checkboxes, decision triggers, notes-to-self.

Context and why this exists:
- We hire a mix of hosted-api infra, serving-runtime, and product infra engineers. Hybrid (remote + office) hires often need a clearer early safety net than the default 1:1 schedule.
- Goal: reduce churn from poor mentor matches, catch ramp blockers early, and give managers an operational playbook for the first month.

Role summary (to paste into req):
- Role title: Senior Inference Engineer (hybrid)
- Core outcome 0-90d: Deliver one production rollout of a quantization profile that reduces per-token cost by >=10% without >1% quality regression.

Hiring plan highlights (for coordination with hiring manager):
- Interview loop: phone screen (talent), technical take-home (4–6 hour), onsite loop: system design + kernel optimization + culture/collaboration + manager round.
- Scorecard: Must-have: systems + GPU inference intuition. Nice-to-have: distributed autoscaling experience, infra for private deployments.
- Interview rubric summary (shorthand):
  - Systems design (30%): graded 1-4 on tradeoffs, deployment plan, and failure modes
  - Kernel/optimization depth (30%): graded 1-4 on vectorization/quantization thinking and experimental approach
  - Collaboration/pass-the-baton (20%): graded 1-4 on docs, PR hygiene, mentoring experience
  - Cultural fit & mission alignment (20%): graded 1-4, include specific examples of product thinking

Early onboarding checklist (Day 0 to Day 30) - items are checkboxes for manager/mentor/people-ops:
- Pre-Day 0 (people-ops): account + laptop shipped, KMS onboarding scheduled, initial swag + welcome doc sent
- Day 0 (manager): welcome call (30m), 90-day outcomes reviewed (15m), immediate teammate introductions (30m)
- Day 1-3 (mentor): environment setup pairing (2h/day), run a first small PR: 'hello infra' (logging added + smoke test)
- Week 1 (manager + mentor): prioritize a low-risk production-facing ticket the new hire can own; assign doc owner for rollout checklist
- Week 2: review first PRs, schedule 1:1 weekly cadence, run a fast experiment to exercise local perf profile (baseline numbers captured)
- Week 3: milestone check: has new hire shipped a non-trivial change? capture blockers in the triage table below
- Day 30: 30-day review with manager, mentor present (15m), signals and go/no-go decisions recorded

Triage table (use this for Day 7/14/21 quick captures) - short notes, do not over-index on prose:
- Signal kinds to capture: onboarding tickets open > 5, PRs blocked > 2, failing CI > 3 due to infra unfamiliarity, lack of access to required systems > 1, escalation to SRE or security for missing perms
- Fast remediation options: extend pairing time with mentor, swap in a second mentor for a 1-week shadow, reduce scope to a clearly delimited ticket, move to observation phase (no owner on high-risk tasks)

Mentor pairing protocol (practical rules):
- Mentor selection: ideally in the same subdomain, not the direct manager; 1:1 mentor capacity capped at 4 active mentees
- Commitment: 3x 90-min sessions in first 2 weeks + 3x 30-min async check-ins in next 2 weeks
- Replace mentor (escape-hatch): if mentee reports low signal after 14 days or manager reports repeated blocker, trigger swap. People-ops owns swap logistics and an optional 2-week 'float' mentor assigned from bench.

Escape-hatch decision rubric (manager + people-ops):
- Trigger if ANY of the following immutable rules occur by day 21:
  - >60% of onboarding tickets unresolved and no clear owner assigned
  - Repeated interpersonal friction flagged in 1:1s with concrete examples
  - Technical ramp is off-track: candidate cannot complete baseline sandbox tasks after paired sessions and remediation attempts
- Options on trigger: additional mentor + narrowed scope, formal performance improvement plan (very rare; coordinate with legal), or role pivot (switch to internal project with less customer risk)

Communication templates (quick snippets to use in Slack/email):
- To mentor pool: 'Hi — new hire X starts Day 0 in team Y. Please RSVP if you can do a 90-min pairing session this week. Tagged skills: kernel opt, quantization.'
- To candidate at Day 7: 'Quick check-in: how is access and pairing going? Any blockers I should escalate?'

Notes-to-self / unresolved questions (working):
- Should we standardize a 2-week mentor shadow (where mentor owns PR reviews) for hires without prior infra experience? pros/cons list below.
- Need to align with SRE on a fast-track perms flow: currently takes 3–5 days for HSM access — too slow for Day 1 experiments.
- Investigate creating a 1-page 'first PR template' for infra changes to reduce review friction.

Action items (owners):
- People-ops: draft mentor swap SOP and share with hiring managers (due 2025-11-14)
- Manager (Samir): create 0-30 outcome tracker in shared drive and assign first low-risk ticket (due 2025-11-07)
- Mentor lead (Lina): run a 45-min mentor onboarding clinic for mentors next week

Quick retrospective prompts for Day 30 review:
- What went well about pairing/setup?
- What were persistent blockers?
- Was mentor the right level of seniority/overlap?
- Quick wins for improving the process for the next hire

Sprinty, sketchy, intentionally practical - use and iterate.

---
