title: Q3 focus threads and delegate roster — all-hands prep v0.1
preview:
Purpose:

Quick working doc for the exec + all-hands narrative and the delegate roster for Q3 planning. We need a compact slate for the town, a clear set of draft OKRs that map to product and infra asks, and a lightweight owner matrix so follow-ups happen after the meeting. This version is intentionally rough — use it to iterate before the exec sync (Weds) and the all-hands (Fri).

Context / constraints:
- Company steering: keep two high-impact cross-company initiatives (Performance Economics and Private Deployments ramp) and 2 enabling bets (Console polish for rollouts, Optimize insights).
- Budget posture: heads and infra spend mostly flat; aggressive efficiency asks needed to avoid mid-quarter rebaseline.
- Timing: engineering release windows in late July — any OKR that requires model infra changes must be scoped to a single-sprint uplift or flagged as multi-quarter.

Outcome we want from event:
- Exec alignment on top-3 tactical priorities for Q3 (commit and signal to team).
- Public OKR draft for the company to react to within 48h after all-hands (comment period).
- A delegate roster with named owners and 2-week check-ins for early risk discovery.

Draft top-level OKRs (candidate list — short, measurable, owner):
1) OKR: Reduce hosted API 95th percentile latency by 20% on common tails
   - Why: customer SLAs and conversion on trial workloads
   - Success metric: 95p latency improves from 820ms -> <=660ms on p95 for gen endpoints (tracked per-model)
   - Owner: Marcus Rivera
   - Key results (suggested):
     - KR1: Kernel tuning + new batching policy deployed to 50% of traffic without SLO regressions
     - KR2: Prefix/KV cache hit rate +15% on inference corpus set
     - KR3: Completed observability hook for per-route token-level trace (console)

2) OKR: Lower per-token inference cost for mid-sized workloads by 15%
   - Why: unit economics for scale customers and Dedicated renewals
   - Owner: Priya Kapoor
   - KRs:
     - KR1: Shipping Optimize recommendations for batching/quantization for top-10 customers
     - KR2: Launch the 'burst pricing' experiment to validate 2% uplift in reserved utilization
     - KR3: Reduce model spin-up time median from 12s -> 7s for cold starts

3) OKR: Private deployments — baseline security and onboarding for 3 pilots
   - Why: Enterprise pipeline and revenue motion
   - Owner: Diego Morales
   - KRs:
     - KR1: 3 pilot customers onboarded with private control plane + audit logs
     - KR2: KMS/HSM integration validated against compliance checklist
     - KR3: Private health-check automation and rollback policy documented in Console

Enablers / company-level guardrails (non-OKR but must be tracked):
- Console rollout flow: reduce time-to-pin model <-> route by 40% (Product/Console team lead Lina Park).
- Reliability SLO cost tradeoff playbook: ops + SRE to publish a clear escalation and fallback policy for model fallbacks.
- Hiring/time: freeze discretionary hiring for cloud infra until Q3 midpoint unless critical (exec decision flag).

All-hands 40-minute micro-slate (Fri):
- 0:00–0:03 Opening (CEO quick frame on why these lanes) — Ava Chen
- 0:03–0:10 Exec snapshot: three priorities + one-liner impact statements — Marcus, Priya, Diego (1–2 mins each)
- 0:10–0:18 Concrete examples (2 short customer vignettes: Hosted perf improvement -> conversion; Private pilot -> compliance win) — Solutions Eng
- 0:18–0:28 Roadmap pointers and demo of Console quickflow (Lina Park) — 6–8 minute demo, 2 mins Q
- 0:28–0:34 Risks & tradeoffs: cost posture and contingency playbook — SRE lead
- 0:34–0:37 Ask to org: 48-hour comment window + how to propose changes to OKRs (PR/issue template) — Company Ops
- 0:37–0:40 Rapid close + call for volunteers for pilot squads

Communication and follow-up cadence:
- Day 0 (all-hands): Publish slides + 1-page with the 3 OKR drafts and delegate roster.
- Day 2: Comment period closes — compile feedback and push updated doc.
- Week 1: Exec sign-off and publish final company OKRs.
- Weeks 1–6: Biweekly delegate check-ins; 2-page status note captured each check-in in the shared roadmap page.

Delegate roster (working):
- Priority 1 (Latency): Marcus Rivera — engineering lead, + SRE liaison (Samir Patel), data lead (Ana Gomez)
- Priority 2 (Cost): Priya Kapoor — product + finance observer (Tao Lin), solutions-eng (Diego Morales)
- Priority 3 (Private): Diego Morales — security lead (Kavita Shah), customer-success owner (Maya Singh)
- Console polish: Lina Park — UX + docs (Riley Chen)
- Communication owner (post-hall): Ava Chen + Growth (Noah Feldman)

Open questions / TODOs before exec sync:
- Exec ask: confirm hiring freeze exception process (who signs?) — action: Legal/Finance to propose wording (due Tue)
- Dataset for the p95 latency claim: need canonical synthetic + production slices. Action: Marcus to drop dataset link and baseline numbers by Tue EOD.
- Customer vignettes: Solutions Eng to confirm permission on names/metrics — action: Maya (CS)
- Demo readiness: Lina to give a dry-run Wednesday (schedule invite)

Risks & mitigations (brief):
- Risk: Kernel tuning introduces a subtle regression on long-context flows. Mitigation: canary + automated regressions on eval set and auto-fallback to previous kernel.
- Risk: Private pilot timeline slips because of network provisioning. Mitigation: pre-approved checklist and a small 'blueprint' turnkey script for pilot infra.
- Risk: Team confusion if OKRs change post-all-hands. Mitigation: strict 48-hour comment window and single exec owner to approve amendments.

Decisions needed at exec sync (Weds):
- Approve top-3 priority slate as company signal (Y/N)
- Approve delegate roster and 2-week check-in cadence (Y/N)
- Approve hiring freeze exceptions process (Y/N)

Notes to self / rough bits: 
- Keep the all-hands short and ceremonial; actual operational work happens in follow-up squads.
- Consider small symbolic recognition for squads that hit early wins in Q3 (PR-friendly).

Attachments / links to draft materials (working):
- Slides: /drive/company/presentations/q3-micro-slate-v0.1 (placeholder)
- Baseline telemetry: /drive/eng/telemetry/q2-latency-baseline.csv (placeholder)
- Draft OKR doc (this file will be imported to confluence after sign-off)

If you are an owner listed above: add your 3-line plan in comments below and availability for a 30-min sync before Thurs EOD.

-- Ava

(edited: added private pilot owner + tightened dates 2025-11-07)

title: Role bridging playbook — onboarding + backfill interview plan
preview:
Purpose:
This is a pragmatic, low-friction playbook for bridging a departing or rotating role and getting a backfill hired and productive quickly. Mix of hiring plan + interview rubric + a day-by-day onboarding continuity checklist. For use by hiring managers, people ops partners, and hiring panels.

Scope:
- Individual ICs and managers transitioning roles inside or out of the company.
- Applicable to technical and non-technical roles, but examples skew to engineering/Product/solutions roles.
- Not a formal policy; complement to Confluence role inventory and payroll approvals.

Quick summary (TL;DR):
- 2-week immediate continuity runbook (covering essential tickets, oncall, calendar handoffs).
- 4-week hiring plan to source and interview backfill (PLG-friendly funnel: phone screen -> tech work sample -> final panel).
- Interview rubric with 5 axes, scoring 1-4, and example questions per axis.
- 30/60/90 onboarding checklist for first hire week-by-week with measurable acceptance criteria.

Before you start (note to hiring manager):
- Confirm role need: vacancy vs temporary coverage vs internal rotation. If internal rotation, prioritize internal candidates first (add transfer buffer).
- Capture the 5 things only this person does today. Ask them and their direct partner to write them down in the last 48 hours before the role opens.
- Create a small \"must-know\" handoff doc (<= 1 page) and attach to the ticket.

Immediate continuity checklist (first 48–72 hours):
- Identify single-day backup(s) for calendar invites marked \"owner\".
- Export critical/active issues (Jira filter) with owner tags; add a \"temporary-owner\" label and a 72-hr triage owner.
- Hand off oncall/runbook steps and provide access to logs/keys.
- Slack status message: set fallback contact for team channels and highlight top 3 urgent items.
- Quick 30-min sync between outgoing person and hiring manager to walk through high-impact queues. Record and save to Confluence.

Knowledge capture template (to send to outgoing person):
- 1-line role summary: \"I am responsible for...\"
- 3 success metrics this quarter (quantified if possible)
- Top 5 recurring tickets/alerts and why they matter
- Top 3 stakeholders and their expectations
- Two friction points others should know about
- Access/keys/Credentials inventory (owner note only)
- Good-to-have references (dashboards, playbooks, PRs)

Hiring plan (4-week rapid funnel):
Week 0 (launch): job spec + hiring committee + JD posting internal first
- Draft short JD (3 bullets for mission + 5 required competencies + 2 nice-to-haves).
- Post internal, encourage referrals, open to external after 72 hrs.
- Create Jira req JIRA-XXXX and link to role map.

Week 1 (sourcing + prescreens):
- Screen 8–12 candidates to land 4 phone screens. Use 30-min phone screen script.
- Phone screen: culture fit (10m), technical baseline (10m), logistics/comp (5m).
- Scoreheet captured in Greenhouse/ATS with rubric.

Week 2 (technical loop):
- 2-hr loop with a take-home or short live pairing (choose one):
  - Option A: 3-hour take-home exercise with clear acceptance criteria + 24-48 hr turnaround.
  - Option B: 45-min pairing challenge using a real small task from the team's backlog (not proprietary).
- Interviewers: future manager, peer IC, cross-functional partner.

Week 3 (final + offer):
- Final behavioral + role-fit panel (45–60m).
- Reference checks (2) + comp sync.
- Offer and ramp date agreement.

Interview rubric (single page, canonical):
Scoring: 1 = no evidence, 2 = basic, 3 = strong, 4 = exceptional.
Axes (5):
- Problem solving & systems thinking (sample prompts: \"Walk me through a recent system you designed for latency or cost tradeoffs; what were the tradeoffs?\")
- Execution / ownership (sample prompts: \"Tell me about a time you owned an ambiguous cross-team project; what did you ship?\")
- Technical depth or craft (sample prompts for eng: \"Explain a kernel or optimization you implemented; why was it better?\")
- Collaboration & communication (sample prompts: \"How do you handle conflict with a stakeholder who has different priorities?\")
- Redwoods-aligned product sense (sample prompts: \"Given Redwood's mission, what would you prioritize for the first 90 days in this role?\")

Panel guidance:
- Use behavioral STAR format. One question per axis; one follow-up probe.
- Capture a 1-sentence decision rationale after the interview.
- Avoid whiteboard puzzles; prefer live debugging or design at a whiteboard but focused on product tradeoffs.

Sample phone screen script (30m):
- 0–3m: intro, role elevator
- 3–13m: career arc + concrete impact (what they've shipped)
- 13–23m: technical baseline or case question (one small system tradeoff)
- 23–28m: culture or matching expectations (ambiguity, remote, growth)
- 28–30m: logistical Qs + next steps

Take-home tech exercise guidance (if used):
- Keep to 3–4 hours of candidate time. Realistic but scaffolded.
- Provide sample input + acceptance criteria and at least one failing test.
- Grade on correctness + design + README clarity. Provide rubric to reviewers.

Offer / comp tips:
- Present base + clear equity philosophy and expected short-term growth. 
- For internal transfers, prioritize parity with existing band + note retention bonus if critical.

30/60/90 onboarding checklist (hiring manager + mentor):
Day 0–7 (week 1):
- Account setup checklist (okta, email, repo access, cloud consoles) - baseline complete by EOD day 1.
- First-week goals: read 3 docs (product overview, infra runbook, current sprint board) + 2 shadow sessions.
- 1:1 schedule: weekly for first 90 days with explicit objectives.
- Acceptance criteria week 1: can run local stack or reproduce one production read-only query; submitted first small PR (non-blocking).

Days 8–30 (month 1):
- Pair with 2 teammates to own an improvement ticket; clear owner-by-date.
- Deliver a short write-up of problem context and suggested next steps.
- Acceptance criteria month 1: merged PR with tests or documented handoff of operational responsibility.

Days 31–60 (month 2):
- Lead a small feature or major bugfix cycle; own rollout checklist.
- Mentor feedback checkpoint and revision of 60-day plan.
- Acceptance criteria month 2: can independently triage critical alerts and complete a small rollout.

Days 61–90 (month 3):
- Drive 2 cross-functional syncs and present first impact metrics.
- Transition to normal 1:1 cadence and career goals discussion.
- Acceptance criteria month 3: measurable impact tied to original success metrics; documented knowledge artifacts.

Hiring panel calibration checklist (before final round):
- Read candidate scoreheets for previous rounds (manager notes + phone screen).
- Confirm interviews used consistent scoring axes.
- Agree on must-have vs nice-to-have checklist.

Success metrics (how we know this playbook worked):
- Time-to-first-responsibility: <21 days (new hire has first merged PR or operational signoff).
- Time-to-full-coverage: <90 days (new hire owns primary responsibilities).
- Candidate quality: panel average >= 3.0 across 5 axes.
- Internal transfer satisfaction: manager + transferee net-promoter >= 7 at 60 days.

Risk log (quick):
- Risk: critical oncall knowledge not captured. Mitigation: pair shadow for 3 rotations before handoff.
- Risk: candidate ghosting late. Mitigation: pre-emptive backup shortlist of 2 internal referrals.

Notes to people ops partner (action items):
- Confirm scorecard template in ATS matches this rubric.
- Schedule hiring panel at time zones that work for key stakeholders; default to async feedback if needed.
- Flag comp leveling questions early.

Unresolved questions / TODOs:
- Create a short repo of 2–3 canonical pairing tasks the eng teams can reuse (owner: Solutions Eng).
- Decide policy for take-home exercises vs live pairing across different bands.

Appendix: quick example interview prompts (copyable)
- \"Describe an engineering tradeoff you made to lower latency by 2x. What did you measure, what did you change, what didn't you do?\"
- \"Give a concrete example of a cross-org conflict you resolved. How did you prioritize stakeholders?\"
- \"Here's a small JSON document representing a prompt distribution; how would you instrument and monitor model latency over time?\"

Notes to self (informal):
- Keep this short and iterated. Drive docs should be the fast-moving copy; authoritative policy in Confluence.
- This doc frequently referenced by hiring managers during internal rotation bursts — keep a short checklist pinned in Slack #people-ops.

End.

- Scoreheet captured in Greenhouse/ATS with rubric.

Week 2 (technical loop):
- 2-hr loop with a take-home or short live pairing (choose one):
  - Option A: 3-hour take-home exercise with clear acceptance criteria + 24-48 hr turnaround.
  - Option B: 45-min pairing challenge using a real small task from the team's backlog (not proprietary).
- Interviewers: future manager, peer IC, cross-functional partner.

Week 3 (final + offer):
- Final behavioral + role-fit panel (45–60m).
- Reference checks (2) + comp sync.
- Offer and ramp date agreement.

Interview rubric (single page, canonical):
Scoring: 1 = no evidence, 2 = basic, 3 = strong, 4 = exceptional.
Axes (5):
- Problem solving & systems thinking (sample prompts: \"Walk me through a recent system you designed for latency or cost tradeoffs; what were the tradeoffs?\")
- Execution / ownership (sample prompts: \"Tell me about a time you owned an ambiguous cross-team project; what did you ship?\")
- Technical depth or craft (sample prompts for eng: \"Explain a kernel or optimization you implemented; why was it better?\")
- Collaboration & communication (sample prompts: \"How do you handle conflict with a stakeholder who has different priorities?\")
- Redwoods-aligned product sense (sample prompts: \"Given Redwood's mission, what would you prioritize for the first 90 days in this role?\")

Panel guidance:
- Use behavioral STAR format. One question per axis; one follow-up probe.
- Capture a 1-sentence decision rationale after the interview.
- Avoid whiteboard puzzles; prefer live debugging or design at a whiteboard but focused on product tradeoffs.

Sample phone screen script (30m):
- 0–3m: intro, role elevator
- 3–13m: career arc + concrete impact (what they've shipped)
- 13–23m: technical baseline or case question (one small system tradeoff)
- 23–28m: culture or matching expectations (ambiguity, remote, growth)
- 28–30m: logistical Qs + next steps

Take-home tech exercise guidance (if used):
- Keep to 3–4 hours of candidate time. Realistic but scaffolded.
- Provide sample input + acceptance criteria and at least one failing test.
- Grade on correctness + design + README clarity. Provide rubric to reviewers.

Offer / comp tips:
- Present base + clear equity philosophy and expected short-term growth. 
- For internal transfers, prioritize parity with existing band + note retention bonus if critical.

30/60/90 onboarding checklist (hiring manager + mentor):
Day 0–7 (week 1):
- Account setup checklist (okta, email, repo access, cloud consoles) - baseline complete by EOD day 1.
- First-week goals: read 3 docs (product overview, infra runbook, current sprint board) + 2 shadow sessions.
- 1:1 schedule: weekly for first 90 days with explicit objectives.
- Acceptance criteria week 1: can run local stack or reproduce one production read-only query; submitted first small PR (non-blocking).

Days 8–30 (month 1):
- Pair with 2 teammates to own an improvement ticket; clear owner-by-date.
- Deliver a short write-up of problem context and suggested next steps.
- Acceptance criteria month 1: merged PR with tests or documented handoff of operational responsibility.

Days 31–60 (month 2):
- Lead a small feature or major bugfix cycle; own rollout checklist.
- Mentor feedback checkpoint and revision of 60-day plan.
- Acceptance criteria month 2: can independently triage critical alerts and complete a small rollout.

Days 61–90 (month 3):
- Drive 2 cross-functional syncs and present first impact metrics.
- Transition to normal 1:1 cadence and career goals discussion.
- Acceptance criteria month 3: measurable impact tied to original success metrics; documented knowledge artifacts.

Hiring panel calibration checklist (before final round):
- Read candidate scoreheets for previous rounds (manager notes + phone screen).
- Confirm interviews used consistent scoring axes.
- Agree on must-have vs nice-to-have checklist.

Success metrics (how we know this playbook worked):
- Time-to-first-responsibility: <21 days (new hire has first merged PR or operational signoff).
- Time-to-full-coverage: <90 days (new hire owns primary responsibilities).
- Candidate quality: panel average >= 3.0 across 5 axes.
- Internal transfer satisfaction: manager + transferee net-promoter >= 7 at 60 days.

Risk log (quick):
- Risk: critical oncall knowledge not captured. Mitigation: pair shadow for 3 rotations before handoff.
- Risk: candidate ghosting late. Mitigation: pre-emptive backup shortlist of 2 internal referrals.

Notes to people ops partner (action items):
- Confirm scorecard template in ATS matches this rubric.
- Schedule hiring panel at time zones that work for key stakeholders; default to async feedback if needed.
- Flag comp leveling questions early.

Unresolved questions / TODOs:
- Create a short repo of 2–3 canonical pairing tasks the eng teams can reuse (owner: Solutions Eng).
- Decide policy for take-home exercises vs live pairing across different bands.

Appendix: quick example interview prompts (copyable)
- \"Describe an engineering tradeoff you made to lower latency by 2x. What did you measure, what did you change, what didn't you do?\"
- \"Give a concrete example of a cross-org conflict you resolved. How did you prioritize stakeholders?\"
- \"Here's a small JSON document representing a prompt distribution; how would you instrument and monitor model latency over time?\"

Notes to self (informal):
- Keep this short and iterated. Drive docs should be the fast-moving copy; authoritative policy in Confluence.
- This doc frequently referenced by hiring managers during internal rotation bursts — keep a short checklist pinned in Slack #people-ops.

End.

title: Sprint signal brief and exec intake (WIP)
preview:
Purpose & framing:
This doc is a compact working brief for the leadership intake and the cross-company workshop we will run the week before the all‑hands. Goal: converge on a 6–8 item slate of company priorities + 3 clear exec asks and draft OKRs to seed team planning. This is intentionally lightweight: we want debates surfaced, not polished prose.

Context reminders (one-liners):
- Market: adoption of dedicated/private inference accelerating among enterprise customers. Cost/perf narrative is our sales lever.
- Product bets: finish Redwood Optimize pilot (cost tooling), stabilize a Dedicated autoscaling SLO, and finalize Private onboarding flow for two pilot accounts.
- Constraint: resource budget is tighter than last quarter—we need to trade scope for impact.

Intended outcomes from intake + workshop:
- Prioritized company slate (ranked A/B/C) with owners and expected customer impact metric
- Draft company-level OKRs (one objective + 3 key results) for each A-level slate item
- 3 exec-level asks (funding, headcount, cross-team unblock) with justification and fallback
- All‑hands narrative outline and a one-slide story arc per priority

Who should attend (leadership intake):
- CEO (founders), CRO, CTO, VP Product, Head of Eng, Head of SRE, Head of Applied ML, Head of Solutions Eng, Head of Customer Success.
- Prep note: bring one slide / 200 words summarizing request and impact.

Workshop agenda (90–120m):
1) 0–10m: opening context, sprint tempo, desired decisions (Sofia)
2) 10–25m: lightning OPS reports (capacity, runway, SRE ops risk)
3) 25–55m: priority pitches (8–10 x 3m pitches from leads)
4) 55–75m: scoring / vote (impact x effort) + clustering (facilitator: Priya)
5) 75–95m: draft OKR sketching in breakout groups (3 groups)
6) 95–110m: report back + decision calibration (leadership)
7) closing: owner assignments and immediate next steps

Decision rubric we will use (to guide votes):
- Customer impact (revenue, retention, strategic proof) — upweighted 40%
- Differentiation (makes Redwood stickier vs. cloud vendors) — 25%
- Implementation risk & speed (how quickly we can show outcomes) — 20%
- Cost/operational burden (ongoing maintenance) — 15%

Provisional priority candidates (brainstorm list — bring alternatives to workshop):
- Dedicated autoscale SLO: finalize policy + real‑time tracing for capacity; target: reduce request rejection rate to <0.1% under burst
- Optimize: public beta for intelligent batching + quant recommendation engine with per-workload cost delta report
- Private onboarding: 2 pilot on-prem installs + hardened KMS integration for compliance teams
- Runtime latency tuning: kernel selection improvements to reclaim 10–15% latency headroom for long sequences
- Console rollouts: enable canary/A-B for model versions + automatic fallback by policy
- Developer experience: SDK v2 (streaming, tool calling ergonomics) + migration plan for top 50 customers
- Eval/quality guardrails: production evals tied to core prompt set + regression alerting
- International region pilot (EU+APAC): region routing and fallbacks for latency-sensitive accounts

Example drafted OKR (template) — for discussion, not final:
Objective: Make Redwood the most predictable inference platform for enterprise teams
KR1: Decrease median P90 latency for 2048-token workloads by 12% across hosted models
KR2: Ship dedicated autoscale SLO and achieve <0.1% rejection during two industry load tests
KR3: Complete Private onboarding flow for 2 pilot customers and document 2 compliance signoffs

Exec asks (format: ask / owner / why / ask amount / fallback):
1) Ask: Short-term headcount (2 SWE backend, 1 SRE) to accelerate autoscale SLO work / Owner: Head of Eng (Daniel) / Why: current runway shows bottleneck in infra executions / Ask: 3 FTEs for 4 months (contract + hiring budget) / Fallback: narrow scope to policy only (defer tracing).
2) Ask: $200k pilot budget for Private deployment pilots (infrastructure + compliance consultancy) / Owner: Head Product / Why: closes two high-value deals that are in negotiation / Fallback: convert to credit-based partner offer and delay consultancy.
3) Ask: Temporary cross-team war-room for all-hands launch week (2 weeks) — request 1 PM + 1 Design floater + on-call rotations / Owner: Product Ops / Why: ensure zero-drop cadence for launch and support / Fallback: staggered launch windows for smaller cohorts.

Risks & open questions (we should surface in the workshop):
- Is there appetite to trade SDK v2 timeline vs. runtime latency work? Two big engineering needs that compete for same SWEs.
- Sales commitments: which customers are timing-sensitive for Private pilots (list needed before intake).
- Observability retention costs: can we compress retention windows for debug buckets to save cost?

Pre-reads (please read before the intake):
- Ops runway snapshot (link to Confluence) — key metrics: burn, infra commitments, reserved capacity utilization
- Top 6 customer asks (sheet linked) — customers asking for Private / Dedicated improvements
- Draft all-hands story arc (one-pager attached below in this doc)

Immediate deliverables after workshop (owner + due date):
- Finalized A/B/C slate + owners (Sofia) — due 48h after workshop
- Draft company OKRs (Priya) — due 72h after workshop
- Exec ask packet (slides + financials) (Daniel + Priya) — due 72h after workshop
- All‑hands slide deck skeleton (Comms + Product) — due 5 days after workshop

All‑hands narrative (one-paragraph sketch):
Open with context: "We built the runtime that makes inference fast — now we must make it predictable and cost-effective at scale." Follow with 3 pillar headlines aligned to priorities: (1) predictable capacity and SLAs (Dedicated), (2) smarter cost controls for customers (Optimize), (3) enterprise-ready privacy & compliance (Private). End with a practical call: what each team will do this quarter and how we will measure success.

Facilitation notes (practical):
- Keep pitches to 3 minutes. Timer in the room.
- Use MURAL board for clustering (link in Slack thread).
- Voting: each leader gets 6 points (3 high-impact, 2 medium, 1 wildcard). Translate scores to A/B/C.

Open items to resolve in intake meeting:
- Confirm list of pilot customers for Private and sales owner for each (Sales ops to provide by EOD Monday).
- Agree on definition of "rejection" for autoscale SLO (SRE to propose).
- Decide whether to fund headcount request from ops runway or special hiring bucket (Finance + CEO).

Notes to self / drafting traces (Sofia):
- Keep language tight for all-hands: avoid product jargon in opening slides — use customer stories.
- Prepare a short Q&A doc addressing anticipated questions (SRE outages, price implications, pilot timelines).
- Follow up: schedule 30-min sync next week with Sales leadership to lock pilot list.

Quick links (placeholders) and artifacts to attach to final doc:
- Confluence: ops runway (to attach)
- Sheet: customer ask tracker (to attach)
- Slack: #all-hands-prep thread
- PR: draft deck skeleton in repo

TODO / backlog (for after the all‑hands):
- Run 2 cross-team rehearsal sessions (ops + comms)
- Finalize FAQ and publish to developers.redwood.ai portal
- Create small survey for staff to capture misalignments after the all‑hands

Questions I want from reviewers now (please add inline comments):
- Do the scoring weights look fair?
- Any missing priority candidates that should be on the pitch list?
- Are the ask amounts realistic given runway?

— end working draft —

If you’re reading: add your 3-minute pitch as a comment below (title: team-name — 3m pitch).

title: Talent credentialing and rapid skill-eval protocol (pilot)
preview:
Purpose:
This doc outlines a lightweight, skills-first credentialing flow for mid-to-senior IC hires that shortens time-to-decision while improving onboarding signal. The goal is a reproducible pilot that: reduces interview overload, creates verifiable micro-credentials, and maps hiring outcomes directly to onboarding unlocks.

Scope and constraints:
- Pilot scope: backend, infra, and ML-serving adjacent ICs (eng levels IC3–IC5). Not aimed at non-engineering roles in this first phase.
- Timebox: 3-month pilot starting Sept 2025 with 30 hires cap.
- Privacy: candidate artifacts stored in the private hiring gdrive folder and SSO-protected Confluence page.
- Non-binary signal: we use granular pass thresholds per credential rather than a single pass/fail score.

High-level flow (candidate-facing):
1) Quick intake (30m) — recruiter screen to verify role fit and constraints.
2) Micro take-home (72h, 3–5 hour expected effort) — focused on a single real-world micro-problem with automated test harness or reproducibility instructions.
3) 60m focused deep-dive (panel of 2 interviewers) — design + code review of take-home; emphasis on trade-offs and correctness.
4) Behavioral + collaboration (30–45m) — async evidence review + 1:1 with hiring manager for product/context fit.
5) Credential issuance & offer decision — committee reviews micro-credentials and behavior anchors.

Why this differs from our standard loop:
- We move heavier cognitive workload to a time-boxed take-home to minimize repeated live interviews.
- Credentials are modular: candidates may pass some and not others; hiring committee can map partial passes to role variants or onboarding plans.
- Onboarding tie-ins: each credential unlocks a tailored first-30-day path and mentoring checkpoints.

Credentials (pilot set):
- Service Design & Reliability (SDR): simple service design, SLA reasoning, design for degradations.
- Code Composition & Readability (CCR): idiomatic code, tests, small library design.
- Production Debugging (PD): log/metric investigation, root-cause reasoning on synthetic incidents.
- Collaboration & Communication (C&C): written asynchronous updates, PR thread handling, stakeholder alignment.
- Security & Data Hygiene (S&DH) — baseline checks for data handling where relevant.

Scoring rubric (1–5 anchors):
- 5: Exemplar: clear, reproducible, anticipates edge cases, pragmatic trade-offs, scalable.
- 4: Strong: small gaps but solid trade-offs and tests, concise communication.
- 3: Competent: correct solution with some clarity or scaling gaps; acceptable for most IC3 roles.
- 2: Partial: major gaps in reasoning or missing important failure modes.
- 1: Insufficient: unsafe/incorrect or cannot articulate reasoning.

Panel calibration examples (short anchors for interviewers):
- SDR 5 anchor: presented a simple service with clear latency/cost trade-offs and fallback plan for cache misses; included a test plan and monitoring signals.
- CCR 5 anchor: small library with clear API, unit tests, and short README showing usage and decisions.
- PD 5 anchor: quickly scoped likely root cause from a synthetic metric spike, proposed a mitigation and a follow-up plan.

Decision rules (committee):
- Hiring threshold: at least 3 credentials scored >=4 OR 4 credentials scored >=3 with C&C >=4.
- If candidate is strong in two technical credentials but missing SDR, hiring manager may onboard into an embed role with mandatory SDR credentialing during the first 60 days.
- Borderline cases: recruiter + hiring manager propose an accelerated pre-commit course (2 weeks) and re-evaluate.

Onboarding mapping (what a credential unlocks):
- SDR pass: primary rotation onto production services, access to service runbook and Production Slack channel.
- CCR pass: codebase ownership rights + pairing schedule with senior engineer.
- PD pass: ability to take on-page pager rotations after 30 days and access to runbooks.
- C&C pass: assigned as primary owner for cross-team syncs in the first sprint.

Interviewer checklist (to be completed in the debrief doc):
- Time allocation: 10m intro, 35m deep dive, 15m focused closure and notes.
- Use anchor examples; write explicit strengths/weaknesses vs anchors.
- Add 1–2 pragmatic questions that matter for day-one tasks.
- File evidence links: take-home repo, test outputs, candidate writeup.

Data capture template (where we store signals):
- Google Sheet row per candidate: candidate id, role, take-home score per credential (1–5), panel scores, overall recommendation, interviewers, notes link.
- Confluence hiring folder: candidate artifacts, sanitized take-home ledger, and committee decision minutes.

Pilot metrics (3-month):
- Time-to-offer median (target <= 10 days from recruiter screen).
- Interviewer hours per hire (target <= 6 hours).
- Offer-to-accept ratio for pilot cohort.
- Activation delta: % of hires reaching fully productive milestones at 30 days vs baseline.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: take-home burden may decrease candidate funnel. Mitigation: clear expectation doc + compensated take-home (gift card) for senior roles.
- Risk: inconsistent interviewer scoring. Mitigation: weekly calibration sessions and shared anchor library.
- Risk: onboarding mismatch when credential gaps exist. Mitigation: mandatory early learning sprints and mentoring checkpoints.

Operational notes for pilot execution:
- Recruiters: qualifying script + explicit expectations sheet to share.
- Hiring managers: short hiring rubric meeting (15m) before candidate sends take-home.
- Interviewers: complete panel feedback within 48 hours; auto-reminder from hiring ops Slack bot.
- Data owner: People Ops (Aisha) will maintain pilot sheet and run-weekly sync.

Next steps (immediate):
- Finalize take-home templates for SDR, CCR, PD by Aug 29.
- Create Confluence evidence page and automated GSheet logging by Sept 5.
- Run interviewer calibration workshop Sept 8 (1 hour).
- Start candidate intake Sept 15.

Open questions (to resolve in pilot kickoff):
- Compensation for take-home work for different levels (what is fair for IC4+?).
- How to expose partial credentials in interview feedback to non-hire candidates without legal/compliance exposure.
- Where to store micro-credential badges and whether they should be portable across teams.

Notes to self / rough thoughts:
- Consider badges as conditional access tokens in our internal LMS — would make onboarding unlock automation much simpler.
- Keep the take-home scope narrow: prefer a single focused deliverable vs multiple smaller tasks.
- We should run a quick candidate survey post-process to measure perceived fairness and friction.

Appendix: sample micro-take-home (CCR):
- Problem: write a small CLI tool that reads a JSONL dataset, filters by date and tag, and emits aggregated counts. Provide unit tests and a short README explaining design and complexity. Expectation: 3–4 hours.
- Evaluation: correctness, test coverage, README clarity, API design, and edge-case handling.

Contact: Aisha Grant (people-ops) — pilot owner.

- 5: Exemplar: clear, reproducible, anticipates edge cases, pragmatic trade-offs, scalable.
- 4: Strong: small gaps but solid trade-offs and tests, concise communication.
- 3: Competent: correct solution with some clarity or scaling gaps; acceptable for most IC3 roles.
- 2: Partial: major gaps in reasoning or missing important failure modes.
- 1: Insufficient: unsafe/incorrect or cannot articulate reasoning.

Panel calibration examples (short anchors for interviewers):
- SDR 5 anchor: presented a simple service with clear latency/cost trade-offs and fallback plan for cache misses; included a test plan and monitoring signals.
- CCR 5 anchor: small library with clear API, unit tests, and short README showing usage and decisions.
- PD 5 anchor: quickly scoped likely root cause from a synthetic metric spike, proposed a mitigation and a follow-up plan.

Decision rules (committee):
- Hiring threshold: at least 3 credentials scored >=4 OR 4 credentials scored >=3 with C&C >=4.
- If candidate is strong in two technical credentials but missing SDR, hiring manager may onboard into an embed role with mandatory SDR credentialing during the first 60 days.
- Borderline cases: recruiter + hiring manager propose an accelerated pre-commit course (2 weeks) and re-evaluate.

Onboarding mapping (what a credential unlocks):
- SDR pass: primary rotation onto production services, access to service runbook and Production Slack channel.
- CCR pass: codebase ownership rights + pairing schedule with senior engineer.
- PD pass: ability to take on-page pager rotations after 30 days and access to runbooks.
- C&C pass: assigned as primary owner for cross-team syncs in the first sprint.

Interviewer checklist (to be completed in the debrief doc):
- Time allocation: 10m intro, 35m deep dive, 15m focused closure and notes.
- Use anchor examples; write explicit strengths/weaknesses vs anchors.
- Add 1–2 pragmatic questions that matter for day-one tasks.
- File evidence links: take-home repo, test outputs, candidate writeup.

Data capture template (where we store signals):
- Google Sheet row per candidate: candidate id, role, take-home score per credential (1–5), panel scores, overall recommendation, interviewers, notes link.
- Confluence hiring folder: candidate artifacts, sanitized take-home ledger, and committee decision minutes.

Pilot metrics (3-month):
- Time-to-offer median (target <= 10 days from recruiter screen).
- Interviewer hours per hire (target <= 6 hours).
- Offer-to-accept ratio for pilot cohort.
- Activation delta: % of hires reaching fully productive milestones at 30 days vs baseline.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: take-home burden may decrease candidate funnel. Mitigation: clear expectation doc + compensated take-home (gift card) for senior roles.
- Risk: inconsistent interviewer scoring. Mitigation: weekly calibration sessions and shared anchor library.
- Risk: onboarding mismatch when credential gaps exist. Mitigation: mandatory early learning sprints and mentoring checkpoints.

Operational notes for pilot execution:
- Recruiters: qualifying script + explicit expectations sheet to share.
- Hiring managers: short hiring rubric meeting (15m) before candidate sends take-home.
- Interviewers: complete panel feedback within 48 hours; auto-reminder from hiring ops Slack bot.
- Data owner: People Ops (Aisha) will maintain pilot sheet and run-weekly sync.

Next steps (immediate):
- Finalize take-home templates for SDR, CCR, PD by Aug 29.
- Create Confluence evidence page and automated GSheet logging by Sept 5.
- Run interviewer calibration workshop Sept 8 (1 hour).
- Start candidate intake Sept 15.

Open questions (to resolve in pilot kickoff):
- Compensation for take-home work for different levels (what is fair for IC4+?).
- How to expose partial credentials in interview feedback to non-hire candidates without legal/compliance exposure.
- Where to store micro-credential badges and whether they should be portable across teams.

Notes to self / rough thoughts:
- Consider badges as conditional access tokens in our internal LMS — would make onboarding unlock automation much simpler.
- Keep the take-home scope narrow: prefer a single focused deliverable vs multiple smaller tasks.
- We should run a quick candidate survey post-process to measure perceived fairness and friction.

Appendix: sample micro-take-home (CCR):
- Problem: write a small CLI tool that reads a JSONL dataset, filters by date and tag, and emits aggregated counts. Provide unit tests and a short README explaining design and complexity. Expectation: 3–4 hours.
- Evaluation: correctness, test coverage, README clarity, API design, and edge-case handling.

Contact: Aisha Grant (people-ops) — pilot owner.

title: Contributor Growth Pathway & Interview Lite Rubric
preview:
Purpose:
This doc combines a short, practical contributor growth pathway (onboarding + 30/90 plan) with an \"interview-lite\" rubric for fast-moving hiring loops. Goal: reduce ambiguity in early-stage offers, speed up decisions for small teams, and provide managers a repeatable first-90-days template.

Scope / When to use:
- Use for IC hires (E3-E5 equivalent) and internal lateral moves where we want a lightweight loop (2-3 interviews) instead of a full multi-panel process.
- Not for senior ICs (staff/principal) or leadership hires — those keep the full rubric.

Why this exists (rationale):
- Hiring velocity: small teams need quick decisions.
- Predictability: ensure consistent expectations for ramp and early performance.
- Signal alignment: capture enough data to evaluate craft + team fit without over-indexing on whiteboard perfection.

Hiring plan (high level):
- Requisition -> Screening -> Interview-lite loop -> Offer -> 30/90 onboarding plan locked in with manager and buddy.
- Interview-lite loop: Recruiter screen (30m), Technical pairing or take-home (optional), Manager deep-dive (45m), Team/peer culture chat (30m). Aim to keep total interviewing time < 2.5 hours.

Stakeholders and responsibilities:
- Hiring manager: defines success criteria and first-90-day objectives, picks buddy, signs offer.
- Recruiter: screens, coordinates loop, captures salary band expectations.
- People Ops (Aisha): approves final offer above band, maintains template, runs calibration.
- Interviewers: score using rubric and add short written evidence (1-2 bullets).

Interview-lite rubric (use 1-4 scale; also capture binary hire/pass recommendation):
- Scoring guidance: 1 = not yet; 2 = basic competence; 3 = strong; 4 = exceptional / above level.

Core dimensions (keep short):
1) Problem-solving & clarity (how they structure, clarify assumptions)
2) Execution & shipping (product-minded tradeoffs, prior delivery examples)
3) Code/craft fundamentals (readability, testability, simple design)
4) Collaboration & communication (cross-team, feedback loops)
5) Role-fit & mission alignment (interest in the product, practical motivation)

Example rubric row (to paste into interviewer's note):
- Problem-solving: 3 — framed the problem, asked clarifying Qs, proposed sensible decomposition (evidence: worked through paging/latency tradeoff example)
- Execution: 2 — limited concrete examples of finished projects; tended to focus on design rather than delivery (evidence: mid-sized refactor anecdote, no metrics)
- Craft: 3 — clean pseudocode, good tests suggestions (evidence: unit test structure described)
- Collaboration: 4 — strong cross-team example, gave credit to others, described conflict resolution
- Role-fit: 3 — curious about serving economics, asked smart product questions
- Recommendation: hire-with-caveat (ask for short take-home or pair to validate delivery details)

Calibration notes for interviewers:
- Use specific evidence. One-line behavior examples are required for each score (what did they say / do).
- Pull score to a binary: PASS if the median dimension >= 3 and no dimension = 1.
- If split, default is \"manager tie-breaker\" but encourage calibration chat within 48 hours.

Manager decision checklist (pre-offer):
- Clear 3 objectives for 30 days, 90 days, and owner for each objective.
- Buddy assigned and introduced in offer email thread.
- Ramp tasks queued in repo/board (first-week tickets already created).
- Dev environment checklist shared (access, VPN, KMS keys if needed).
- Compensation & leveling confirmed with People Ops.

Sample 30/90 microcurriculum (template manager uses with new hire):
- First week (onboarding sprint):
  - Day 0: welcome, HR + tooling setup, team intro, buddy assigned.
  - Day 1-3: dev environment + small 'hello world' patch + pair with buddy to ship it.
  - Day 4-5: attend product sync, read design and ops runbooks for the service owner.
- First 30 days:
  - Complete 2 small tickets from backlog end-to-end.
  - Attend two oncall handoffs / incident blameless postmortems as observer.
  - Deliver first written doc: \"area readback\" (short summary of system and improvement idea).
- 30-90 days:
  - Lead a small change that impacts customers (feature or perf).
  - Show measurable impact (latency, error rate, throughput, developer time saved).
  - Begin regular 1:1 cadence with manager, feedback checkpoint at 45 days.

Buddy rotation & responsibilities (practical):
- Buddy should be peer-level or one level above. Primary week 1 contact, does pairing and code reviews for first two PRs.
- 15-min daily sync for first 5 days, then 2x week for weeks 2-4.
- Buddy hands off to team mentor for longer-term technical growth.

Operational checklist (people ops):
- Offer packet template updated? (TODO)
- Access list to common infra (train infra-team to grant dev access within 48 hrs).
- Spreadsheet for new hires to track env access, hardware needs, and onboarding status.

Success metrics (how manager will know ramp is on track):
- Within 30d: 2 merged PRs, dev env complete, participates in squad sync.
- Within 90d: owns at least one customer-impacting rollout (can be small), demonstrates proactive debugging.
- Manager subjective: show curiosity and collaborative behaviors.

Decision governance & edge cases:
- If a candidate has exceptional collaboration but weaker craft, consider a trial contract (3-month contractor) with clear success criteria.
- If hire is below expectations at 45d, use a \"focused ramp plan\" with documented checkpoints. If no measurable progress by 90d, follow performance improvement process.

Templates (paste into interviewer's note / manager onboarding):
- 30d objectives (example): \"Understand the ingestion pipeline; ship an end-to-end fix that reduces median latency by 10% for local experiments; own backlog for a small area.\"
- 90d objectives (example): \"Lead a rollout with feature flags; improve observability for your owned area; mentor one teammate on the code path.\"

Unresolved questions / things to decide (notes to self):
- Tie-break rule for ties > should we use score-weighted average or manager only? (current default: manager with calibration chat)
- Do we require take-homes for certain roles (data infra)? Need list by role.
- Consider an abbreviated \"trial hire\" template for contractors -> convert to full-time.

Last bits of guidance (for interviewers and managers):
- Keep interviews conversational; focus on examples that show how they shipped.
- Require evidence statements — they remove ambiguity.
- Keep the loop short but make onboarding guarantees (dev env in 48 hrs, buddy assigned).

Quick TODOs:
- [ ] Update offer template to include buddy plan and explicit 30/90 objectives.
- [ ] Share rubric in people-ops channel and collect feedback by 2025-09-10.
- [ ] Create a small repo with first-week starter tickets (infra + frontend + api).

Notes: this is intentionally compact to be copied into candidate tracking (Greenhouse / Lever) and into manager offer emails. We will iterate after 3 hires using this flow.

Comments / freeform brainstorming (raw):
- Could also have a \"trial deliverable\" option: 2-week paid assignment instead of take-home — better signal and lower candidate drop-off.
- Letting buddies own dev env acceleration might be a small team-level KPI.

End.

title: Cross-Role Rapid Assessment Kit — 72hr Practicals, Interviewer Rubrics, Day-Zero Remote Setup
preview:
Purpose and scope:
This is a lightweight, reusable kit for running a 72-hour practical assessment across roles (SWE/backend, ML engineer/infra, product manager, SRE/ops). The goal is to: 1) get a sense for hands-on problem solving and communication, 2) reduce onsite time by pushing practicals earlier, 3) provide consistent scoring and a minimal day-zero checklist so accepted candidates can be productive on day 1.

Intended use and constraints:
- Use for mid-to-senior IC hires (IC3-IC5).
- Volunteer-based practicals: candidate chooses to accept practical phase after initial screen.
- Should take ~6–10 hours total for candidate over 72 hours (split tasks + writeups).
- Not a take-home whiteboard replacement — emphasize realistic artifacts (small PR, infra terraform snippet, decision memo, runbook).
- Timeboxed and scaffolded to respect candidate bandwidth; compensation offered when >6 hours.

How to pick tasks by role (examples):
- SWE (backend): 72-hour practical = small service feature + test + deploy notes
  - Deliverable: git repo with README, single endpoint implemented, 2 unit tests, and a short PR description that notes scaling considerations (max 500 words).
  - Data: starter repo template + sample dataset (100–500 rows).

- ML infra/Applied ML: 72-hour practical = model serving pipeline stub + latency/throughput note
  - Deliverable: Jupyter notebook or small repo that loads a provided model, wraps inference in a server (FastAPI) and includes a one-page infra diagram and perf/quant tradeoffs.

- Product Manager (platform/product): 72-hour practical = 1-page product brief + rollout plan + acceptance criteria
  - Deliverable: 1–2 page doc: problem statement, target personas, success metrics (2–3), rollout phases, launch risks and mitigation. Include 3 interview-style follow-up questions for cross-functional partners.

- SRE/Infra: 72-hour practical = incident runbook + short playbook + small infra-as-code snippet
  - Deliverable: runbook for a simulated high-latency GPU serving incident, a Terraform/CloudFormation snippet showing an autoscaling policy, and a short retro identifying the top 3 root causes and mitigations.

Candidate instructions (to send in initial email):
- You have 72 hours from receipt to submit the deliverables. Focus on clarity, tradeoffs, and a working artifact—not production polish.
- Budget: plan ~6–10 hours across the window. If you need more time, reply to request an extension (allowed once for up to 48 hours).
- We provide starter templates, a sandbox repo, and a dataset where applicable.
- Compensation: $200 for >6 hours of work (USD, paid if candidate completes the practical).

Interviewer / grader guidance:
- Reviewers should be two people from the core hiring team (one primary role expert + one cross-functional reviewer). Aim for independent grading first, then a short calibration call.
- Score each deliverable on a 1–5 scale for: Correctness, Design/Tradeoffs, Clarity/Communication, Practicality/Deployability. Record short notes (2–4 bullet lines).

Scoring rubric (flat, copyable):
- Correctness (1–5): Does the artifact meet acceptance criteria? 1 = irrelevant/doesn't run; 5 = meets requirements and handles edge cases.
- Design/Tradeoffs (1–5): Shows thoughtful design, understands constraints, quantifies tradeoffs.
- Clarity/Communication (1–5): README/PR text/doc is concise, easy to follow, decisions explained.
- Practicality/Deployability (1–5): Is the solution realistic to ship (infra, testing, rollback, monitoring considered)?

Pass threshold guidance (team-level decision):
- Strong pass: avg score >= 4.0 AND no single category < 3.
- Soft pass: avg score between 3.2—3.99, discuss in calibration and prioritize followup interviews focused on weaknesses.
- Reject: avg < 3.2 OR major safety/security concern.

Interviewer calibration notes (short):
- Keep calibration sessions under 30 minutes. Share red/amber/green flags in chat prior to sync.
- Avoid grading harshly on polish; prioritize reasoning and tradeoffs.
- For PM candidates, treat the brief as a conversation starter; evaluate ability to synthesize feedback.

Example red/amber/green quick list (copyable):
- Red: Security-sensitive mistake (e.g., logging secrets), or solution would cause major production regression.
- Amber: Missing test coverage for critical paths, unclear rollout plan, or undocumented assumptions.
- Green: Thoughtful monitoring plan, clear rollback, clear rationale for tradeoffs.

Communication templates (short) — to candidate:
- Invite to practical: "We enjoyed our initial conversation. As the next step we offer a short, paid practical to better understand how you approach hands-on problems. You have 72 hours and an estimated 6–10 hour budget. Please review the attached template and reply to confirm. Compensation is provided upon completion."
- Extension approved: "Extension granted until <new date>. Please prioritize clarity and submit when ready."

Day-Zero remote-first onboarding checklist (minimal, to attach to offer/acceptance email):
- Pre-arrival: account creation requests sent (SSO, email alias), laptop shipping scheduled (if needed), NDA signed, payroll/team paperwork done.
- Day 0 access (validate): Slack, GitHub, JIRA/Linear, internal wiki, repo access for starter template, VPN/VPC access (if private deployment candidate).
- First-3-days priorities: 1) Pair with assigned buddy for office tour and tooling; 2) Complete security orientation (KMS/HSM basics, secret handling); 3) Run local starter repo and create first tiny PR; 4) Schedule 1:1 with hiring manager and mentor.
- Hardware/software quick checklist: OS image, Docker + docker-compose, Python 3.10, Node LTS, kubectl, Terraform v1.x, preferred editor configs (dotfiles link).

Mentor pairing & onboarding sprint (example 10-day mini-sprint):
- Day 1: kickoff, access checks, run starter local project, 30m product orientation.
- Day 2–3: shadow buddy, land first small PR, security/infra intro.
- Day 4–6: small feature/bug assignment with mentor pairing.
- Day 7–10: handoff and autonomy ramp, demo to team, retro on onboarding friction.

Minimal acceptance criteria for fast hires (for hiring committee):
- Completed practical with clear artifacts and independent deployable proof-of-concept (or reproducible instructions).
- Two reviewers with overall non-reject and at least one strong pass in a core category.
- Candidate demonstrates asynchronous communication (concise PR/msg updates) during the practical window.

Notes on fairness and accessibility:
- Provide alt formats on request (e.g., longer times, phone-based walk-through).
- Explicitly state in invite that candidates can reach out to request accommodations.
- Keep tasks role-representative, not biased by prior internal tooling (provide adapters/starter scripts).

Operational checklist for People Ops coordinator (copy-and-paste in ticket):
1) Create candidate sandbox repo from template and grant permissions.
2) Send practical invite + templates + compensation note.
3) Assign two reviewers and schedule 20m calibration call window after submissions.
4) Track submission and trigger payment on completion.
5) Log scores + notes to hiring packet (Confluence or candidate folder).

Open questions / things to iterate on (working):
- Should PM practicals include a short user interview simulation? (pros/cons)
- Compensation thresholds—should senior bands get higher flat rate? (proposal: scale by band: mid=$200, senior=$350)
- Integrations: can candidate github apps be used for private repos without excess admin friction?

Appendix: quick templates (copyable snippets)
- PR description template: Summary, What I changed, How to run, Testing notes, Rollout plan, Open questions.
- Runbook skeleton: Symptoms -> Immediate checks -> Mitigation steps -> Escalation path -> Post-incident notes.
- One-page product brief headers: Problem, Why now, Target users, Metrics (north star + 2 supporting), Launch plan, Risks/mitigations.

Last updated by Ava Chen 2025-09-02 — comment below with feedback or proposed changes.

- Extension approved: "Extension granted until <new date>. Please prioritize clarity and submit when ready."

Day-Zero remote-first onboarding checklist (minimal, to attach to offer/acceptance email):
- Pre-arrival: account creation requests sent (SSO, email alias), laptop shipping scheduled (if needed), NDA signed, payroll/team paperwork done.
- Day 0 access (validate): Slack, GitHub, JIRA/Linear, internal wiki, repo access for starter template, VPN/VPC access (if private deployment candidate).
- First-3-days priorities: 1) Pair with assigned buddy for office tour and tooling; 2) Complete security orientation (KMS/HSM basics, secret handling); 3) Run local starter repo and create first tiny PR; 4) Schedule 1:1 with hiring manager and mentor.
- Hardware/software quick checklist: OS image, Docker + docker-compose, Python 3.10, Node LTS, kubectl, Terraform v1.x, preferred editor configs (dotfiles link).

Mentor pairing & onboarding sprint (example 10-day mini-sprint):
- Day 1: kickoff, access checks, run starter local project, 30m product orientation.
- Day 2–3: shadow buddy, land first small PR, security/infra intro.
- Day 4–6: small feature/bug assignment with mentor pairing.
- Day 7–10: handoff and autonomy ramp, demo to team, retro on onboarding friction.

Minimal acceptance criteria for fast hires (for hiring committee):
- Completed practical with clear artifacts and independent deployable proof-of-concept (or reproducible instructions).
- Two reviewers with overall non-reject and at least one strong pass in a core category.
- Candidate demonstrates asynchronous communication (concise PR/msg updates) during the practical window.

Notes on fairness and accessibility:
- Provide alt formats on request (e.g., longer times, phone-based walk-through).
- Explicitly state in invite that candidates can reach out to request accommodations.
- Keep tasks role-representative, not biased by prior internal tooling (provide adapters/starter scripts).

Operational checklist for People Ops coordinator (copy-and-paste in ticket):
1) Create candidate sandbox repo from template and grant permissions.
2) Send practical invite + templates + compensation note.
3) Assign two reviewers and schedule 20m calibration call window after submissions.
4) Track submission and trigger payment on completion.
5) Log scores + notes to hiring packet (Confluence or candidate folder).

Open questions / things to iterate on (working):
- Should PM practicals include a short user interview simulation? (pros/cons)
- Compensation thresholds—should senior bands get higher flat rate? (proposal: scale by band: mid=$200, senior=$350)
- Integrations: can candidate github apps be used for private repos without excess admin friction?

Appendix: quick templates (copyable snippets)
- PR description template: Summary, What I changed, How to run, Testing notes, Rollout plan, Open questions.
- Runbook skeleton: Symptoms -> Immediate checks -> Mitigation steps -> Escalation path -> Post-incident notes.
- One-page product brief headers: Problem, Why now, Target users, Metrics (north star + 2 supporting), Launch plan, Risks/mitigations.

Last updated by Ava Chen 2025-09-02 — comment below with feedback or proposed changes.

title: Candidate acceptance contingency and 5-day boarding template
preview:
PURPOSE:
Short reference for: (1) offer acceptance contingency steps when candidate hesitates or declines, (2) a hardened 5-day boarding checklist (first business week) for hiring manager + ops, and (3) a compact interviewer decision rubric used by hiring panels.

SCOPE:
Applies to IC and manager-level offers across engineering, product, DX. Private-deploy/enterprise hires follow additional security checklist (see CONF-7821).

PART A — OFFER ACCEPTANCE CONTINGENCY (quick playbook)
- Goal: avoid offer loss and minimize time-to-hire by having a clear 3-tier fallback plan when an accepted offer is at risk or candidate counter-offers.
- Owners: recruiter (primary), hiring manager (secondary), people-ops lead (escalation).

When candidate signals hesitation or delay (>48h after verbal acceptance):
1) Immediate recruiter steps (0-24h):
   - Call candidate to surface objections (comp, start date, team fit, visa). Use script: "Quick check-in — what concerns would make you pause?"
   - Send short one-pager with offer summary + one-line reminders about equity cliff/start date.
   - If comp is blocker, confirm flexibility band and get hiring manager approval for any adjustments (record decision in RECRUIT-410).
2) Negotiation window (24-72h):
   - If counter-offer flagged, ask candidate for specifics and timeline. Note: avoid immediate counter-bid without manager sign-off.
   - People-ops to prepare final-approval email template for hiring manager/exec sign-off when needed.
3) Fallback (72h+ or decline):
   - Re-open priority list: contact top-of-slate alternates (fast-track interviews if required).
   - If role is critical, trigger "accelerated refill" (recruiter + hiring manager sync in 24h) and mark role urgent in tracker (RECRUIT-410).

COMMUNICATION TEMPLATES (snippets)
- To candidate when probing: "We want to make sure this role is a fit — can you share the one or two things holding you back? Happy to loop in the hiring manager to clarify."
- To hiring manager for comp flex: "Candidate X raised a counter-offer. Can you confirm max flexibility (salary band / sign-on / start date) within 48h?"
- To alternate candidate: "Wanted to check if you’re still open — we had a strong candidate but the slot may reopen. Quick 30-min call available tomorrow?"

DECISION LOGGING (must): recruiter records every commit/ask in RECRUIT-410 with timestamp and whether manager approved change. Keep short notes only.

PART B — 5-DAY BOARDING CHECKLIST (first business week)
Owners: hiring manager (HM) = responsible for role onboarding; people-ops (PO) = access, payroll; IT/infra = hardware and VPN.

Pre-day-0 (before start):
- PO: background checks complete, payroll set, contract signed and scanned in HR system.
- IT: laptop shipped or reserved; asset tag + basic hardware spec confirmed.
- HM: first-week 1:1 scheduled, team intro Slack channel created, 30-60-90 doc stub shared.

Day 0 / first morning:
- HM: welcome + team kickoff (15m), buddy intro, run through calendar for week.
- IT: confirm device delivery / login (VPN, Okta MFA), add to team Google Drive and Slack.
- PO: benefits overview link + point of contact.

Day 1:
- HM: role context deep-dive (60m) — product/infra map, current priorities, immediate ownerable task (small shipping ticket).
- Buddy: walkthrough of dev environment and repo checkouts.
- Security: complete minimum compliance training (15m).

Day 2:
- HM + new hire: define 7-day ramp task and success criteria.
- HM: schedule meet-and-greets with cross-functional partners.
- IT: confirm access to internal dashboards and monitoring tools.

Day 3-4:
- New hire: start the first shipping microtask (assign ticket), buddy reviews PR process.
- HM: daily 1:1 stand-in (15m) to remove blockers.

Day 5 (end of week):
- HM: week-1 review — confirm accessibility, task status, and set 30/60/90 expectations.
- PO: run first-week intake survey (3 quick questions) and log any outstanding onboarding issues.

CHECKPOINTS (must-haves by day 5): device functional, primary accounts active, at least one PR opened, payroll verified.

Quick templates to assign microtask: 
- "Please pick issue #1234 (label: onboard-micro) and submit a PR by EOD Tue. Buddy: review and leave two actionable comments"

PART C — INTERVIEWER DECISION RUBRIC (compact)
Purpose: harmonize panel scoring so offers are comparable and defensible. Keep it short — 3 axes + overall hire/no-hire call.
Axes (each 0-3):
- Technical competence (0 = no relevant skill; 3 = clearly exceeds requirements and can mentor others)
- Problem solving & trade-offs (0 = cannot decompose; 3 = anticipates edge-cases and cost/latency tradeoffs)
- Team and communication (0 = not collaborative; 3 = clear influence + psychological safety examples)

Scoring guidance (quick):
- 3: gives specific, recent examples; can explain decisions to non-experts; shows ownership experience.
- 2: meets bar, some gaps but recoverable with coaching.
- 1: partial answer or shallow examples; needs further screening.
- 0: significant red flags (cannot reason about core responsibilities).

Panel decision rule (simple majority):
- Offer recommended if >=2 reviewers give a 2 or 3, and no 0s in technical competence.
- Escalate to hiring manager and people-ops if split (one 3, one 1) — require calibration discussion.
- Hard no if two or more axes have 0 from different reviewers.

Interview notes template (one-line per area):
- Tech: [score] — evidence summary (2–3 bullets)
- Problem solving: [score] — approach described, trade-offs
- Collaboration: [score] — cross-team impact examples
- Risks / open questions: short list

PART D — HIRING SPRINT PLAN (48–96h rapid refill)
When a spot reopens unexpectedly:
- Day 0: recruiter and HM triage candidate slate and identify 3 fast alternates; schedule 30-min screen for each within 48h.
- Day 1–2: pair-screen + panel loop (compact: 60m panel), decision call within 24h.
- Goal: extend offer within 72–96h of trigger.

NOTES / RUNBOOK (rough):
- Have 2 alternates kept warm at all times for critical roles; update tracker weekly.
- Keep a short "offer playbook" with approved sign-on ranges per level to avoid delays.
- For remote hires, account for equipment shipping (add 3 business days to earliest start date) and mention explicitly in offer.

OPEN QUESTIONS / TODOs:
- Add manager-level calibration sessions monthly? (Priya to pilot Sep 2025)
- Link to enterprise security onboarding flow (CONF-xxxx) — need to insert.
- Build an automated Slack reminder for recruiters when candidate acceptance >48h pending (RECRUIT-410).

APPENDIX (quick references):
- Offer email template ID: CONF-7821/offers/email-v2 (internal)
- Refill tracker: RECRUIT-410 board (kanban)
- Payroll contact: payroll@redwood-inference.com

NOTES TO SELF: keep this doc intentionally terse — playbook for fast ops, not the long-form onboarding guide. Collect feedback after two pilot re-fills.

title: Asynchronous hire triage and Day0 remote hire inventory
preview:
Purpose:
This is a lightweight working doc for people-ops + hiring managers to triage incoming offers and to run a compact Day0 inventory for remote hires asynchronously. The goal: reduce friction for fast hires, make responsibilities explicit, and capture interviewer calibration notes when some feedback is async.

Scope:
- Applies to IC hires, contractors converting to perm, and most remote new hires across eng/product/design.
- Not a full 90-day plan — this is pre-boarding and Day0 readiness + interview feedback triage for distributed loops.

Quick triage flow (one-liner):
1) Offer accepted -> people-ops sends welcome packet + Day0 checklist -> manager confirms first-week goals -> IT/infra ticket created -> account invites issued -> async interviewers submit rubric scores within 48h.

Pre-boarding checklist (people-ops owner unless noted):
- HR paperwork: e-sign offer, W-8/W-9 (if applicable), NDA, benefits enroll link.
- Background check kick-off (if required) - tracker: HR-412.
- Equipment ask: laptop preference form sent; standard build vs custom request. (Owner: IT)
- Asset policy/MDM: add to Jamf/Intune + confirm enrollment link in mail.
- Access invites: Slack, Google Workspace, GitHub dept/team, Okta group, Zendesk (if support), Confluence page access.
- Cloud: AWS IAM role request template done; add to central billing project. (Owner: infra-onboard)
- Security: send first-day mandatory training and SSO MFA enrollment instructions.
- Manager setup: first-week goals doc skeleton + buddy assignment.

Day0 inventory (what must be green before Day0 morning):
- Laptop received and imaged OR confirmed pickup time.
- Primary corporate email active and forwarded to personal (if needed).
- Slack and Okta SSO verified.
- GitHub org invite accepted and repo read access confirmed.
- Access to Confluence onboarding space + product wiki.
- AWS console / GCP test access validated for staging.
- Calendar invite: 1:1 + team standup + onboarding orientation.

Asynchronous interview feedback rubric (short form for async reviewers):
- Use a 1-5 scale on three axes: Core Skills (role fit), Collaboration & Communication, Unknowns/Risk.
- Quick guidance: 1 = insufficient evidence, 3 = meets bar for L3/L4 depending on role, 5 = exceptional / hire-and-bump.
- Free text required: top-2 strengths, top-2 concerns, suggested probe if we want a follow-up interview.
- Tag with recommended level: IC3 | IC4 | IC5 and confidence: low/medium/high.

Calibration notes to reviewers (people-ops / hiring mgr):
- Be explicit about concrete signal: \"designed X system that scaled to Y\" vs vague \"worked on scaling.\"
- If you saw a behavioral gap but the candidate has compensating strengths, call it out and recommend a targeted follow-up (no more than 30m).
- Avoid writing subjective shorthand (e.g., \"meh\"). Use evidence.

Async feedback template (paste into reviewer comment):
- Score: Core Skills 4 / Collaboration 3 / Risk 2
- Strengths: bullet list (2)
- Concerns: bullet list (2)
- Suggested follow-up question: one line
- Recommend: hire | hire-with-conditions | follow-up | no-hire (circle one)

Manager Day0 responsibilities (quick checklist):
- Send welcome DM in team Slack with meeting links and buddy intro.
- Populate first-week goals on shared doc and invite new hire to edit.
- Run 30-minute orientation: team structure, immediate priorities, communication norms.
- Confirm access to required infra/tools and escalate any missing items to people-ops+IT.

IT/Infra quick action list (tickets):
- Priority: laptop imaging ticket + shipping confirmation.
- IAM groups: add to 'eng-readers', 'deploy-sandbox' as applicable.
- Secrets: ensure vault path created and new hire has read pattern for staging keys. (Security approval required for prod access.)

Candidate communication cadence (first 7 days):
- Day -3: Welcome packet + Day0 checklist (people-ops).
- Day -1: Equipment confirmation + calendar + Slack invite reminder.
- Day 0 AM: orientation + buddy check-in.
- Day 1 PM: 15-min manager sync + JIT FAQ note.
- Day 3: retro with buddy (what worked / blockers).
- Day 7: check-in: manager + people-ops quick survey.

Open questions / TODOs (notes to self):
- Consider short video walkthrough of common first-week tasks for remote hires (short screencast). Owner: Marisol.
- Decide on one canonical place for async interview scores (Confluence page vs HR tracker). Current trackers: HR-412 and onboarding runbook — consolidate.
- Determine SLA for reviewer feedback: 48h after interview, escalate to hiring manager if missing.
- Confirm whether contractors converting to perm follow same background check flow (legal question).

Appendix: short templates
- Welcome DM: \"Hi <name>, welcome to <team>! I'm <manager>. Here's your first-day link: <zoom>. Your buddy is <name>. Ping any time.\"
- Follow-up request to reviewer: \"Quick ask: can you post a 2-sentence rationale for your score in the hiring tracker by EOD? If not, ping <manager>.\"

References & links:
- Onboarding Day0 runbook (Confluence): https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/HR/pages/Onboarding+Day0+Runbook
- Hiring tracker: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/HR-412

Notes to self (informal):
- This doc purposely minimal — for heavy hires we should attach role-specific 30/60/90.
- Keep this as a living template; prefer short checkboxes and copy-paste templates for managers.
- Next iteration: add a small automated checklist email that compiles missing items and pings owners.

End of doc.

title: tempo-reset-and-handoff-playbook-q3-v0-1
preview:
Purpose:
This doc is a lightweight, operational playbook for a compact company-wide ‘Tempo Reset’ and leadership handoff to kick off Q3 planning. Aim is to provide a short, repeatable runbook that centers three things: clarity of North Star priorities, a compact OKR framing for each org, and a pragmatic set of asks for managers to execute in the following two weeks.

Context / Why now:
- After the product ramp and hiring bursts in H1, we’re seeing drift in cross-team focus and inconsistent rollout expectations.
- Leadership wants a fast, low-friction sync (not a long-form all-hands) to align on what to decouple, what to double-down on, and how to measure success for the next 6–10 weeks.
- This is intentionally tactical: align, handoff, and equip managers with specific deliverables.

High-level objectives for the Tempo Reset (one-pager version):
1) Calibrate: 1–3 company-level priorities with crisp tradeoffs and resource signals.
2) Commit: each org proposes 1–2 measurable outcomes that ladder up to company priorities.
3) Accelerate: concrete manager-level actions and decisions for the next 14 days.

Audience:
- All staff: 45-minute live session + follow-up 30-minute team breakouts.
- Leadership: pre-brief 1-hour prior to live session.
- Managers: post-session execution checklist and synchronized delivery calendar.

Proposed Run of Show (45 minutes):
- 00:00–00:05 — Quick framing from CEO (why we’re resetting tempo; top-level constraints)
- 00:05–00:12 — Ops + Product short state: 3 signals (latency/cost/regressions) and immediate gaps
- 00:12–00:22 — Leadership 3 priority callouts (each leader 2 minutes) — language: focus, de-risk, defer
- 00:22–00:30 — Concrete commitments: Product, Infra, DX, CS each present 1 measurable outcome + owner
- 00:30–00:40 — Q&A panel (typed questions prioritized by staff upvotes)
- 00:40–00:45 — Manager asks and next steps (closing by COO)

Manager Execution Checklist (post-session, required within 14 days):
- Translate company priorities into 1–2 team outcomes with metrics (owner, metric, target).
- Publish a 1-page plan in team drive (template provided).
- Sync with cross-functional stakeholders; list blockers and choices (escalate any capacity conflicts).
- Confirm rollout windows for any user-facing changes; submit rollout tickets to release-engineering.

Draft Company-level OKR Framing (working hypotheses):
- Objective A: Make inference predictable in cost and latency for producer applications.
  - KR A1: Reduce median hosted API p50 latency for 8k tokens by 12% vs baseline (measured week-over-week).
  - KR A2: Decrease per-token cost for core inference workloads by 8% through batching/quantization recommendations adoption.

- Objective B: Increase reliability and graceful degradation coverage for customer SLAs.
  - KR B1: Implement model-variant fallback flows for top 3 customers, validated in staging and observability dashboards.
  - KR B2: Reduce incident MTTR for serving runtime incidents by 25%.

- Objective C: Improve developer velocity for customers onboarding Redwood Private.
  - KR C1: Reduce average onboarding time from contract to production by 20%.
  - KR C2: Publish two new onboarding templates and one automated checklist in Console docs.

Note: These are intentionally coarse; each product area should propose refined KRs during team breakouts.

All-staff Deliverables (days 0–14):
- Day 0: Publish annotated recording and TL;DR (one paragraph per leader) + team breakout assignments.
- Day 3: Managers publish draft team outcomes and blockers in the drive.
- Day 7: Cross-functional review window; leadership will call any unresolved escalations.
- Day 14: Finalize team outcomes; ops to publish the consolidated execution calendar.

Communication plan (who says what):
- CEO: 2-paragraph memo (tone: candid, decisive) at time of reset.
- COO (closing): 3 concrete operational asks for managers; post in #company-ops and email digest.
- Product/Infra leads: brief technical notes for SRE/engineers and a pointer to rollout tickets.

Risks & mitigations:
- Risk: Reset perceived as top-down reorg.
  - Mitigation: emphasize constrained horizon (6–10 weeks), optional deferrals, and manager autonomy on execution approach.
- Risk: Too many small KRs diluting focus.
  - Mitigation: leadership filter — accept max 2 team outcomes per org that ladder to company priorities.
- Risk: Lack of measurement hygiene (disparate metrics).
  - Mitigation: ops will publish KPI baseline templates and a required metric owner for each KR.

Templates & artifacts to publish with this doc:
- 1-page team outcome template (QA checklist, metric definition)
- Breakout facilitator guide (questions and timeboxes)
- Consolidated 14-day execution calendar (with release windows)

Open questions for leadership (to resolve before live session):
- Which three company signals should we prioritize in the CEO framing? Candidate list: latency/cost, customer onboarding time, uptime/MTTR.
- Are there any hard resource constraints we should call out (e.g., hiring freezes for certain roles, reserved infra for dedicated customers)?
- Which customers (top 3) will we use as the pilot group for fallback flows?

Next steps (owner/action/due):
- Maya Chen: draft CEO memo and circulating deck (v0) — due 2025-06-06.
- Ethan Patel: assemble baseline KPI dashboards for 3 candidate signals — due 2025-06-05.
- Lara Gómez: create breakout facilitator guide and templates — due 2025-06-07.
- Tom Brooks: coordinate recording + live logistics (AV, Slack channels, voting tool) — due 2025-06-09.

Notes to self / informal:
- Keep live session under 50 minutes — record-only deep dives as followups.
- Use a typed Q&A flow to avoid slowing the session (staff vote top 5 questions).
- Consider a short post-session pulse survey (3 questions) to measure clarity and confidence.

Draft comms snippet for CEO memo (first paragraph idea):
"We’re convening a brief Tempo Reset to ensure we’re all marching toward the same measurable outcomes for the next 6–10 weeks. This is not a restructuring; it’s a short, collective calibration: pick the most important signals, commit to a few outcomes, and remove the noise that distracts execution."

If this looks good, I’ll turn the content into a shareable slide TL;DR + publish the manager templates in the team drive.

----
