title: Dedicated Burst Capacity — Customer Success Onboarding Checklist (GA + Beta)
preview:
Purpose
This checklist standardizes Customer Success onboarding for Dedicated customers enabling the Burst Capacity option. It’s designed to (a) validate config safely, (b) align customer expectations on behavior/limits/denials, (c) ensure billing + auditability are correct, and (d) establish clear escalation paths.

Scope
- Applies to Dedicated tenants with Burst enabled (beta or GA).
- Burst is regional (no cross-region bursting in initial release).
- Uses Redwood-managed shared burst pools segmented by region + hardware class.

Owners & routing
- CS owner (primary): assigned CSM / rollout lead
- Solutions Engineering (technical lead): Markus Klein / Simran Kaur (or delegate)
- SRE escalation: on-call SRE (Dedicated/Fleet) via incident process
- Platform escalation: Control Plane / Admission Controller on-call

Pre-onboarding intake (CS)
[ ] Confirm customer objectives and peak profile
    - What is the burst use case? (launch, batch job, weekly spike, seasonal)
    - Expected peak multiplier over baseline (e.g., 1.2x, 1.5x, 2x)
    - Duration/frequency of bursts (minutes vs hours; daily vs weekly)
    - Primary region(s) and hardware class requirement
[ ] Confirm the “why burst” vs “resize baseline reservation” decision
    - If peaks are sustained and predictable every day, consider resizing baseline.
    - If peaks are occasional/predictable windows, burst is usually appropriate.
[ ] Confirm customer readiness on client behavior
    - Customer understands burst is best-effort within contracted caps and fleet safety.
    - Customer agrees to implement recommended retry/backoff behavior on denials.
[ ] Identify customer stakeholders
    - Engineering owner (who will implement client-side behavior)
    - SRE/ops contact (who receives alerts internally)
    - Finance/billing contact
    - Security/compliance contact (if applicable)

Commercial + packaging verification (CS + Sales + Finance as needed)
[ ] Confirm customer’s Burst tier and contractual limits
    - Baseline reserved capacity (Dedicated GPUs / throughput entitlement)
    - Max burst ratio (cap relative to baseline)
    - Absolute cap (if applicable)
    - Cooldown window behavior (if contract references it)
    - Priority tier (if applicable)
[ ] Confirm billing line item + metering unit
    - What we bill for: burst usage beyond baseline (per contract), measured as burst GPU-seconds and/or burst tokens (depending on SKU)
    - Confirm invoice line item label and reporting cadence
[ ] Confirm SLA language and “what’s protected”
    - Baseline Dedicated SLOs remain the protected target.
    - Burst traffic may be denied/throttled to protect fleet + neighbors.
    - Customer understands denial responses and that burst availability is not guaranteed during extreme fleet conditions.

Technical enablement prerequisites (Solutions + Platform)
[ ] Confirm tenant is Dedicated and pinned to eligible region/hardware
    - Region: ____________________
    - Hardware class: ____________________ (e.g., H100, L40S)
[ ] Confirm the burst pool is GA/allow-listed for that region/hardware
    - If not available, capture timeline expectations and alternatives.
[ ] Confirm customer routing setup and model deployment constraints
    - Models in use: ____________________
    - Any pinned model versions / rollout policies that interact with routing/fallback

Control plane configuration checklist (Platform/SE with CS oversight)
Note: changes must be auditable; avoid “manual tweaks” without a paper trail.

[ ] Burst enablement flag set for tenant
    - Enabled = true
    - Effective timestamp recorded
[ ] Set tenant-level burst limits
    - max_burst_ratio: ____ (recommended starting point: conservative; tune upward after observing neighbor impact)
    - per-tenant cap (if applicable): ____
    - cooldown_window_seconds: ____
    - queue/backpressure behavior expectation communicated to customer
[ ] Priority tier (if applicable)
    - Tier: ____ (gold/silver/standard)
    - Confirm anti-starvation protections are active
[ ] Denial reason codes surfaced to customer-facing layer
    - Confirm routing/gateway returns stable denial reason codes and request IDs
    - Ensure customer knows which reason codes are “retryable” vs “stop and wait”
[ ] Audit logging validation
    - Verify audit events exist for: enable/disable, limit changes, admin override
    - Confirm retention meets customer requirements (esp. regulated customers)
[ ] RBAC/permissions check
    - Only authorized admins can modify burst config
    - Support tooling access follows least privilege

Validation & safety checks (must pass before customer uses burst in production)
[ ] Feature flags and kill switch posture
    - Confirm global kill switch behavior is understood by CS (what happens if triggered)
    - Confirm per-tenant disable can be executed quickly if needed
[ ] Metering correctness spot-check
    - Validate burst usage is not double-counted on retries (idempotency behavior)
    - Confirm reconciliation checks pass for a small test window
    - Identify internal dashboard/report used by CS to confirm numbers
[ ] Routing + admission controller integration test
    - Run a controlled burst test in staging or a limited prod window
    - Confirm deterministic behavior under normal telemetry conditions
    - Confirm graceful behavior under partial saturation (some admitted, some denied)
[ ] Neighbor protection / SLO gate awareness
    - Confirm SLO regression gating exists and is enabled for the region/hardware
    - Confirm CS knows what customer-visible symptom looks like if burst is gated (more denials)

Customer-facing enablement (CS + SE)
[ ] Share customer doc links and the “what to expect” summary
    - Explain baseline vs burst capacity in plain language
    - Explain that burst is capacity borrowing from managed pools with guardrails
[ ] Confirm customer implements correct retry strategies
    - On burst denial: exponential backoff + jitter
    - Respect Retry-After / cooldown hints if provided
    - Use idempotency keys for request retries when available/appropriate
    - Avoid immediate tight-loop retries (causes more denials + noisy dashboards)
[ ] Align on dashboards and alert thresholds
    - Who watches Redwood dashboards (CS/SRE) vs customer dashboards
    - What is “normal” denial % during busy times
    - What latency deltas should trigger investigation
[ ] Confirm customer understands denial modes
    - Immediate deny vs queued/backpressured behavior (as implemented for their integration)
    - 429s when burst cap reached are expected; distinguish from platform errors
    - Provide a short mapping: reason code → recommended action

Dashboards walkthrough (CS runs this live; record notes)
Goal: customer can self-diagnose: (a) “are we bursting?” (b) “why are we denied?” (c) “are we hurting neighbors / are we being protected?”

[ ] Show: Per-tenant burst usage
    - Burst usage over time, peak window view
    - % traffic served on baseline vs burst
[ ] Show: Denials + reason codes
    - Denials by reason code
    - Correlate denials to pool saturation, per-tenant cap, cooldown, global safety cap
[ ] Show: Pool saturation (region/hardware)
    - Pool utilization / queue depth
    - Telemetry lag indicator (important for interpreting transient spikes)
[ ] Show: Neighbor impact indicators (high-level)
    - p95 latency deltas for non-burst tenants / tail amplification indicators
    - Explain that these are guarded via SLO gates/circuit breakers
[ ] Show: Audit log access (if customer requests)
    - Where to find “burst config changed” events and by whom

Operational readiness (before flipping customer to production burst)
[ ] Identify the customer’s burst window and agree on a safe rollout plan
    - Phase 1: small burst test (time-boxed)
    - Phase 2: expand window / cap gradually
    - Phase 3: steady-state
[ ] Confirm escalation contacts and hours
    - Customer primary + backup
    - Redwood CS owner
    - Redwood SE owner
[ ] Confirm support pathways
    - What qualifies for: standard support ticket vs escalation vs incident
    - What artifacts customer should include (request IDs, timestamps, region, model, denial reason code, graphs)

Escalation playbook (CS quick reference)
Use this section when something goes wrong during onboarding or production use.

A) Customer reports “burst denials higher than expected”
- First checks:
  [ ] Verify per-tenant burst caps and cooldown in control plane
  [ ] Check pool saturation and global safety cap status
  [ ] Check denial reason code distribution (cap vs saturation vs cooldown)
  [ ] Check telemetry lag panel (was there lag during the window?)
- If needed:
  [ ] Engage Platform on-call to validate admission controller behavior
  [ ] If telemetry lag suspected, capture timestamps + panels; reference prior issues (telemetry smoothing/hotfix patterns)

B) Customer reports “429s / overload when burst cap reached”
- Confirm expected behavior and coach retry/backoff
- Validate they aren’t tight-loop retrying without jitter
- Confirm they’re using idempotency keys where applicable
- If 429s occur far below expected cap, treat as “denials higher than expected” and follow A)

C) Non-burst neighbor complains about latency during another tenant’s burst
- Treat as high priority (neighbor protection is a top-level goal)
- Steps:
  [ ] Check SLO regression gate status and any automated burst reduction
  [ ] Check tail latency deltas + time correlation with burst window
  [ ] Engage SRE on-call; reference overload mitigation runbook
  [ ] Be ready to recommend temporary cap reduction or disable burst for offender tenant while investigating

D) Billing / metering questions
- First checks:
  [ ] Pull burst usage report export for the billing period
  [ ] Cross-check against dashboards and known burst windows
  [ ] Confirm no double-counting (retry behavior; idempotency)
- If mismatch persists:
  [ ] Open a billing investigation ticket with timestamps and tenant ID
  [ ] Loop in Finance contact for reconciliation process

E) “Config change not reflected in Console”
- Validate expected propagation delay (cache/refresh intervals)
- Confirm audit log shows change applied
- If Console remains stale beyond SLA:
  [ ] Capture screenshots + timestamps
  [ ] Engage Console backend (control plane read APIs) and file ticket

Post-onboarding monitoring (first 72 hours)
[ ] Day 0 (launch day)
    - Monitor denial % and pool saturation during first burst window
    - Capture before/after metrics for customer: throughput increase, latency impact
[ ] Day 1–3
    - Confirm no neighbor SLO regressions correlated with bursts
    - Confirm metering numbers look reasonable and internally consistent
    - Review customer feedback on “predictability” and denial reason clarity
[ ] Tuning loop
    - If stable: consider increasing burst ratio/cap in small increments
    - If unstable: reduce cap, increase cooldown, or adjust customer retry pattern before increasing capacity requests

Customer handoff summary (CS sends after onboarding)
Include:
- Enabled: yes/no; effective date
- Config summary: region/hardware, max burst ratio, absolute cap, cooldown, priority tier
- Links: dashboards, docs, denial reason code explainer
- Support escalation: how to open a ticket; what artifacts to include
- Billing: what will appear on invoice; reporting cadence

Notes / fields to fill per customer
Customer name: ____________________
Tenant ID: ____________________
Region / hardware: ____________________
Burst limits (ratio/cap): ____________________
Cooldown: ____________________
Priority tier: ____________________
First burst window date/time: ____________________
CS owner: ____________________
SE owner: ____________________

Revision history
- 2025-03-06: Initial draft for beta onboarding
- 2025-05-20: Added metering/idempotency checks + reason-code mapping emphasis
- 2025-06-11: GA polish: clarified neighbor protection escalation and dashboard walkthrough order

  [ ] Engage SRE on-call; reference overload mitigation runbook
  [ ] Be ready to recommend temporary cap reduction or disable burst for offender tenant while investigating

D) Billing / metering questions
- First checks:
  [ ] Pull burst usage report export for the billing period
  [ ] Cross-check against dashboards and known burst windows
  [ ] Confirm no double-counting (retry behavior; idempotency)
- If mismatch persists:
  [ ] Open a billing investigation ticket with timestamps and tenant ID
  [ ] Loop in Finance contact for reconciliation process

E) “Config change not reflected in Console”
- Validate expected propagation delay (cache/refresh intervals)
- Confirm audit log shows change applied
- If Console remains stale beyond SLA:
  [ ] Capture screenshots + timestamps
  [ ] Engage Console backend (control plane read APIs) and file ticket

Post-onboarding monitoring (first 72 hours)
[ ] Day 0 (launch day)
    - Monitor denial % and pool saturation during first burst window
    - Capture before/after metrics for customer: throughput increase, latency impact
[ ] Day 1–3
    - Confirm no neighbor SLO regressions correlated with bursts
    - Confirm metering numbers look reasonable and internally consistent
    - Review customer feedback on “predictability” and denial reason clarity
[ ] Tuning loop
    - If stable: consider increasing burst ratio/cap in small increments
    - If unstable: reduce cap, increase cooldown, or adjust customer retry pattern before increasing capacity requests

Customer handoff summary (CS sends after onboarding)
Include:
- Enabled: yes/no; effective date
- Config summary: region/hardware, max burst ratio, absolute cap, cooldown, priority tier
- Links: dashboards, docs, denial reason code explainer
- Support escalation: how to open a ticket; what artifacts to include
- Billing: what will appear on invoice; reporting cadence

Notes / fields to fill per customer
Customer name: ____________________
Tenant ID: ____________________
Region / hardware: ____________________
Burst limits (ratio/cap): ____________________
Cooldown: ____________________
Priority tier: ____________________
First burst window date/time: ____________________
CS owner: ____________________
SE owner: ____________________

Revision history
- 2025-03-06: Initial draft for beta onboarding
- 2025-05-20: Added metering/idempotency checks + reason-code mapping emphasis
- 2025-06-11: GA polish: clarified neighbor protection escalation and dashboard walkthrough order

title: Partner SLA Exceptions Log — June 2025
preview:
Purpose: lightweight living log for partner-facing SLA exceptions (one-off breaches, tolerance requests, temporary mitigations). Intended for cross-functional visibility: eng, SRE, account teams, and partner ops. Use this as the single source for ad-hoc exception details until formal RFC or contract amendment is recorded.

Table (each row = one exception case):
Date | Partner | SLA metric | Breach? | Duration / window | Estimated affected traffic (min/day or pct) | Root cause summary | Immediate mitigation | Owner (R/T) | ETA to full resolution | Current status / notes
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2025-06-01 | LuminaSearch | 99.9% availability on hosted API (region: eu-west-1) | Yes | 45m (2025-06-01 03:10-03:55 UTC) | ~7% of Lumina requests (peak window) | Autoscaler failed to spin new GPU nodes due to transient API quota error (cloud provider rate-limit). Autoscale retry logic hit backoff. | Re-routed Lumina traffic to dedicated pool in another region; temporarily increased concurrency limits for their API key. | Liam O'Connor (eng-sre) | 2025-06-12 | Mitigation reduced user impact; partner requested credit. Follow-up: add telemetry for provider quota errors and fast-path fallback. Jira: JIRA-OPS-519.

2025-06-03 | HealthAI (pilot) | 95% p99 latency for embeddings generation | Yes (partial) | bursty spikes, cumulative 2h over day | ~1500 requests (pilot) | Model quantization pipeline regression after new runtime kernel selection; longer kv-cache miss penalty on sequence length >1024. | Rolled back kernel candidate for HealthAI model variant; activated per-client caching override. | Aisha Karim (applied-ml) | 2025-06-20 | Partner on pilot; internal postmortem planned. Recommended longer-term: add per-workload kernel-hinting for critical partners. Linked PR: github.com/redwood/kv-opt/pull/278.

2025-06-07 | EdgeCDN integrator | Error rate: 5xx spikes on embedding POSTs | Yes | 30m | ~2% of their overall traffic (low volume but high sensitivity) | Downstream parsing error in our request validation that failed on an uncommon content-type header; earlier change tightened header validation. | Quick fix: relax validation for that header for their org ID + issued feature flag. | Ethan Park (platform-eng) | 2025-06-09 | Partner requested temporary exception to strict validation until client SDK patched. Patch scheduled 2025-06-09.

2025-06-09 | RetailX (dedicated) | Throughput SLA: sustained 500qps | No (near miss) | short dips during nightly maintenance windows | <0.5% of daily traffic | Scheduled reserved capacity rebalancing overlapped with nightly DB compaction; autoscale headroom was temporarily lower. | Agreed maintenance window change and added buffer capacity for 00:00-03:00 UTC for next 30 days. | Priya Desai (product-ops) | 2025-07-01 | Signed statement of temporary tolerance for maintenance windows. Finance asked for formal credit calculation if recurrence.

Open actions / runbook items (short):
- Add \"partner-exception\" label to outgoing incident emails for automated routing to AMs. Owner: Aisha. Due: 2025-06-14.
- Instrument provider quota error metric (count + source) and create PagerDuty alert at 5/min in a 15m window. Owner: Liam. Due: 2025-06-18.
- Create small canary config to exercise new runtime kernel with >1024 seq length in prod-like infra before promoting. Owner: Applied ML. Due: 2025-06-25.
- Finance: draft credit calc template for partner-impact incidents (minutes * average rps * token-cost-sensitivity). Owner: Finance ops. Due: 2025-06-20.

Quick notes / principles for this tracker:
- This is intentionally lightweight: prefer bullet facts, one-line root cause, and a clear single owner for follow-up.
- Anything that becomes a recurring exception (>3 occurrences in 30d) should be escalated to formal SLA amendment or product change request.
- Keep partner communications copy in linked_artifacts (emails/AM notes).

Recent decisions captured here:
- Temporary acceptance of maintenance-window tolerances for certain Dedicated customers where we schedule DB/infra jobs.
- For pilot partners, allow temporary per-org kernel rollbacks if they prevent latency regressions while we investigate.

Open questions for leadership (brief):
- Threshold to auto-issue partner credits (minutes of unavailability vs severity) — propose starting with >30m outage OR >1% daily traffic impact. Feedback requested.
- Do we formally log accepted exceptions in the contract repository (legal) or keep ad-hoc here until a pattern emerges? Legal prefers written amendments; ops prefers one-off recorded here.

Last updated by: Priya Desai — 2025-06-10 09:12 UTC

If you edit: add a short update line and the date at the bottom.

title: Role Immersion 1-Week Hackathon + Eval Slate
preview:
Purpose
This doc outlines a compact, hands-on 1-week role immersion ‘hackathon’ used as both an advanced-stage interview and a new-hire rapid-onboard experiment. Goal: compress signal for senior ICs and senior engineers while giving candidates a real, scoped piece of work that mirrors the first 30 days on the team. It doubles as a starter sprint for hires who accept.

Why we run this
- Traditional take-home assignments can be noisy and disconnected. A short immersive week gives better signal about collaboration, prioritization, and code hygiene.
- Faster candidate experience (7 days vs multi-week loops).
- Doubles as realistic onboarding: accepted hires flip this week into their first deliverables.

Scope and target roles
- Best for senior software engineers, infra engineers, SRE candidates, and senior product/platform engineers. Not suited for junior grads or strictly PM-only roles.
- Scope each hackathon around a vertical slice: an API endpoint + infra changes + observability hook, or a small performance optimization with measurable metrics.

High-level timeline (candidate-facing)
- Day 0 (pre-week): offer candidate brief + environment access OR schedule remote kickoff call. Provide the repo skeleton, data, and a short spec (1–2 pages).
- Day 1: kickoff (30–45m) with hiring manager + mentor. Clarify scope and define success metrics.
- Day 2–4: development window (asynchronous). Mentor available for 2 scheduled office hours/day.
- Day 5: final polish and submit PR. Candidate prepares 10–15 min walk-through.
- Day 6: demo + live Q&A (30–45m) with hiring panel (engineer, manager, peer).
- Day 7: scoring, debrief, decision. If hire accepted, convert to week-1 onboarding sprint.

Deliverables (candidate)
- Working PR in our sample repo with tests and CI passing (or clear failing tests with explanation).
- Short README that states problem, approach, trade-offs, areas left unfinished, and next steps.
- 10–15 minute demonstration recording or live presentation.

Evaluation rubric (panel) — 100 point scale
1) Technical correctness and product fit (30 pts)
   - Does the solution meet the acceptance criteria? (0–15)
   - Appropriate trade-offs and constraints considered? (0–10)
   - Tests + CI + reproducible run steps? (0–5)

2) System design and reliability thinking (20 pts)
   - Thoughtful about failure modes, retries, SLOs, instrumentation? (0–10)
   - Approaches are production-appropriate (config, rollout, monitoring)? (0–10)

3) Code quality and maintainability (15 pts)
   - Readability, modularity, clear abstractions? (0–8)
   - Security and data handling (sanitization, secrets)? (0–4)
   - Appropriate comments and docstrings? (0–3)

4) Collaboration and communication (15 pts)
   - Responsiveness during week, clarity in PR description, structured demo? (0–8)
   - Able to receive feedback and iterate? (0–7)

5) Ownership and prioritization under constraints (10 pts)
   - Delivered the highest impact subset first, clear MVP? (0–10)

6) Culture & team fit signals (10 pts) — lightweight, not determinative
   - Curiosity, humility, alignment with engineering norms. (0–10)

Pass thresholds and gating
- Recommend hire if total >= 78 and no fatal security/data concerns.
- If 65–77, recommend an additional focused interview (pairing).
- Below 65 -> no hire. Hiring manager can escalate for edge cases.

Mentor rotation (ops)
- Mentor pool: 2 engineers from target team + 1 cross-team reviewer. Mentors should not be interviewers on the final panel to avoid bias.
- Office hours: 2x 30m slots/day (AM/PM) + asynchronous Slack availability. Mentor responsibilities: unblock, clarify spec, observe code health, log interactions.
- Mentor handoff: create a short mentor note template after each engagement: context, what was suggested, candidate responses, any persistent risks.

Hiring panel composition (day 6)
- Hiring manager (decision owner)
- Two senior engineers (one infra/stack-specific)
- One cross-functional stakeholder (PM or SRE depending on role)
- Interview coordinator/People Ops observer (optional)

Logistics checklist (people ops)
- Repo and template: ensure repo skeleton is up-to-date with CI, minimal dataset, and CLEAR README.
- Access: temporary token and sandbox env for candidate with 7-day expiry.
- Linear ticket and candidate folder: create IMMERSE-<candidate> ticket and link artifacts.
- Calendar invites: schedule kickoff, demo, and panel slots before week starts.
- Compensation guidance: if paid trial is required for candidates preferring paid assessments, flag to recruiting to issue stipend.

Communication templates (short)
- Kickoff email (candidate): short intro, expectations, timeline, mentor contacts, acceptance criteria, and env access link.
- Slack join message (private channel): welcome note + where mentors will hang out + troubleshooting tips.

Conversion to onboarding (if hire accepts)
- Flip PR to a draft onboarding branch and migrate remaining TODOs into a week-1 sprint board.
- Assign mentor and set 1:1 schedule for first 30 days.
- Retain the scoring notes as part of the onboarding docket — signal areas to focus (tests, infra knowledge, codebase areas).

Risk and mitigations
- Candidate ghosting mid-week: set clear deadlines and a single-day extension policy; if no response, conclude loop.
- Bias toward presentation skills: panel explicitly weights technical correctness higher and uses rubric to anchor.
- Time zone mismatch: allow asynchronous demo recording and schedule panel within 72h of submission.

Notes to hiring manager (practical)
- Keep scope intentionally small: it should be finishable as an MVP in 3–4 focused days. Anything that requires large infra changes should be stubbed or simulated.
- Encourage candidates to explain trade-offs in README instead of trying to perfect every optimization.

Experiment metrics (people ops)
- Time-to-offer (days) for candidates using the hackathon vs standard loop. Goal: reduce by 40%.
- Quality signal: % hires crossing probation with fewer performance notes (compare cohorts).
- Candidate NPS for interview experience (post-loop survey). Target: >=8/10.

Open TODOs (WIP)
- Finalize paid stipend policy for candidates in non-local timezones.
- Create a reusable repo template per hiring vertical (SRE, BE infra, Console).
- Author a short candidate-facing FAQ that explains IP, expectations, and data handling.

Quick reference templates (copy/paste)
- Kickoff subject: "Role Immersion Week — Kickoff and Details"
- Kickoff body: "Thanks for being flexible — attached is the repo, spec, and mentor schedule. We’ll do a 30m kickoff on DAY1 to align. Deliverables: working PR, README, 10–15m demo. If you prefer a stipend, reply and we’ll arrange it. — Aisha (People Ops)"

End of doc.

title: Quarter Cadence Calibration — alignment compact v0.1
preview:
Purpose: a compact working pack for the leadership sync + company sync (one-pager to build from). This is intentionally short — we want a tight set of decisions and asks that can be converted into the exec update slides and the town segment.

High-level desired outcomes (end of quarter):
- Clear product priority (hosted API performance improvements) with measurable SLOs and a single top-level owner.
- Two runway bets scoped (Dedicated capacity pricing experiment + Private onboarding pilot) with go/no-go criteria.
- Company alignment on hiring tempo (engineering vs. solutions) and a decision on a single headcount reallocation.

Why this doc exists (notes to self):
- We have lots of long decks; we need one short narrative + decision register for execs.
- All-hands should be short (30–35 minutes) and outcome-driven. No long feature demos; use pre-recorded demos in pre-read.

OKR hypotheses (v0.1) — keep these tight (objectives + candidate KRs):
Objective A: Reduce inference unit cost and tail latency for hosted API
- KR A1: 15% lower 95th percentile token latency for standard gen workloads (baseline: Jan 2025).
- KR A2: 20% reduction in per-token compute cost via batching & kernel tweaks on our top 3 models.
- KR A3: Deploy adaptive caching to 40% of production traffic with <1% error regressions.

Objective B: Secure first 3 enterprise Private deployments (compliance-first)
- KR B1: Close 2 paid pilots with SOC2+ controls by end of quarter.
- KR B2: Private onboarding time (from signed contract to q3-ready instance) <= 6 weeks for 80% of customers.
- KR B3: Documented and automated KMS integration pattern + runbook.

Objective C: Developer velocity & platform observability
- KR C1: Ship console route-level tracing + token-level cost view to 80% of internal dev teams (GA).
- KR C2: Reduce mean time to rollback for model releases to <10 minutes via automatic canary policies.
- KR C3: Add 30 representative evals and connect quality alerts to the alerting channel.

Notes on prioritization:
- If capacity/cost work (Obj A) competes with Private pilots (Obj B), tilt toward Obj A until the Dedicated pricing experiment completes gating metrics. Ops impact amplifies revenue across both hosted and dedicated.

Exec update wireframe (3 slides, 3 mins each):
1) Snapshot: where we are vs. previously committed SLOs + top signal (latency/cost trend chart) — owner: Head of Serving Runtime (Carlos).
2) Decisions needed: 3 items (listed below) with tradeoffs and recommended choices — owner: COO (Priya).
3) Ask & runway: hiring ask, budget contingency, go/no-go gates for two pilots — owner: CEO/COO.

All-hands (30–35 minute run): proposed timings and owners
- 0:00–0:03 Welcome & top-line metric (CEO) — one chart: ARR + NPS + latency trend.
- 0:03–0:10 Product signal + what we shipped (Pre-recorded demo link in pre-read; 2 min highlight live) — Head Product.
- 0:10–0:20 Priority decisions and what we need from the org (live panel: Eng, Product, GTM) — moderated by Morgan.
- 0:20–0:28 Customer spotlight (pre-recorded clip + 2 min Q&A) — CS lead.
- 0:28–0:35 Closing: three concrete team asks + where to find follow-ups (Slack threads + survey link).

Decision register (open items we must take at leadership sync):
1) Headcount shift: move 2 IC hires from infra to serving-runtime. Recommendation: approve (risk: backlog on infra).
2) Dedicated pricing experiment: discount path vs. throughput commitment model. Recommendation: throughput commit with initial 3-month pilot.
3) Private pilot acceptance criteria: security checklist completeness + 1 performance benchmark. Recommendation: pilot acceptance once checklist and benchmark pass.

Contingency triggers (traffic lights) — automated metrics to surface in ops channel
- Green: 95th percentile latency < target and costs trending down 10% month-over-month.
- Amber: latency within 10% of target OR costs flat; requires ops + product sync and capacity review.
- Red: latency > target OR costs increase > 10% — immediate freeze on new model rollouts and escalation to SRE + exec.

Pre-reads & assets to share (todo):
- Short pre-read PDF (1 page) with the three decisions and the two pilot scopes — must be in people's inbox 48h before sync. (Owner: Morgan).
- Demo clips uploaded to GDrive and linked in the pre-read (Owner: Product).
- One-pager for managers with suggested team-level talking points (Owner: People Ops).

Unresolved questions / friction points (call these out explicitly):
- We still lack a single canonical baseline for token-cost calculations across Dedicated and Hosted. Who owns reconciliation? (Recommend: finance + infra to align — ticket OPS-921).
- Dependencies for Private pilot (networking + legal) are still loosely scoped; need SLA template review.

Immediate next steps (this week):
- Finalize decision recs for the 3 items above (Priya / Morgan) — due: 2025-03-14.
- Draft 1-page pre-read and manager brief (Morgan) — due: 2025-03-15.
- Ask Eng leads to confirm owners for each KR (Carlos to collect) — due: 2025-03-17.

Meeting prep checklist (for owners):
- Slide: top-line metric chart (1 slide).
- Slide: decisions + recommended option (1 slide each decision).
- Slide: clear ask of the org + next meeting to deconflict (1 slide).
- Upload pre-recorded demos and check playback.

text:
Purpose: a compact working pack for the leadership sync + company sync (one-pager to build from). This is intentionally short — we want a tight set of decisions and asks that can be converted into the exec update slides and the town segment.

High-level desired outcomes (end of quarter):
- Clear product priority (hosted API performance improvements) with measurable SLOs and a single top-level owner.
- Two runway bets scoped (Dedicated capacity pricing experiment + Private onboarding pilot) with go/no-go criteria.
- Company alignment on hiring tempo (engineering vs. solutions) and a decision on a single headcount reallocation.

Why this doc exists (notes to self):
- We have lots of long decks; we need one short narrative + decision register for execs.
- All-hands should be short (30–35 minutes) and outcome-driven. No long feature demos; use pre-recorded demos in pre-read.

OKR hypotheses (v0.1) — keep these tight (objectives + candidate KRs):
Objective A: Reduce inference unit cost and tail latency for hosted API
- KR A1: 15% lower 95th percentile token latency for standard gen workloads (baseline: Jan 2025).
- KR A2: 20% reduction in per-token compute cost via batching & kernel tweaks on our top 3 models.
- KR A3: Deploy adaptive caching to 40% of production traffic with <1% error regressions.

Objective B: Secure first 3 enterprise Private deployments (compliance-first)
- KR B1: Close 2 paid pilots with SOC2+ controls by end of quarter.
- KR B2: Private onboarding time (from signed contract to q3-ready instance) <= 6 weeks for 80% of customers.
- KR B3: Documented and automated KMS integration pattern + runbook.

Objective C: Developer velocity & platform observability
- KR C1: Ship console route-level tracing + token-level cost view to 80% of internal dev teams (GA).
- KR C2: Reduce mean time to rollback for model releases to <10 minutes via automatic canary policies.
- KR C3: Add 30 representative evals and connect quality alerts to the alerting channel.

Notes on prioritization:
- If capacity/cost work (Obj A) competes with Private pilots (Obj B), tilt toward Obj A until the Dedicated pricing experiment completes gating metrics. Ops impact amplifies revenue across both hosted and dedicated.

Exec update wireframe (3 slides, 3 mins each):
1) Snapshot: where we are vs. previously committed SLOs + top signal (latency/cost trend chart) — owner: Head of Serving Runtime (Carlos).
2) Decisions needed: 3 items (listed below) with tradeoffs and recommended choices — owner: COO (Priya).
3) Ask & runway: hiring ask, budget contingency, go/no-go gates for two pilots — owner: CEO/COO.

All-hands (30–35 minute run): proposed timings and owners
- 0:00–0:03 Welcome & top-line metric (CEO) — one chart: ARR + NPS + latency trend.
- 0:03–0:10 Product signal + what we shipped (Pre-recorded demo link in pre-read; 2 min highlight live) — Head Product.
- 0:10–0:20 Priority decisions and what we need from the org (live panel: Eng, Product, GTM) — moderated by Morgan.
- 0:20–0:28 Customer spotlight (pre-recorded clip + 2 min Q&A) — CS lead.
- 0:28–0:35 Closing: three concrete team asks + where to find follow-ups (Slack threads + survey link).

Decision register (open items we must take at leadership sync):
1) Headcount shift: move 2 IC hires from infra to serving-runtime. Recommendation: approve (risk: backlog on infra).
2) Dedicated pricing experiment: discount path vs. throughput commitment model. Recommendation: throughput commit with initial 3-month pilot.
3) Private pilot acceptance criteria: security checklist completeness + 1 performance benchmark. Recommendation: pilot acceptance once checklist and benchmark pass.

Contingency triggers (traffic lights) — automated metrics to surface in ops channel
- Green: 95th percentile latency < target and costs trending down 10% month-over-month.
- Amber: latency within 10% of target OR costs flat; requires ops + product sync and capacity review.
- Red: latency > target OR costs increase > 10% — immediate freeze on new model rollouts and escalation to SRE + exec.

Pre-reads & assets to share (todo):
- Short pre-read PDF (1 page) with the three decisions and the two pilot scopes — must be in people's inbox 48h before sync. (Owner: Morgan).
- Demo clips uploaded to GDrive and linked in the pre-read (Owner: Product).
- One-pager for managers with suggested team-level talking points (Owner: People Ops).

Unresolved questions / friction points (call these out explicitly):
- We still lack a single canonical baseline for token-cost calculations across Dedicated and Hosted. Who owns reconciliation? (Recommend: finance + infra to align — ticket OPS-921).
- Dependencies for Private pilot (networking + legal) are still loosely scoped; need SLA template review.

Immediate next steps (this week):
- Finalize decision recs for the 3 items above (Priya / Morgan) — due: 2025-03-14.
- Draft 1-page pre-read and manager brief (Morgan) — due: 2025-03-15.
- Ask Eng leads to confirm owners for each KR (Carlos to collect) — due: 2025-03-17.

Meeting prep checklist (for owners):
- Slide: top-line metric chart (1 slide).
- Slide: decisions + recommended option (1 slide each decision).
- Slide: clear ask of the org + next meeting to deconflict (1 slide).
- Upload pre-recorded demos and check playback.

Slack threads & channels: #company-sync (central thread), #ops-decision (for the register), #eng-serving (for KR alignment).

Quick sibling notes (handy links and reminders):
- Use the short survey (link in pre-read) to collect top 3 team blockers post-sync.
- Keep the Q&A light — collect longer Qs in a follow-up doc and triage via team leads.

Tone + communication guidance:
- Be explicit about trade-offs; show the minimal data needed to make a call.
- Avoid feature-level granularity in the all-hands; route product detail to managers.
- Call out customer impact clearly for any decision that changes SLOs or pricing.

Draft messages (for copy/paste):
- Pre-read email subject: "Pre-read: Quarter Cadence Calibration (15 min) — decisions & asks"
- Body (short): "Please read the attached one-pager before our leadership sync. We will decide on 3 items and share the summary at the company sync. If you are an owner for a KR, ping Carlos with your estimate. — Morgan"

- Metric snapshot charts: Carlos (due 2025-03-14)

title: Account advancement campaign skeleton and pilot playbook
preview:
Purpose:
Quick, reusable campaign skeleton to convert mid-enterprise customers to Dedicated capacity, Private deployments, and premium SLA add-ons. Target use cases: predictable throughput, data isolation, compliance requirements, and cost-stability for heavy token-volume apps. This is a lightweight, test-first playbook designed to be run as a 6–8 week pilot before a full commercial offer.

High-level outcome goals (pilot-level):
- Business: convert one pilot into a paid Dedicated/Private contract or clear path to decision within 8 weeks.
- Technical: validate performance SLOs (P95 latency < target) and migration blockers (network, auth, datastore access).
- Commercial: agree on SLAs and pricing band; internal win criteria = expected MRR uplift >= $X/month (team to fill).

Segmentation and candidate signals (how we pick targets):
- Signals: sustained token consumption growth >= 30% month-over-month, repeated throttling/errors on hosted tiers, security/compliance requests logged in support tickets, roadmap mention of high-throughput features.
- Ideal candidate profile: platform/infra teams at mid-enterprise or above; ML/Inference-heavy product; legal or security team already engaged.

Campaign phases and timeline (6–8 weeks):
1) Discovery & qualification (Week 0–1):
   - Quick health check: usage trends, errors, concurrency, KV cache hit rate.
   - Stakeholder map: identify ML eng, infra, security, procurement.
   - Decide pilot scope: Dedicated capacity vs Private deployment vs SLA-only trial.
2) Proposal & pilot design (Week 1–2):
   - Draft pilot SOW (1–2 pages): objectives, success metrics, timeline, responsibilities, data access.
   - Technical plan: ingress/egress, VPC/networking, billing/usage visibility, KV cache migration plan.
   - Commercial anchor: provisional pricing band and optional add-ons (monitoring retention, dedicated support).
3) Pilot execution (Week 2–6):
   - Kickoff call, technical onboarding, 2-week steady-state run, weekly check-ins.
   - Ops checklist: RBAC, KMS/HSM keys, audit log hookup, trace sampling.
   - Measurement: token cost per request, P50/P95 latency, error rate, throughput, cost delta vs hosted.
4) Review & commercial close (Week 6–8):
   - QBR-style review with exec summary, metric dashboard, risks, recommended next steps.
   - Negotiation: SLA terms, duration, payment cadence.
   - Contract handoff to sales/legal; run implementation readiness checklist for full rollout.

Outreach sequences (multi-channel) — sample cadences: 
- Day 0: Intro email to primary CSM + customer engineering lead (subject lines below).
- Day 3: Slack ping to primary engineering contact + brief technical note with a proposed pilot window.
- Day 7: Follow-up with short one-pager and ask to confirm stakeholders for kickoff.
- Week 2: Invite to pilot kickoff meeting.
- Weekly: Progress notes + data snapshots.

Sample email subjects and snippet templates (to A/B test):
- Subject A: "Pilot proposal: predictable LLM throughput + cost guardrails for <Customer>"
  Snippet: "Quick note—saw your spike in inference usage last month. We have a 6-week pilot we can stand up on Dedicated capacity to validate latency and cost at scale. Want to run this with your infra team?"
- Subject B: "SLA + private deployment options for data-sensitive inference workloads"
  Snippet: "If compliance or data residency is blocking a rollout, we can draft a Private deployment SOW and run a short validation pilot. Ideal for SOC2/ISO-concerned teams."

Technical gating checklist (pilot):
- Access: service account + minimal prod-like dataset or simulator.
- Network: VPC peering/VPN validated, IP allowlists confirmed.
- Security: KMS integration tested, audit log stream validated.
- Model parity: baseline model variant confirmed and test prompt set uploaded.
- Observability: tracing + per-token cost telemetry enabled, dashboard shared.

Pilot success metrics (examples to include on QBR slide):
- Delivery: percent of requests hitting Dedicated nodes vs fallback.
- Performance: P50/P95 latency improvement vs baseline, tail latency reduction.
- Reliability: incidence of throttling/errors during pilot.
- Economics: per-1M-token unit cost, delta to hosted baseline, projected monthly cost at steady state.
- Business impact: feature velocity enabled, estimated revenue/profit improvement or ops time saved.

Risk and mitigation table (simple bullets):
- Risk: Network misconfiguration delays onboarding. Mitigation: run a network dry-run 48 hours before pilot.
- Risk: Unexpected token spend during pilot. Mitigation: soft quota + alerting and pre-defined throttle policy.
- Risk: Security review slows procurement. Mitigation: parallel legal pack, red-team checklist, SOC2 artifact pointers.

QBR slide outline (one-slide-per-bullet, 6–8 slides total):
- Slide 1: Executive summary + ask (decision needed).
- Slide 2: Pilot scope and timeline.
- Slide 3: Usage and performance vs baseline.
- Slide 4: Risk & mitigation and outstanding technical items.
- Slide 5: Cost comparison and pricing recommendation.
- Slide 6: Commercial terms & SLA options.
- Slide 7: Next steps and acceptance criteria for rollout.

Commercial packaging suggestions (to offer after successful pilot):
- Bronze: Dedicated burst capacity + standard SLA (lower price).
- Silver: Reserved Dedicated capacity + 99.9% availability SLA + quarterly architecture review.
- Gold: Private deployment (VPC) + 99.95% SLA + on-call support + extended observability retention.
- Add-ons: extended trace retention, premium support hours, custom quantization/placement consulting.

Tracking sheet columns (minimal columns for tracking progress):
- Customer, CSM owner, Eng owner, Candidate signal, Pilot type, Pilot start, Pilot end, Current status, Key metrics collected, Risk flags, Next action, Commercial owner, Estimated ARR uplift.

Decision rubric (pilot -> offer):
- Green: Meets performance and reliability targets + commercial alignment -> propose Silver/Gold.
- Amber: Partial wins, outstanding security/legal items -> extend trial or targeted remediation.
- Red: Major blockers (network/security/price) -> de-prioritize or propose alternate architecture.

Sales/legal prep checklist (to hand to AE & legal once pilot recommendation made):
- Draft Master Services Agreement template ready with selected SLA.
- Confirmation of billing model (commit vs usage).
- CPRA/data transfer addendum if needed.
- Procurement timeline estimate and approver list.

Notes to self / open questions (work-in-progress):
- Need a small ROI calculator for CSMs to plug in token volumes and get projected MRR uplift.
- Which internal pricing bands are we comfortable offering as an "introductory pilot" discount? Ops to confirm.
- Can we pre-authorize a small burst capacity pool so pilots don’t need long provisioning windows? Follow up with infra.

Action items (next steps for this doc):
- [ ] Share with Solutions Eng for technical sanity-check (Ava)
- [ ] Build pilot SOW template in Confluence (Diego)
- [ ] Create a one-slide QBR template for CSM presentation (Priya)
- [ ] Wire a simple ROI sheet in Sheets and link into tracking template

References and links:
- Confluence: Account expansion policy (internal)
- Jira: CSUP-412 pilot tracking epic
- Slack: #cs-dedicated-upgrades (used for async updates)

Tone: intentionally short, playbook-first, plug-and-play. Keep it minimal so CSMs can copy and run without heavy approvals. This is a living doc — capture pilot learnings and iterate.

title: Peak quarter prep and Townloop brief v0-1
preview:
Purpose:
This note is the working brief for the Peak Quarter prep window. It stitches together: a concise exec-update for FY Q2 planning, a fresh OKR draft to surface at the townloop (pre-all-hands cross-team sync), and the all-hands run-of-show and preflight checklist. Use this as the single source for leads preparing slides, demos, and the CEO update.

Context and goals (why now):
- We need a short, crisp narrative to align the company on where to concentrate engineering and GTM energy for the upcoming quarter.
- Townloop: a 60-minute cross-functional rehearsal focused on alignment, risk escalation, and decision signals before the public all-hands.
- All-hands: a 45–60 minute townhall with exec update, a product highlight demo, and Q&A. The goal is clarity on priorities, commitments, and measurable outcomes.

Key timeline (draft):
- Week -3 (March 17): Distributed deadline for slide decks + one-pager summaries from each org lead.
- Week -2 (March 24): Townloop rehearsal (60m). Execs, PM leads, eng leads, ops.
- Week -1 (March 31): Final slide freeze + speaker rehearsal. Post-deck lightning review w/ comms.
- Day 0 (April 7): Company all-hands (11:00–12:00 PT). Follow-up survey closes 48h after.

Townloop agenda (60m):
- 0–5m: Opening (Ava) — framing + expected outcomes for the all-hands.
- 5–20m: Exec update highlights (CEO/Head of Product) — 2 slides each, 3 minute limit.
- 20–35m: OKR draft walkthrough & ask for decisions (Product + Eng) — surface tradeoffs.
- 35–45m: Risks & mitigations (SRE/Infra/Product Ops) — top 5 risks.
- 45–55m: Comms & slide flow check (Comms lead) — confirm final order and backup slides.
- 55–60m: Fast decisions & actions (Document owners assigned).

All-hands run-of-show (45–60m):
- 0–3m: Welcome + housekeeping (HR/Comms).
- 3–12m: CEO narrative: The mission, market context, and 3 company-level asks. (Use 3 bullets, each tied to a measurable signal.)
- 12–25m: Product highlight demo (PM + Eng) — production demo of a Hosted API latency win + short customer quote.
- 25–35m: OKR snapshot (40–60s per objective) — 3 company-level OKRs with top KRs.
- 35–50m: People & culture (hires, promotions, shoutouts).
- 50–60m: Live Q&A (moderated, up to 10 questions).

Draft Company-level OKRs (Q2 2025) — for townloop decisioning:
Objective A: Make inference predictable and economical for tier-1 production apps.
- KR A1: Achieve 99.5% of production requests within 120ms tail-latency on Redwood Hosted (p95 target).
- KR A2: Reduce median per-token cost for standard text-gen workloads by 18% vs. Q1 baseline via batching + quantization profiles.
- KR A3: Customer impact: onboard 6 customers to Redwood Dedicated pilot with SLA commitments.

Objective B: Ship the Redwood Private automated installer and first on-prem customer reference.
- KR B1: Complete private installer beta with two supported OS images and KMS integration.
- KR B2: Deliver an on-prem reference deployment and run three enterprise acceptance tests end-to-end.
- KR B3: Publish compliance playbook (SOC2-lite checklist for installer customers).

Objective C: Improve visibility and dev experience for platform users.
- KR C1: Ship token-level cost dashboard and per-route perf traces to GA (>=80% of paid accounts).
- KR C2: Reduce mean time to troubleshoot (MTTT) by 25% via instrumented traces and runbooks.

Notes on metrics & measurement:
- Baselines: use the product analytics dashboards and billing data from Feb 2025. Each KR must include owner and data source.
- Measurement cadence: weekly OKR dashboard sync; monthly exec review.

Open questions for townloop (decision/assignment needed):
- Which of the three objectives should be prioritized for company PR at the all-hands? (Recommend A + C.)
- Are the latency and cost KR targets achievable without additional hardware commitments for Dedicated? Eng leads to confirm by Townloop.
- Who signs off on the customer quote used in the product demo? CS + legal to confirm.

Risks and mitigation (top 5):
1) Dedicated capacity shortages during demo window — mitigation: pre-record demo + automatic fallback to Hosted.
2) Metrics volatility from billing pipeline — mitigation: freeze KR definitions; use last 30-day rolling average.
3) On-prem installer regressions — mitigation: limit first installs to sandboxed customer and run ops runbook.
4) All-hands slide overrun — mitigation: enforce strict speaker timers; one backup slide deck for extended Q&A.
5) Survey response bias — mitigation: incentivize feedback and allow anonymous submissions.

Preflight checklist (owners in parentheses):
- Slides received and reviewed by comms (Comms: Leah).
- Demo runbook + recording ready (Eng: Jonah).
- OKR owners assigned and KRs validated with data sources (Product Ops: Samantha).
- Legal/PR clearance for customer quote (Legal: Marco).
- Townloop attendees confirmed and calendar invites locked (Ava).

Communication artifacts (deliverables):
- Exec one-pager for execs + board (1 page).
- All-hands slides (master deck + backup).
- Short blog post skeleton for product highlight post (publishable after approval).
- Post-event survey link + summary template.

Post-all-hands follow-up plan:
- 48h: close survey; leads to summarize top feedback and any new asks.
- 7 days: publish final OKR sheet and assign action items.
- 14 days: cross-team SCRUM of owners to begin KR sprint planning.

Quick comms draft (CEO opener):
"We built a playbook last quarter for predictable serving — in Q2 we double down on measurable economics and reliability. Short-term, that means hitting latency targets while reducing cost per token; medium-term, it means giving customers a private option that is easy to deploy and safe to run. Today you’ll hear where we are, the risks we’re watching, and the concrete asks for each team.

Ready-made decisions for townloop: 
- Confirm top-priority objective (A/C recommended).
- Lock latency and cost targets or widen tolerances by +10% if infra needs change.
- Approve demo backup plan and legal sign-off path for customer quote.

Action items (immediate):
- Jonah: upload demo runbook + S3 link by 2025-03-10.
- Samantha: publish OKR baseline spreadsheet (data sources annotated) by 2025-03-12.
- Leah: first pass on slides due 2025-03-14.
- Marco (Legal): confirm customer quote clearance by 2025-03-20.

Appendix: Helpful links and references:
- Product roadmap PR: https://github.com/redwood/roadmap/pull/412
- OKR template: https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/OKR-TEMPLATE
- Hosting SLA draft: JIRA-7890

Notes to self / scratchpad (informal):
- Consider adding a short customer video clip instead of live demo if infra flaky.
- Keep OKR language plain; execs should be able to read KRs in <=10s each.
- Use the townloop to force small binary decisions; avoid open-ended debates there.

End of brief. Please add comments here with edits, owners, and any blocker flags.
text:
Purpose:
This note is the working brief for the Peak Quarter prep window. It stitches together: a concise exec-update for FY Q2 planning, a fresh OKR draft to surface at the townloop (pre-all-hands cross-team sync), and the all-hands run-of-show and preflight checklist. Use this as the single source for leads preparing slides, demos, and the CEO update.

Context and goals (why now):
- We need a short, crisp narrative to align the company on where to concentrate engineering and GTM energy for the upcoming quarter.
- Townloop: a 60-minute cross-functional rehearsal focused on alignment, risk escalation, and decision signals before the public all-hands.
- All-hands: a 45–60 minute townhall with exec update, a product highlight demo, and Q&A. The goal is clarity on priorities, commitments, and measurable outcomes.

Key timeline (draft):
- Week -3 (March 17): Distributed deadline for slide decks + one-pager summaries from each org lead.
- Week -2 (March 24): Townloop rehearsal (60m). Execs, PM leads, eng leads, ops.
- Week -1 (March 31): Final slide freeze + speaker rehearsal. Post-deck lightning review w/ comms.
- Day 0 (April 7): Company all-hands (11:00–12:00 PT). Follow-up survey closes 48h after.

Townloop agenda (60m):
- 0–5m: Opening (Ava) — framing + expected outcomes for the all-hands.
- 5–20m: Exec update highlights (CEO/Head of Product) — 2 slides each, 3 minute limit.
- 20–35m: OKR draft walkthrough & ask for decisions (Product + Eng) — surface tradeoffs.
- 35–45m: Risks & mitigations (SRE/Infra/Product Ops) — top 5 risks.
- 45–55m: Comms & slide flow check (Comms lead) — confirm final order and backup slides.
- 55–60m: Fast decisions & actions (Document owners assigned).

All-hands run-of-show (45–60m):
- 0–3m: Welcome + housekeeping (HR/Comms).
- 3–12m: CEO narrative: The mission, market context, and 3 company-level asks. (Use 3 bullets, each tied to a measurable signal.)
- 12–25m: Product highlight demo (PM + Eng) — production demo of a Hosted API latency win + short customer quote.
- 25–35m: OKR snapshot (40–60s per objective) — 3 company-level OKRs with top KRs.
- 35–50m: People & culture (hires, promotions, shoutouts).
- 50–60m: Live Q&A (moderated, up to 10 questions).

Draft Company-level OKRs (Q2 2025) — for townloop decisioning:
Objective A: Make inference predictable and economical for tier-1 production apps.
- KR A1: Achieve 99.5% of production requests within 120ms tail-latency on Redwood Hosted (p95 target).
- KR A2: Reduce median per-token cost for standard text-gen workloads by 18% vs. Q1 baseline via batching + quantization profiles.
- KR A3: Customer impact: onboard 6 customers to Redwood Dedicated pilot with SLA commitments.

Objective B: Ship the Redwood Private automated installer and first on-prem customer reference.
- KR B1: Complete private installer beta with two supported OS images and KMS integration.
- KR B2: Deliver an on-prem reference deployment and run three enterprise acceptance tests end-to-end.
- KR B3: Publish compliance playbook (SOC2-lite checklist for installer customers).

Objective C: Improve visibility and dev experience for platform users.
- KR C1: Ship token-level cost dashboard and per-route perf traces to GA (>=80% of paid accounts).
- KR C2: Reduce mean time to troubleshoot (MTTT) by 25% via instrumented traces and runbooks.

Notes on metrics & measurement:
- Baselines: use the product analytics dashboards and billing data from Feb 2025. Each KR must include owner and data source.
- Measurement cadence: weekly OKR dashboard sync; monthly exec review.

Open questions for townloop (decision/assignment needed):
- Which of the three objectives should be prioritized for company PR at the all-hands? (Recommend A + C.)
- Are the latency and cost KR targets achievable without additional hardware commitments for Dedicated? Eng leads to confirm by Townloop.
- Who signs off on the customer quote used in the product demo? CS + legal to confirm.

Risks and mitigation (top 5):
1) Dedicated capacity shortages during demo window — mitigation: pre-record demo + automatic fallback to Hosted.
2) Metrics volatility from billing pipeline — mitigation: freeze KR definitions; use last 30-day rolling average.
3) On-prem installer regressions — mitigation: limit first installs to sandboxed customer and run ops runbook.
4) All-hands slide overrun — mitigation: enforce strict speaker timers; one backup slide deck for extended Q&A.
5) Survey response bias — mitigation: incentivize feedback and allow anonymous submissions.

Preflight checklist (owners in parentheses):
- Slides received and reviewed by comms (Comms: Leah).
- Demo runbook + recording ready (Eng: Jonah).
- OKR owners assigned and KRs validated with data sources (Product Ops: Samantha).
- Legal/PR clearance for customer quote (Legal: Marco).
- Townloop attendees confirmed and calendar invites locked (Ava).

Communication artifacts (deliverables):
- Exec one-pager for execs + board (1 page).
- All-hands slides (master deck + backup).
- Short blog post skeleton for product highlight post (publishable after approval).
- Post-event survey link + summary template.

Post-all-hands follow-up plan:
- 48h: close survey; leads to summarize top feedback and any new asks.
- 7 days: publish final OKR sheet and assign action items.
- 14 days: cross-team SCRUM of owners to begin KR sprint planning.

Quick comms draft (CEO opener):
"We built a playbook last quarter for predictable serving — in Q2 we double down on measurable economics and reliability. Short-term, that means hitting latency targets while reducing cost per token; medium-term, it means giving customers a private option that is easy to deploy and safe to run. Today you’ll hear where we are, the risks we’re watching, and the concrete asks for each team.

Ready-made decisions for townloop: 
- Confirm top-priority objective (A/C recommended).
- Lock latency and cost targets or widen tolerances by +10% if infra needs change.
- Approve demo backup plan and legal sign-off path for customer quote.

Action items (immediate):
- Jonah: upload demo runbook + S3 link by 2025-03-10.
- Samantha: publish OKR baseline spreadsheet (data sources annotated) by 2025-03-12.
- Leah: first pass on slides due 2025-03-14.
- Marco (Legal): confirm customer quote clearance by 2025-03-20.

Appendix: Helpful links and references:
- Product roadmap PR: https://github.com/redwood/roadmap/pull/412
- OKR template: https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/OKR-TEMPLATE
- Hosting SLA draft: JIRA-7890

Notes to self / scratchpad (informal):
- Consider adding a short customer video clip instead of live demo if infra flaky.
- Keep OKR language plain; execs should be able to read KRs in <=10s each.
- Use the townloop to force small binary decisions; avoid open-ended debates there.

End of brief. Please add comments here with edits, owners, and any blocker flags.

title: hybrid-handoff-playbook-10-90-day-activation-async
preview:
Purpose:
This playbook provides a compact, asynchronous-first onboarding and handoff template for hires that will operate in a hybrid model (remote + periodic in-office). It is optimized for fast ramp and transfer of domain knowledge for contract-to-perm and cross-functional lateral moves. The document is intentionally checklist-driven so hiring managers, mentors, and new hires can run a 10-day rapid activation followed by a structured 30–90 day growth ladder with clear success signals.

Success criteria (example):
- New hire can independently complete the basic support flow / ship a small scoped change / run the weekly oncall runbook by day 30.
- Positive qualitative mentor feedback by week 6.
- Role-readiness score >= 80% on the role-competency checklist by day 90.

Quick notes on behavior and assumptions:
- Async-first: most prep, knowledge transfer, and checks are written or recorded; synchronous touchpoints are minimized and intentionally short.
- Manager is accountable for role outcomes, mentor is accountable for daily ramp and access.
- Security and tool-access must be completed in preboarding to avoid day-1 wait states.

Preboarding (to complete before day 0)
- HR: offer packet signed, benefits enrollment links sent, contractor-to-perm conversion note updated.
- IT: laptop order, MDM enrolment, baseline apps installed (Slack, Okta, VSCode, VPN), access to repo read-only.
- Security: two-step auth enabled, training link sent.
- Manager: first-week calendar template and short intro notes prepared; expectations doc with 3-month goals created and linked.
- Mentor assigned and introduced over Slack with a short 30s intro pin message.

Day 0 checklist (first 24 hours)
- ASYNC items (primary):
  - Watch: \"Welcome to Redwood Inference\" (10m recorded intro by People Ops).
  - Read: team charter, role expectations doc, 3-month impact plan (short).
  - Complete: basic security training module (auto-verified).
  - Confirm: hardware and network health with IT via ticket.
- SYNC items (optional short):
  - 30m welcome call with manager (intro + logistics).
  - 15m mentor sync (quick plan for days 1–3).

Days 1–10: Rapid activation sprint (goal: context + first small ship/assignment)
- Day 1–2:
  - Setup dev env using written guide (mentored pair once only if blocked).
  - Run smoke tests and open a ticket documenting environment steps and any blockers.
  - Short async intro post in team channel: 5 bullet background + ask for 1 resource recommendation.
- Day 3–5:
  - Complete a guided code-read / architecture walkthrough doc and record 20m follow-up Q&A (mentor leads but asynchronous comments welcome).
  - Pick a micro-ticket (<80pt) from the starter backlog and propose an approach in PR template.
- Day 6–10:
  - Ship the micro-ticket with mentor review.
  - Run through the oncall triage runbook at least once in a sandbox (checklist verification).
  - Submit a 1-page retro: what worked in preboard, blockers, and 3 proposed changes to onboarding.

30-day checklist (solidify competence)
- Deliverable: complete a scoped feature or cross-team bugfix end-to-end with documentation.
- Manager check: 1:1 focused on expectations vs reality; update 3-month plan.
- Mentor check: run mentor scorecard (see below) and log time spent.
- Security & compliance: verification of training and access logs.

60-day checklist (expand impact)
- Deliverable: own a recurring deliverable (weekly report, small integration, or ticket queue).
- Influence: present a 10-minute knowledge share in team meeting (async recording accepted).
- Career conversation: manager provides clear promotion/role growth pathways and a list of skills to prioritize.

90-day checklist (role-readiness handoff)
- Deliverable: demonstrate ability to independently plan & ship a medium-scoped change; QA pass and stakeholder sign-off.
- Role-readiness assessment: manager + mentor + peer reviewer complete role-competency checklist.
- Next steps: assign ownership of recurring tasks; schedule a 6-month growth plan follow-up.

Mentor responsibilities (recommended cadence)
- Week 0–2: daily lightweight check-ins (async notes or 15m drop-in).
- Week 3–6: alternate day check-ins, review PRs within 24h.
- Week 7–12: weekly check-ins and handoff of responsibilities.
- At week 6 & 12: submit a short mentor evaluation note with examples and blockers.

Manager responsibilities
- Prepare 3-month measurable outcomes and publish them before day 0.
- Maintain a short weekly sync (30m) at least until week 6.
- Own performance conversations and escalation to People Ops when ramp stalls.

Evaluation artifacts and signals
- Role-competency checklist (linked artifact) for reviewer inputs.
- PR quality rubric: tests, docs, rollout plan, monitoring hooks.
- Mentor scorecard: pedagogy, responsiveness, transfer completeness.
- Onboarding retro: new-hire submission consolidated into People Ops intake.

Interview rubric notes (for contractors converting to perm)
- Use a lightweight bar for conversion: current delivery, cross-team communication, reliability, and ramp velocity.
- Recommend short async evidence pack: 3 PRs, 2 peer notes, 1 mentor evaluation.

Templates (to copy into JIRA/Confluence)
- 10-day activation tasks (checklist with owners).
- PR template for first 3 tickets (include linked onboarding docs).
- Mentor evaluation form (yes/no + example evidence).
- Role-competency checklist (scoring matrix).

Common failure modes and mitigations
- Blocker: late access to critical systems -> Mitigation: enforce "zero day-1 wait" policy in preboarding and weekly audit.
- Blocker: no mentor bandwidth -> Mitigation: fallback mentor pool and rotate 2nd-opinion reviewers.
- Blocker: low cross-team context -> Mitigation: require two short stakeholder intro calls scheduled by day 7.

Small experiments to run (people-ops runbook ideas)
- Asynchronous office hours (2x/week) where mentors drop short videos answering common onboarding Qs.
- Pair-swap week: new hires pair with a different engineer for 2hrs to broaden context.
- Onboarding retro leaderboard: collect suggested changes and surface monthly to eng managers.

Open questions / TODOs (work-in-progress)
- Should the mentor scorecard be mandatory for contractor-to-perm conversions? (pro/con discussion note to be added).
- Can we automate onboarding gate checks with a single webhook from HR system into JIRA? (technical spec request).

Appendix: quick links and checkers
- Preboard checklist (Confluence): confluence:PeopleOps/Hybrid-Preboard
- Role-competency template (Confluence): confluence:PeopleOps/Role-Competency-Template
- Starter backlog label: repo:starter-backlog/onboarding

If you use this playbook: please run the 10-day activation exactly once on each new hire and submit the onboarding retro by day 15. This doc is intended to be copied into team-level playbooks and iterated; add your micro-experiments and tag People Ops for monthly synthesis.

title: Three-Week Focus Sprint Orchestrator — Q2 prep (draft)
preview:
Purpose

This doc is a working orchestrator for a concentrated, three-week company-wide alignment sprint to finalize OKRs, prep an abbreviated all-staff update, and land escalation/resourcing decisions for Q2. It's intentionally light-weight — think scrappy facilitation + a few mandatory prep artifacts — so we can close outstanding choices and ship a tight comms plan.

Summary / What success looks like
- Finalized company-level OKRs with owners and measurable KRs (published).
- Leadership has a 10-minute executive update deck and 3 prepared talking points each.
- All-staff session (60 min) runs with an interactive prioritization exercise; follow-up assignments assigned within 72 hrs.
- Critical resourcing decisions (Dedicated capacity commits, CS pilot approvals, hiring priority for Infra SRE) made and committed in JIRA.

High-level schedule (three week sprint)
Week 0 (pre-sprint, 3 business days before kickoff):
- Prep: owners fill the OKR one-pager template (1 side) and attach quantitative baselines.
- Slack thread: #clarity-week — list open decisions (tag: decision-required).
- Leadership pre-brief (30m): reviewers highlight 2 red-lines per objective.

Week 1 (alignment & draft closure):
- Day 1: Cross-functional review table (60m) — owners present OKRs, constraints.
- Day 2: Deep-dive pre-reads circulated; two asynchronous votes (impact, confidence).
- Day 3: Decision session (90m) — lock wording, owners, and measurement approach.

Week 2 (resourcing & rollouts):
- Day 1: Resource alignment (finance/people/infra) — commit headcount/credits.
- Day 2: Product/Infra finalize launch/runbooks and fallback plans.
- Day 3: Draft exec update deck and run of show for All-Staff.

Week 3 (all-staff + handoff):
- Day 1: Final leadership rehearsal (45m).
- Day 2: All-staff ceremony (60m) + breakouts (30m) for team-level action mapping.
- Day 3: Publish OKRs, exec notes, and PM action items to Confluence + JIRA tickets created.

Draft company-level OKRs (starting point; each objective needs 1–2 owners and 3–4 measurable KRs)
Objective A — Make inference predictable and cheaper for production customers
- KR1: Reduce median hosted API token cost by 18% quarter-over-quarter (measurement: $/1k tokens). (owner: Ravi P.)
- KR2: Achieve 99.95% availability on Redwood Dedicated for >95% of customer traffic slots. (owner: SRE lead)
- KR3: Deliver 2 model quantization profiles in Optimize recommendations with validated regressions <2% on our eval suite. (owner: Applied ML)

Objective B — Improve developer onboarding and time-to-first-success
- KR1: Cut median onboarding time to first successful call from sign-up to working example from 72h -> 24h. (owner: DevX)
- KR2: Publish 6 new quickstart templates (including private-deploy) and measure adoption rate >= 30% within 45 days. (owner: Developer Experience)
- KR3: Lower developer-reported friction score in NPS mini-survey from 3.6 -> 4.2. (owner: Product)

Objective C — Enterprise readiness and compliance acceleration
- KR1: Complete SOC2 gap list with remediation owners and close 80% of items. (owner: Security & Compliance)
- KR2: Ship private-deploy turnkey installer v0.2 and validate in 2 customer pilots. (owner: Private Deployments)
- KR3: Publish formal SLA matrix with latency targets and pricing for Dedicated. (owner: Product & Finance)

Notes on metrics: baseline numbers are TBD — engineering leads/sdrs to add current values when they fill the one-pager. If baseline missing, tag as BLOCKER in #clarity-week.

All-Staff run-of-show (60 min) — condensed choreography
- 0:00–0:05 Welcome + context (CEO) — 3 lines: why this quarter matters, 1 quick win callout, the expectation of decisions.
- 0:05–0:15 Executive highlights (x3 leaders, 3 min each) — focus: risks, where we need cross-team help, and a single ask.
- 0:15–0:30 Interactive prioritization poll (Menti/Slido) — company-level tradeoffs: latency vs cost vs reliability. Quick breakout into 6 rooms.
- 0:30–0:45 Breakout reports (30s per room) + one-sentence commitments.
- 0:45–0:55 Key operational decisions (Ops/Infra) — what we are committing to buy/stop/hire.
- 0:55–1:00 Close + next steps (VP Ops) — where to find the published OKRs, action owners, and timeline.

Prep checklist (must be actionable and small)
- Owners: fill OKR one-pager by Day -1. Template in linked_artifacts.
- SRE/Infra: provide rough capacity delta for Dedicated requests by Week 1 Day 4.
- Finance: provide commit-level cost delta for proposed Dedicated capacity by Week 2 Day 2.
- DevX: ensure quickstart templates listed and a link is ready in the all-staff pre-read.
- Comms: prepare 1-page pre-read and a short internal blog post draft.

Decisions that need to happen in sprint (explicit)
- Approve two Dedicated capacity tiers for pilot customers (Finance + Infra).
- Greenlight private-deploy installer pilot for 2 named accounts (Sales + Legal sign-off).
- Prioritize infra hiring for SRE vs. Applied ML infra contractor (headcount team decision).

Risk register (top 6)
- Risk: Missing baselines -> measurement noise. Mitigation: owners required to fill baselines or escalate; otherwise tag KR as 'qualitative'.
- Risk: Leadership bandwidth — execs double-booked. Mitigation: 2 rehearsals, asynchronous sign-offs allowed for small wording changes.
- Risk: Too many OKRs (scope creep). Mitigation: cap to 6 company KRs; every extra KR needs 2-exec sign-off.
- Risk: Resource commits slip. Mitigation: Finance to post cost delta and an approval deadline (Week 2 Day 2).
- Risk: All-staff interactivity fails (poll tool outage). Mitigation: backup Google Forms + manual tally.
- Risk: Customer escalations during sprint consuming capacity. Mitigation: designate incident hot-seat rotations (SRE) and freeze non-critical ship decisions for 48 hrs.

Facilitator notes / scripts (rough)
- Opening: prompt the CEO to tell a 60-second customer story that connects to the first objective (cost predictability). Real story wins.
- Poll phrasing: "If you could spend one resource on Q2 to deliver the most impact for customers, would you pick: a) Latency, b) Cost, c) Reliability, d) Developer UX".
- Breakout prompt: "Name one thing your team will stop doing this quarter to free up capacity for the chosen KR". Capture in a shared doc.

Post-sprint handoff (72 hours after all-staff)
- Publish: final OKRs, executive one-pagers, and the recording.
- JIRA: PMs create ticket per KR with acceptance criteria and a 45-day short milestone. Tag: OKR-Q2-2025.
- Follow-up: PM-run retro after 21 days to check signals and drift.

Open questions / TODOs (to resolve in pre-sprint)
- Who is the final approver for infra headcount — CFO or VP Eng? (TODO: Confirm by Day -2)
- Confirm guest list for the All-Staff HQ location (hybrid logistics).
- Baseline numbers: infra and finance to fill by Day -1 or mark as qualitative.

Quick exec update talking points (for 10-min slot)
- 1: Progress snapshot: what we shipped last quarter that materially changed unit economics.
- 2: What we will commit to this quarter and why it's safe to do so (risk + mitigation).
- 3: One thing we need everyone to stop doing that frees capacity.

Appendix: templates and links (placeholders)
- OKR one-pager template: see linked_artifacts.
- All-staff pre-read checklist: 1 page (Comms to paste into Confluence).
- Recording + deck template: GitHub / decks repo link.

Rough owners for next steps
- Sprint lead & facilitator: Ava Chen (company-ops)
- OKR consolidation: Ravi Patel (Product)
- All-staff logistics & comms: Maya Gonzalez (People & Comms)
- Technical runbooks & infra capacity: Liam O'Rourke (Infra)
